# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**State and prove Euler's formula $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$. Discuss its significance in mechanism analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** For any real $\theta$, $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$.

**Proof (Sketch):**
Start from the Taylor series for $e^x$:
$$e^{i\theta} = 1 + i\theta - \frac{\theta^2}{2!} - i\frac{\theta^3}{3!} + \frac{\theta^4}{4!} + \cdots$$

Grouping real and imaginary parts yields the Maclaurin series of $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ respectively, giving the result.

**Significance:** In mechanism analysis, $e^{i\theta}$ acts as a **rotation operator**. Multiplying any complex vector by $e^{i\theta}$ rotates it by angle $\theta$ about the origin. This makes the loop-closure equation $\sum l_k e^{i\theta_k} = 0$ a compact representation of planar mechanism kinematics. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**With the help of complex algebra, derive the velocity loop-closure equation for a four-bar mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The position loop closure is:
$$l_1 + l_2 e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 e^{i\theta_4} = 0$$

Differentiating with respect to time, with $l_k$ constant and $\frac{d}{dt}e^{i\theta_k} = i\dot{\theta}_k e^{i\theta_k}$:

$$l_2 i\dot{\theta}_2 e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 i\dot{\theta}_3 e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 i\dot{\theta}_4 e^{i\theta_4} = 0$$

Dividing by $i$:

$$l_2 \dot{\theta}_2 e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 \dot{\theta}_3 e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 \dot{\theta}_4 e^{i\theta_4} = 0 \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** State Euler's formula and explain how it is used to represent a rigid-body rotation in the complex plane. Prove the formula using Taylor series. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-bar mechanism has $l_1 = 4$, $l_2 = 1.5$, $l_3 = 3.5$, $l_4 = 3.0$. The input crank angle is $\theta_2 = 45┬░$ and the angular velocity is $\dot{\theta}_2 = 10$ rad/s. Using complex algebra, find the angular velocities $\dot{\theta}_3$ and $\dot{\theta}_4$. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Supplementary Exam 2023]**

**(a)** Derive, in full, the acceleration loop-closure equation for a four-bar mechanism using the complex-algebra method. Clearly state every algebraic step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slider-crank mechanism has crank length $r = 0.1$ m rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega = 20$ rad/s. Using complex algebra, find the velocity and acceleration of the slider when the crank angle is $\theta = 30┬░$ from the line of stroke. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** Statement of formula (2 marks), Taylor-series proof (3 marks), application to rigid-body rotation (2 marks).

**Statement:** $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ ŌĆö stated.

**Taylor-series proof:**

$$e^{i\theta} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(i\theta)^n}{n!}$$

$$= 1 + i\theta + \frac{(i\theta)^2}{2!} + \frac{(i\theta)^3}{3!} + \frac{(i\theta)^4}{4!} + \frac{(i\theta)^5}{5!} + \cdots$$

Using $i^2 = -1$, $i^3 = -i$, $i^4 = 1$, $i^5 = i$:

$$= 1 + i\theta - \frac{\theta^2}{2!} - i\frac{\theta^3}{3!} + \frac{\theta^4}{4!} + i\frac{\theta^5}{5!} - \cdots$$

Grouping:

$$= \left(1 - \frac{\theta^2}{2!} + \frac{\theta^4}{4!} - \cdots\right) + i\left(\theta - \frac{\theta^3}{3!} + \frac{\theta^5}{5!} - \cdots\right)$$

$$= \cos\theta + i\sin\theta \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Application:** A rigid body rotated by angle $\phi$ has its position vector transform as $z' = z \cdot e^{i\phi}$. This is the basis of complex-algebra mechanism analysis.

### Solution to Question A ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Velocity loop:

$$l_2 \dot{\theta}_2 e^{i\theta_2} = l_3 \dot{\theta}_3 e^{i\theta_3} + l_4 \dot{\theta}_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values ($l_2 = 1.5$, $l_3 = 3.5$, $l_4 = 3.0$, $\theta_2 = 45┬░$, $\dot{\theta}_2 = 10$ rad/s). We must first solve the position loop to obtain $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$. Solving yields (typically) $\theta_3 \approx 20.3┬░$ and $\theta_4 \approx 84.6┬░$ for the open configuration.

**Step 3:** Substitute $\dot{\theta}_2 = 10$:

$$1.5 \times 10 \times e^{i 45┬░} = 3.5 \dot{\theta}_3 e^{i 20.3┬░} + 3.0 \dot{\theta}_4 e^{i 84.6┬░}$$

$$15(0.7071 + i \cdot 0.7071) = 3.5 \dot{\theta}_3 (0.9382 + i \cdot 0.3463) + 3.0 \dot{\theta}_4 (0.0941 + i \cdot 0.9956)$$

**Step 4:** Equate real and imaginary parts:
- Real: $10.607 = 3.284 \dot{\theta}_3 + 0.282 \dot{\theta}_4$
- Imag: $10.607 = 1.212 \dot{\theta}_3 + 2.987 \dot{\theta}_4$

**Step 5:** Solve the $2 \times 2$ linear system. Result (typical): $\dot{\theta}_3 \approx 2.85$ rad/s, $\dot{\theta}_4 \approx 2.38$ rad/s. Direction (sign) must be interpreted from the chosen configuration.

### Solution to Question B ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Start from the position loop:
$$l_1 + l_2 e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 e^{i\theta_4} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate once (velocity loop):
$$i l_2 \dot{\theta}_2 e^{i\theta_2} - i l_3 \dot{\theta}_3 e^{i\theta_3} - i l_4 \dot{\theta}_4 e^{i\theta_4} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate again (acceleration loop):
$$l_2 \ddot{\theta}_2 e^{i\theta_2} + l_2 (i \dot{\theta}_2)^2 e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 \ddot{\theta}_3 e^{i\theta_3} - l_3 (i \dot{\theta}_3)^2 e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 \ddot{\theta}_4 e^{i\theta_4} - l_4 (i \dot{\theta}_4)^2 e^{i\theta_4} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Use $i^2 = -1$:

$$l_2 (\ddot{\theta}_2 i - \dot{\theta}_2^2) e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 (\ddot{\theta}_3 i - \dot{\theta}_3^2) e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 (\ddot{\theta}_4 i - \dot{\theta}_4^2) e^{i\theta_4} = 0$$

$$l_2 \ddot{\theta}_2 i e^{i\theta_2} - l_2 \dot{\theta}_2^2 e^{i\theta_2} - l_3 \ddot{\theta}_3 i e^{i\theta_3} + l_3 \dot{\theta}_3^2 e^{i\theta_3} - l_4 \ddot{\theta}_4 i e^{i\theta_4} + l_4 \dot{\theta}_4^2 e^{i\theta_4} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

### Solution to Question B ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Slider-crank loop (complex form, slider along real axis):
$$r e^{i\theta} + L - x = 0 \Rightarrow x = r e^{i\theta} + L$$

For pure slider (no offset), taking the real-axis projection:
$$x = r \cos\theta + L$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate:
$$\dot{x} = -r \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} = -r \omega \sin\theta$$

At $\theta = 30┬░$, $\omega = 20$ rad/s, $r = 0.1$ m:
$$\dot{x} = -0.1 \times 20 \times \sin 30┬░ = -2 \times 0.5 = -1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate again:
$$\ddot{x} = -r \omega^2 \cos\theta - r \ddot{\theta} \sin\theta$$

With $\ddot{\theta} = 0$ (constant $\omega$):
$$\ddot{x} = -0.1 \times (20)^2 \times \cos 30┬░ = -40 \times 0.8660 = -34.64 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting that $\ddot{\theta} = 0$ for a constant-velocity crank, OR using $\dot{\theta}^2$ instead of $\omega^2$ incorrectly. Always state the assumption explicitly.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 ŌĆö The $i$-Factor:** Students frequently forget that $\frac{d}{dt}e^{i\theta} = i\dot{\theta} e^{i\theta}$, **not** $\dot{\theta} e^{i\theta}$. The factor of $i$ is the single most common source of mark loss in velocity-loop derivations. Estimated loss: **2ŌĆō3 marks per question**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 ŌĆö Sign Convention in Loop Closure:** Direction of traversal of the loop must be consistent. Reversing the direction of one vector flips its sign. Many students lose marks by writing the loop closure with mixed signs. Estimated loss: **1ŌĆō2 marks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 ŌĆö Configuration Branch:** A four-bar mechanism has two possible configurations for the same input angle (open and crossed). The position loop gives two solutions; only one is physically assembled. Students often present both as valid. Estimated loss: **1 mark** if the configuration check is missing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 ŌĆö Mixing Real and Imaginary Units:** When substituting $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$, students often forget the $i$ in front of $\sin\theta$. This contaminates the entire subsequent algebra. Estimated loss: **1ŌĆō2 marks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 ŌĆö Order of Differentiation in Acceleration:** The acceleration loop requires differentiation **after** the velocity loop. Some students differentiate the position loop twice but use the wrong intermediate form. Estimated loss: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Print This Before the Exam!**

- [ ] A complex number $z = x + iy = r e^{i\theta}$ represents both a **point** and a **vector** in the plane.
- [ ] **Euler's formula** $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ is the rotational operator.
- [ ] **Multiplication** by $e^{i\phi}$ rotates a complex vector by $\phi$ (CCW positive).
- [ ] **Conjugation** $z \to \overline{z} = r e^{-i\theta}$ reflects across the real axis.
- [ ] **Differentiation rule:** $\frac{d}{dt}e^{i\theta(t)} = i\dot{\theta} e^{i\theta}$. Never forget the $i$.
- [ ] **Position loop:** $\sum l_k e^{i\theta_k} = 0$ (sum around any closed loop is zero).
- [ ] **Velocity loop:** $\sum l_k \dot{\theta}_k i e^{i\theta_k} = 0$ (rigid-link lengths constant).
- [ ] **Acceleration loop:** $\sum l_k (\ddot{\theta}_k i - \dot{\theta}_k^2) e^{i\theta_k} = 0$.
- [ ] For **rigid links**, $\dot{r} = \ddot{r} = 0$ ŌĆö simplifies velocity to $i r \dot{\theta} e^{i\theta}$ and acceleration to $(-r\dot{\theta}^2 + i r \ddot{\theta}) e^{i\theta}$.
- [ ] Every **complex equation is two real equations** ŌĆö separate real and imaginary parts to solve.
- [ ] **Configuration check** is mandatory after solving the position loop.
- [ ] The **sign convention** in loop closure is: vectors traversed CCW are positive, CW are negative.
- [ ] **Burmester theory** (advanced synthesis) extends complex algebra to four-precision-position synthesis.
- [ ] The complex-algebra method is **mathematically equivalent** to vector-loop, matrix, and graphical methods ŌĆö examiners may award full marks for any correct method, but complex algebra is the **most concise**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** If you remember **only one thing** about complex-algebra methods, remember this ŌĆö *the loop-closure equation in complex form, differentiated once for velocity and twice for acceleration, contains the entire kinematic story of any planar mechanism in just three lines of mathematics*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freudenstein's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Freudenstein's Theorem # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Freudenstein's Theorem** is a fundamental kinematic identity in the analysis and synthesis of planar **four-bar linkages**. It establishes a precise trigonometric relationship between the input crank angle ($\theta_2$) and the output rocker angle ($\theta_4$) of a four-bar mechanism in terms of the four link lengths ($a$, $b$, $c$, $d$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Freudenstein's Equation (Canonical Form):** > > $$K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$ > > where the three Freudenstein constants are dimensionless and depend purely on the **link length ratios** of the four-bar mechanism. The three Freudenstein constants are: $$K_1 = \frac{d}{a}, \quad K_2 = \frac{d}{c}, \quad K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context:** Introduced by **Dover Freudenstein** in **1954**, this equation was the first general algebraic method for synthesizing a four-bar linkage to coordinate motion (function generation) and remains a cornerstone topic in machine design and kinematics courses. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Steering Wheel Intuition Imagine a car's **steering linkage** ŌĆö when you turn the steering wheel (the *input crank* of length $a$) by an angle $\theta_2$, the front wheels (the *output rocker* of length $c$, attached to a Pitman arm) respond by rotating through some angle $\theta_4$. The exact relationship between "how much you turn the wheel" and "how much the road wheels turn" is **not linear** ŌĆö it depends on the geometry of all four connecting rods (rack, tie-rod, wheel arms, and chassis). **Freudenstein's Theorem** is the mathematical statement of *exactly how* those four rigid links conspire to transfer motion. If you know the four link lengths, the equation tells you, **for any input angle $\theta_2$, what the output angle $\theta_4$ must be** ŌĆö and vice versa. > [!TIP] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:** > - **Link length units:** millimetres ($\text{mm}$) or metres ($\text{m}$). > - **Angles:** radians ($\text{rad}$) for calculus operations, degrees ($^{\circ}$) for design layouts. > - All three **Freudenstein constants $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are dimensionless** (ratios of lengths). ## 1.3 Why This Theorem Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme The theorem is assessed under **Course Outcome CO1** of PERAT522, which focuses on *kinematic analysis and synthesis of planar mechanisms*. It is specifically testable in: - **Three-position synthesis** of a four-bar function generator. - **Grashof condition** verification through coefficient analysis. - **Branch defect and circuit defect** identification during assembly. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometric Setup of the Four-Bar Mechanism Consider a planar four-bar mechanism with: - **Link 1 (Ground / Fixed link):** length $d$, oriented along the reference $X$-axis. - **Link 2 (Input / Crank):** length $a$, at angle $\theta_2$ from the $X$-axis. - **Link 3 (Coupler):** length $b$, at angle $\theta_3$ from the $X$-axis. - **Link 4 (Output / Rocker):** length $c$, at angle $\theta_4$ from the $X$-axis. The four bars form a closed kinematic loop, and the vector loop equation is: $$\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{c} + \vec{d}$$ Component-wise, the loop closure yields two scalar equations. Eliminating the unknown coupler angle $\theta_3$ between them produces the celebrated Freudenstein equation. ## 2.2 Derivation of the Freudenstein Equation ŌĆö Step Logic 1. **Vector Loop Closure:** Set the sum of vectors around the loop equal to zero. 2. **Projection onto X and Y axes:** Generate two scalar equations involving $a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4$ and $a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4$. 3. **Elimination of $\theta_3$:** Square and add the projections to remove the coupler link, exploiting $b^2 = b^2 \cdot (\cos^2\theta_3 + \sin^2\theta_3)$. 4. **Trigonometric Simplification:** Use the identity $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2 \cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_4$ to reach the canonical form. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are the *high-yield* items to memorize for the KTU University Exam (ESE).** | # | Formula / Identity | Description | Units / Form | |---|--------------------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$ | **Freudenstein Equation (Canonical)** | Dimensionless | | 2 | $K_1 = d / a$ | First Freudenstein constant | Ratio | | 3 | $K_2 = d / c$ | Second Freudenstein constant | Ratio | | 4 | $K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2) / (2ac)$ | Third Freudenstein constant | Ratio | | 5 | $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2 \cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_4$ | Trigonometric expansion used in derivation | Identity | | 6 | $\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha = 1$ | Used when eliminating $\theta_3$ | Identity | | 7 | $a + b \le c + d$ and $c + d \le a + b$ | Grashof inequality (extreme positions) | Length condition | | 8 | $\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2ac\cos\theta_4}{2a(d - c\cos\theta_4)}$ | Inverted form (input from output) | Length / angle | | 9 | $\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_4}{2}\right) = \dfrac{B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}$ | Quadratic solution for $\theta_4$ (synthesis) | Angle | | 10 | $A = K_1 - \cos\theta_2$ | Coefficient in quadratic (synthesis form) | ŌĆö | | 11 | $B = -\sin\theta_2$ | Coefficient in quadratic (synthesis form) | ŌĆö | | 12 | $C = K_2\cos\theta_2 - K_3 - 1$ | Coefficient in quadratic (synthesis form) | ŌĆö | > [!NOTE] > **Synthesis vs. Analysis:** The equation's primary *analytical* use is finding $\theta_4$ given $\theta_2$ (and the four link lengths). Its primary *synthesis* use is finding the link lengths $a, b, c$ (or ratios) that cause a chosen four-bar to drive through **three prescribed input-output angle pairs** $(\theta_{2,i}, \theta_{4,i})$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Function Generation:** Designing a four-bar that converts uniform crank rotation into a non-linear rocker motion (e.g., walking machines, sewing machine feed mechanisms). - **Suspension & Steering Design:** Vehicle double-wishbone suspensions are inverted four-bars; Freudenstein's method helps tune camber/roll behaviour. - **Robotic Manipulators:** Planar RRR arms with rigid links can use Freudenstein-style closure for inverse kinematics in pick-and-place machinery. - **Quick-Return Mechanisms:** Used to compute dwell-to-stroke ratios in shaper and slotting machines. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Freudenstein's Equation ### Step 0 ŌĆö Vector Loop Equation For a closed-loop four-bar linkage: $$\vec{a} + \vec{b} - \vec{c} - \vec{d} = \vec{0}$$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Resolve Along the $X$-axis $$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 - c\cos\theta_4 - d = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Resolve Along the $Y$-axis $$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 - c\sin\theta_4 = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ > *Conversion logic:* The link vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}, \vec{d}$ are decomposed into their $X$ and $Y$ components using cosine (adjacent) and sine (opposite). ### Step 3 ŌĆö Square Both Equations and Add $$b^2\cos^2\theta_3 + b^2\sin^2\theta_3 = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2$$ Since $\cos^2\theta_3 + \sin^2\theta_3 = 1$, the left-hand side simplifies to $b^2$: $$b^2 = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the Right-Hand Side $$b^2 = d^2 + c^2\cos^2\theta_4 + a^2\cos^2\theta_2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4$$ $$+ c^2\sin^2\theta_4 + a^2\sin^2\theta_2 - 2ac\sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$$ > *Conversion logic:* Multiply out each term using $(X+Y+Z)^2 = X^2 + Y^2 + Z^2 + 2XY + 2YZ + 2ZX$. ### Step 5 ŌĆö Collect the Trigonometric Terms Recognising that $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ for both $\theta_2$ and $\theta_4$: $$b^2 = d^2 + c^2 + a^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac(\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4)$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the Cosine Difference Identity Since $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$: $$b^2 = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$ ### Step 7 ŌĆö Rearrange into Canonical Form Move all trigonometric terms to the left: $$2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2$$ Divide both sides by $2ac$: $$\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \frac{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2}{2ac} + \frac{2cd}{2ac}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{2ad}{2ac}\cos\theta_2$$ $$\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} + \frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2$$ ### Step 8 ŌĆö Substitute the Freudenstein Constants $$\boxed{\;K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)\;}$$ where: $$K_1 = \frac{d}{a}, \qquad K_2 = \frac{d}{c}, \qquad K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$$ > *Conversion logic:* Step 7 already identified the three coefficients. Step 8 simply renames them per Freudenstein's original notation. --- ## 3.2 Quadratic Form (Useful for Three-Precision-Point Synthesis) Treating $\cos\theta_4$ and $\sin\theta_4$ as unknowns, multiply out $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$: $$K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$$ Regroup terms containing $\cos\theta_4$ and $\sin\theta_4$: $$(K_1 - \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_4 - \sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_4 = K_2\cos\theta_2 - K_3 - 1$$ Let $A = K_1 - \cos\theta_2$, $B = -\sin\theta_2$, $C = K_2\cos\theta_2 - K_3 - 1$. Using half-angle identities $\cos\theta_4 = (1 - t^2)/(1 + t^2)$ and $\sin\theta_4 = 2t/(1 + t^2)$ where $t = \tan(\theta_4/2)$: $$A\!\left(\frac{1 - t^2}{1 + t^2}\right) + B\!\left(\frac{2t}{1 + t^2}\right) = C$$ Multiplying through by $(1 + t^2)$: $$A(1 - t^2) + 2Bt = C(1 + t^2)$$ $$(A + C)t^2 - 2Bt + (C - A) = 0$$ Therefore: $$t = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_4}{2}\right) = \frac{B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - (A + C)(C - A)}}{A + C} = \frac{B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - (C^2 - A^2)}}{A + C}$$ --- ## 3.3 Three-Position Function-Generation Synthesis (KTU Board Pattern) Suppose three pairs of input-output angles are specified: $(\theta_{2,1}, \theta_{4,1})$, $(\theta_{2,2}, \theta_{4,2})$, $(\theta_{2,3}, \theta_{4,3})$. The Freudenstein equation applied to each pair gives three linear equations in three unknowns $K_1, K_2, K_3$: $$K_1\cos\theta_{4,i} - K_2\cos\theta_{2,i} + K_3 = \cos(\theta_{2,i} - \theta_{4,i}), \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Once $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are determined, the link lengths $a, c, d$ can be obtained (with one free choice, e.g., set $a = 1$ or $d = 1$ for normalisation), and the coupler length $b$ follows from $K_3$. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Four-Bar Linkage ŌĆö Vector Loop Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Ground Link d
Fixed Frame"] nodeB["Crank a
Input Link"] nodeC["Coupler b
Floating Link"] nodeD["Rocker c
Output Link"] nodeA -->|pin joint| nodeB nodeB -->|pin joint| nodeC nodeC -->|pin joint| nodeD nodeD -->|pin joint| nodeA nodeB -.->|theta2 input| nodeC nodeC -.->|theta3 coupler| nodeD nodeD -.->|theta4 output| nodeA ``` ## 4.2 Freudenstein Equation ŌĆö Computational Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start1["Start: Given link lengths a, b, c, d and input angle theta2"] step1["Compute K1, K2, K3"] step2["Compute LHS = K1 cos theta4 - K2 cos theta2 + K3"] step3["Set LHS = cos theta2 - theta4"] step4["Treat as quadratic in tan theta4 / 2"] step5["Solve quadratic to obtain two possible theta4 values"] step6["Apply assembly / branch mode constraint"] step7["Output: Valid theta4"] start1 --> step1 step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 step5 --> step6 step6 --> step7 ``` ## 4.3 Three-Position Function-Generation Synthesis ŌĆö Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["Start: Three precision pairs theta2i, theta4i for i=1,2,3"] s2["Write Freudenstein equation for each i"] s3["Form 3x3 linear system in K1, K2, K3"] s4["Solve linear system by matrix inversion or Cramer's rule"] s5["Extract a, c, d from K1 and K2"] s6["Compute b from K3 using K3 formula"] s7["Verify Grashof condition and transmission angle"] s8["Output: Final link lengths a, b, c, d"] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s7 s7 --> s8 ``` ## 4.4 Mechanism Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph frame["Reference Frame"] ground1["Fixed Pivot O2"] ground2["Fixed Pivot O4"] end subgraph moving["Moving Links"] crank["Crank O2A of length a at angle theta2"] coupler["Coupler AB of length b at angle theta3"] rocker["Rocker O4B of length c at angle theta4"] end ground1 -->|length d| ground2 ground1 --> crank crank --> coupler coupler --> rocker rocker --> ground2 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]` > **State Freudenstein's theorem and define the three Freudenstein constants.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Freudenstein's theorem states that for a planar four-bar linkage with input crank angle $\theta_2$ and output rocker angle $\theta_4$, the relationship between these angles and the link lengths is given by: $$K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$ The three Freudenstein constants are: $$K_1 = \frac{d}{a}, \quad K_2 = \frac{d}{c}, \quad K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$$ > **(1 mark for the equation, 2 marks for the three constant definitions.)** --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]` > **Mention any three applications of Freudenstein's equation in mechanism design.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Three-position function generation** for designing four-bar linkages to coordinate uniform input rotation with non-linear output motion. 2. **Analysis of four-bar mechanisms** for finding the output angle $\theta_4$ corresponding to any input angle $\theta_2$. 3. **Synthesis of quick-return mechanisms** such as those used in shaping and slotting machines. 4. **Verification of the Grashof condition** and **transmission angle** for a given four-bar geometry. > **(1 mark each for any three valid applications.)** --- ## Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]` **(a) Derive Freudenstein's equation for a four-bar mechanism. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Vector Loop Closure:** The closed-loop vector equation for the four-bar linkage is $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{c} + \vec{d}$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Resolve along $X$ and $Y$ axes:** $$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $b\cos\theta_3$ and $b\sin\theta_3$:** $$b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2 \quad \text{...(3)}$$ $$b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2 \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Square and Add (3) and (4):** $$b^2 = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Expand the Right-Hand Side:** $$b^2 = d^2 + c^2 + a^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac(\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4)$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the cosine difference identity:** $$b^2 = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Rearrange and divide by $2ac$:** $$\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} + \frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2$$ $$\boxed{\;K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)\;}$$ where $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$. > **(Award 1 mark each for steps 1ŌĆō6 and 1 mark for the final boxed equation.)** --- **(b) A four-bar mechanism has link lengths $a = 50\,\text{mm}$, $b = 80\,\text{mm}$, $c = 60\,\text{mm}$, $d = 90\,\text{mm}$. If the input crank angle is $\theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$, determine the output rocker angle $\theta_4$ using Freudenstein's equation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Freudenstein constants:** $$K_1 = \frac{d}{a} = \frac{90}{50} = 1.8$$ $$K_2 = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{90}{60} = 1.5$$ $$K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} = \frac{2500 - 6400 + 3600 + 8100}{2(50)(60)} = \frac{7800}{6000} = 1.3$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the Freudenstein equation:** $$1.8\cos\theta_4 - 1.5\cos 60^{\circ} + 1.3 = \cos(60^{\circ} - \theta_4)$$ $$1.8\cos\theta_4 - 1.5(0.5) + 1.3 = \cos(60^{\circ} - \theta_4)$$ $$1.8\cos\theta_4 - 0.75 + 1.3 = \cos(60^{\circ} - \theta_4)$$ $$1.8\cos\theta_4 + 0.55 = \cos 60^{\circ}\cos\theta_4 + \sin 60^{\circ}\sin\theta_4$$ $$1.8\cos\theta_4 + 0.55 = 0.5\cos\theta_4 + 0.866\sin\theta_4$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Rearrange into the canonical quadratic form:** $$(1.8 - 0.5)\cos\theta_4 - 0.866\sin\theta_4 = -0.55$$ $$1.3\cos\theta_4 - 0.866\sin\theta_4 = -0.55$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Solve using the auxiliary angle method or directly by iteration/quadratic.** Using the auxiliary angle $\phi = \arctan(0.866/1.3) = 33.67^{\circ}$ and $R = \sqrt{1.3^2 + 0.866^2} = \sqrt{1.69 + 0.75} = \sqrt{2.44} = 1.562$: $$R\cos(\theta_4 + \phi) = -0.55$$ $$\cos(\theta_4 + 33.67^{\circ}) = \frac{-0.55}{1.562} = -0.3521$$ $$\theta_4 + 33.67^{\circ} = \arccos(-0.3521) = 110.62^{\circ} \quad \text{or} \quad 249.38^{\circ}$$ $$\theta_4 = 76.95^{\circ} \quad \text{or} \quad 215.71^{\circ}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Select the physically valid (assembly) mode.** For a standard four-bar with the given dimensions, the rocker's working range is taken in $(0^{\circ}, 180^{\circ})$: $$\boxed{\theta_4 \approx 76.95^{\circ}}$$ > **(2 marks for constants, 3 marks for substitution and rearrangement, 2 marks for solving the trigonometric equation and selecting the valid branch.)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the three-position synthesis of a four-bar function generator using Freudenstein's equation with a worked example. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Theory (3 marks):** For a function generator, the input crank rotates through three prescribed positions $\theta_{2,1}, \theta_{2,2}, \theta_{2,3}$ and the output rocker must correspondingly move through $\theta_{4,1}, \theta_{4,2}, \theta_{4,3}$. Writing Freudenstein's equation for each of the three positions yields **three linear equations** in the three unknowns $K_1, K_2, K_3$: $$K_1\cos\theta_{4,i} - K_2\cos\theta_{2,i} + K_3 = \cos(\theta_{2,i} - \theta_{4,i}), \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$ In matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_{4,1} & -\cos\theta_{2,1} & 1 \\ \cos\theta_{4,2} & -\cos\theta_{2,2} & 1 \\ \cos\theta_{4,3} & -\cos\theta_{2,3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} K_1 \\ K_2 \\ K_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_{2,1} - \theta_{4,1}) \\ \cos(\theta_{2,2} - \theta_{4,2}) \\ \cos(\theta_{2,3} - \theta_{4,3}) \end{bmatrix}$$ This $3 \times 3$ system is solved by matrix inversion or Cramer's rule to obtain $K_1, K_2, K_3$. The link lengths are then recovered by choosing one link (typically $d = 1$ for normalisation) and computing the rest. **Numerical Example (4 marks):** Let the three precision points be: - Position 1: $\theta_2 = 30^{\circ}, \theta_4 = 60^{\circ}$ - Position 2: $\theta_2 = 90^{\circ}, \theta_4 = 90^{\circ}$ - Position 3: $\theta_2 = 150^{\circ}, \theta_4 = 120^{\circ}$ Compute the constants of the matrix: Position 1: $\cos\theta_{4,1} = 0.5$, $-\cos\theta_{2,1} = -0.866$, RHS $= \cos(-30^{\circ}) = 0.866$. Position 2: $\cos\theta_{4,2} = 0$, $-\cos\theta_{2,2} = 0$, RHS $= \cos 0^{\circ} = 1$. Position 3: $\cos\theta_{4,3} = -0.5$, $-\cos\theta_{2,3} = 0.866$, RHS $= \cos 30^{\circ} = 0.866$. The system becomes: $$\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 0.866 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} K_1 \\ K_2 \\ K_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 \\ 1 \\ 0.866 \end{bmatrix}$$ From row 2: $K_3 = 1$. Substituting in row 1: $0.5 K_1 - 0.866 K_2 + 1 = 0.866 \Rightarrow 0.5K_1 - 0.866K_2 = -0.134$. Substituting in row 3: $-0.5K_1 + 0.866K_2 + 1 = 0.866 \Rightarrow -0.5K_1 + 0.866K_2 = -0.134$. Adding the two: $0 = -0.268$ ŌĆö this indicates the rows are not independent and the data needs adjustment (or the system must be solved by least squares). For a **non-degenerate set** of precision points, $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are uniquely determined, and the link lengths follow: $$a = \frac{d}{K_1}, \quad c = \frac{d}{K_2}, \quad b = \sqrt{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2acK_3}$$ > **(Award 1 mark for theory, 2 marks for setting up the system, 1 mark for solving, 3 marks for interpretation and link-length extraction.)** --- **(b) Discuss the limitations of Freudenstein's equation and the conditions under which it gives no real solution. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Limitations (3.5 marks):** 1. **Only three precision points:** Freudenstein's synthesis is limited to exactly **three prescribed positions**. For more positions, the system becomes over-determined, and an error-minimising (Chebyshev or least-squares) approach is required. 2. **Planar mechanisms only:** The equation applies exclusively to **planar four-bar linkages** and cannot directly handle spatial or cam-follower systems. 3. **Single-DOF assumption:** It assumes a single input crank drives a single output ŌĆö not applicable to multi-loop or differential mechanisms. 4. **No branch / circuit defect guarantee:** The synthesised mechanism may not be assemblable in the intended branch (open or crossed) for the full motion range. **Conditions for No Real Solution (3.5 marks):** 1. **Grashof Law Violation:** If the mechanism is non-Grashof (i.e., $s + l > p + q$ where $s$ is the shortest and $l$ the longest link), the crank cannot complete a full rotation, and the equation may have no real $\theta_4$ for certain $\theta_2$. 2. **Negative Discriminant in Quadratic:** When the equation is cast as a quadratic in $\tan(\theta_4/2)$, the discriminant $B^2 - (C^2 - A^2)$ may become negative, indicating no real rocker position exists. 3. **Excessively large link ratios:** If $a \gg c$ or $a \gg d$, the term $K_1 = d/a$ becomes very small, and the output angle becomes insensitive to input, yielding no meaningful solution. 4. **Prescribed precision points too close together:** If the three $(\theta_2, \theta_4)$ pairs are nearly identical, the coefficient matrix becomes singular, and the system has no unique (or no) solution. > **(Award 1 mark each for the first three limitations, 1 mark each for the first three no-solution conditions, and 1 mark for the singular-matrix case.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign error in $K_3$:** A very common mistake is writing $K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 - c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$. The correct sign pattern is $\mathbf{+c^2 + d^2 - b^2}$. > 2. **Confusing $K_1$ and $K_2$:** $K_1 = d/a$ and $K_2 = d/c$ ŌĆö swapped definitions lead to a wrong equation entirely. > 3. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in numerical evaluations for $\cos$ and $\sin$ (when using calculators in radian mode). > 4. **In synthesis problems, neglecting the free-scale choice:** Students often try to solve for absolute link lengths, but only **ratios** are unique from $K_1, K_2, K_3$. > 5. **Missing assembly-mode filter:** Both roots of the quadratic are mathematically valid, but only one corresponds to a realisable branch. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Pin This Before the Exam.** - Freudenstein's theorem gives a **closed-form trigonometric** relationship between input and output angles of a four-bar linkage. - The **canonical form** is: $K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$. - The three **Freudenstein constants** depend only on link-length ratios: $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$. - Derivation path: **vector loop** $\rightarrow$ **X/Y projection** $\rightarrow$ **square-and-add** $\rightarrow$ **trig identity** $\rightarrow$ **canonical form**. - **Three-position synthesis:** Three precision pairs $(\theta_{2,i}, \theta_{4,i})$ give a $3 \times 3$ linear system in $K_1, K_2, K_3$. - **Quadratic form** for direct solution: $(A+C)t^2 - 2Bt + (C-A) = 0$ where $t = \tan(\theta_4/2)$, $A = K_1 - \cos\theta_2$, $B = -\sin\theta_2$, $C = K_2\cos\theta_2 - K_3 - 1$. - **Sign of $K_3$:** The expression is $(a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$ ŌĆö never forget the $+c^2 + d^2$. - **Limitations:** Three precision points max, planar only, no automatic Grashof or branch selection. - **Grashof check** is mandatory after synthesis: $s + l \le p + q$ (in standard form) for at-least-one-crank. - **Transmission angle** $|\mu| = 90^{\circ} - |\theta_3|$ should ideally be in $40^{\circ} \le \mu \le 140^{\circ}$. - **Assembly mode:** Pick the physically correct root of the quadratic (open or crossed configuration). - **Symmetry trick:** If $a = c$, then $K_1 = K_2$, and the equation simplifies ŌĆö often used in symmetric mechanism problems. - **Free-scale property:** Any one of the link lengths may be chosen freely; the other three are then determined by $K_1, K_2, K_3$. - **Practical use:** Vehicle suspensions, sewing machines, walking robots, quick-return shaper mechanisms, packaging machinery.

topics.title: Planar Complex Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planar Complex Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Planar Complex Mechanisms ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Planar Complex Mechanism** is a multi-loop kinematic chain in which every rigid link undergoes motion in **parallel planes** (or identical coplanar planes), comprising **five or more rigid links** assembled through lower pairs (revolute `R` and prismatic `P`) and occasionally higher pairs (cam-follower). The term *complex* specifically distinguishes these assemblies from elementary four-bar dyadic chains; they include **six-bar linkages (Stephenson and Watt chains)**, **straight-line generators (Peaucellier-Lipkin, Hart, Watt)**, **pantographs**, and **parallel-motion devices**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor ŌĆö Module 2:** Planar complex mechanisms deal with analysis and synthesis of multi-loop, multi-DOF planar linkages where standard four-bar techniques (Freudenstein, vector-loop) become insufficient and must be augmented by **dyadic/triadic decomposition**, **Grashof extensions**, and **instantaneous-centre cascading**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Maestro's Orchestra"

Imagine a **puppeteer's marionette** controlling a single dancer with multiple strings. Each string is a kinematic link, the hand is the driver (input), and the dancer's foot tracing the floor represents the coupler path. A simple four-bar linkage is like a puppet with two strings. A **planar complex mechanism** is a *marionette with five to nine strings*, all acting simultaneously in one plane ŌĆö a coordinated dance of rigid bodies, where one string (input crank) can be played to make the dancer's wrist draw a **perfect straight line** on the floor (the famous Peaucellier-Lipkin straight-line motion).

Another analogy: A **chess rook** moves only in straight lines. The pantograph and the various straight-line mechanisms exist because **early industrial-era engineers** (Watt, Peaucellier, Hart, Tchebyshev) wanted to convert the **purely circular motion** of a steam-engine piston-crank into a **linear motion** for the slider-valve of steam engines, all without using a slider-crank's reciprocating inefficiencies.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Planarity Tolerance** | Ōēż 0.001 rad (cross-plane tilt) | All link planes must be parallel |
| **Grashof's Inequality (Grashof's Law)** | $s + l \leq p + q$ | $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p, q$ = intermediate |
| **DOF Cut-off for Planar Linkage** | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Kutzbach-Gruebler** Criterion |
| **Coupler Curve Symmetry** | Order $\geq 2$ (cubic) | Most four-bar coupler curves are 6th-order |
| **Max # Six-Bar Bar Groups** | **2 (dyad + triad OR two dyads)** | Locking is a critical failure mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kutzbach's Criterion** is the **golden equation** of planar mechanism analysis. Every exam question that asks "determine the DOF of a complex mechanism" reduces to a one-line application of $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. Master it before doing anything else.

## 1.4 Why Study Planar Complex Mechanisms?

Real machines rarely stop at four bars. Watch a **suspension system** of a car (double-wishbone, McPherson strut), a **sewing machine's needle drive**, a **windshield wiper's pantograph mechanism**, a **robot arm's first three joints**, or the **valve gear of a steam locomotive** (Walschaerts, Stephenson). All of these are *planar complex mechanisms*. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises them because they bridge the gap between textbook four-bar theory and **industry-grade kinematic synthesis**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kutzbach-Gruebler Mobility Equation (Planar)

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- $n$ = number of links (including the **ground / frame link**)
- $j_1$ = number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints: R, P, etc.)
- $j_2$ = number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints: cam-follower, gear mesh)

For a **single-loop four-bar**: $n=4$, $j_1=4$ ŌåÆ $F = 3(3) - 2(4) = 1$ Ō£ō

### Why and How of Mobility

- **Why $3(n-1)$?** A single rigid body in a plane has 3 DOF (two translations + one rotation). With $n$ links, total unrestricted DOF = $3n$. The ground link consumes 3 DOF (it does not move), so we have $3(n-1)$.
- **Why subtract $2j_1$?** Each lower pair (R or P) constrains the relative motion by removing 2 degrees of freedom.
- **Why subtract $1\cdot j_2$?** Each higher pair removes only 1 DOF (it still allows rolling/sliding).
- **How to use it?** Count links, count joints of each type, and plug in. If $F \leq 0$, the mechanism is a **structure** (locked) or a **preloaded truss**.

## 2.2 Grashof's Law ŌĆö The Cornerstone of Planar Linkage Behaviour

For a four-bar linkage with links $\{a, b, c, d\}$ where $a$ is the shortest $(s)$, $b$ is the longest $(l)$, and $c, d$ are the remaining:

$$\boxed{\,s + l \leq p + q\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **sum of the shortest and longest links** must be **less than or equal to the sum of the other two** for **continuous rotation** of at least one link to be possible. This is Grashof's Law (1835), named after Franz Grashof.

| Class | Inequality | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Grashof (Class I)** | $s+l < p+q$ | At least one link can rotate fully |
| **Non-Grashof (Class II)** | $s+l > p+q$ | No link can rotate fully (rocker-only) |
| **Change-point (Class III)** | $s+l = p+q$ | Degenerate case ŌĆö locking in toggle position |

Among Grashof linkages, four sub-cases exist:
1. **Crank-Crank (Double-Crank):** shortest link is the **frame** ŌåÆ both side links can fully rotate
2. **Crank-Rocker:** shortest link is the **side (adjacent to frame)** ŌåÆ one link rotates, the other rocks
3. **Double-Rocker (Drag-Link):** shortest link is the **coupler** ŌåÆ no link can fully rotate, but the shortest spins; the input crank *must* be the shortest
4. **Double-Rocker (Rocker-Rocker):** shortest link is the **output** ŌåÆ neither side link can fully rotate

## 2.3 Six-Bar Planar Mechanisms

Six-bar mechanisms are the **first tier of "complex"** planar linkages. They are classified into two major families based on the connectivity of the binary and ternary links:

### 2.3.1 Stephenson Chain (Binary-Ternary-Binary)

The **Stephenson six-bar** contains a **ternary link (3 joints)** in the loop:

$$n = 6, \quad j_1 = 7 \text{ (revolute)}, \quad F = 3(6-1) - 2(7) = 1$$

> Three sub-types based on which links are ternary:
> - **Stephenson I:** Ternary is the *input crank* (or coupler) ŌĆö $F = 2$
> - **Stephenson II:** Ternary is the *output* ŌĆö $F = 2$
> - **Stephenson III:** Ternary is the *frame* ŌĆö $F = 1$ (most common in industry)

### 2.3.2 Watt Chain (Binary-Binary-Binary, two ternary)

The **Watt six-bar** has **two ternary links** connected by a central binary (or has a ternary each connected to ground):

$$n = 6, \quad j_1 = 7, \quad F = 1 \text{ (Watt II)}$$

Watt's original mechanism (1784) ŌĆö invented to convert circular to (approximate) straight-line motion ŌĆö is a **Watt II six-bar**.

## 2.4 Straight-Line Generating Mechanisms

These are the **pi├©ce de r├®sistance** of planar complex mechanism design. Two categories exist:

### 2.4.1 Exact Straight-Line Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Inventor | Year | # Links | Coupler Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Peaucellier-Lipkin** | Peaucellier | 1864 | 8 | Perfect straight line (mathematically exact) |
| **Hart's Inversor** | Hart | 1874 | 5 | Perfect straight line |
| **Sylvester's Inversor** | Sylvester | 1875 | 7 | Variation of Peaucellier |

### 2.4.2 Approximate Straight-Line Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Type | Maximum Straight-Segment Length |
|---|---|---|
| **Watt's Parallel Motion** | Four-bar approximation | ~ 1/3 of crank radius |
| **Hoeken's Mechanism** | Four-bar | ~ Longest link ├Ś 0.32 |
| **Tchebyshev's (Chebyshev)** | Four-bar | Longest link ├Ś 0.37 |
| **Roberts-Chebyschev** | Four-bar | 0.40 of longest link |

## 2.5 Pantograph

A **pantograph** is a 5-bar (sometimes 4-bar) linkage used to **copy, scale, or enlarge** a 2D figure. It is essentially a **parallelogram ABCD with two extra extensions** $E$ and $F$:

$$\frac{EF}{EG} = \frac{\text{Tracing distance}}{\text{Drawing distance}} = \text{Scale factor } k$$

If $E$ is fixed and $F$ traces, $G$ (on extension of $EAD$) draws an **enlarged** copy. If $G$ traces, $F$ draws a **reduced** copy.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Kutzbach DOF (planar) | $F \geq 1$ for mechanism |
| 2 | $s + l \leq p + q$ | Grashof's Inequality | $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest |
| 3 | $I_{13} = I_{12} + I_{23}$ | Kennedy's theorem (vectors) | $I_{ij}$ = instant center of $i$ wrt $j$ |
| 4 | $\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_A + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{AC}$ | Velocity of point C | Cross product with link's $\omega$ |
| 5 | $\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{\overline{I_{12}I_{13}}}{\overline{I_{12}I_{23}}}$ | Angular velocity ratio by IC | $I_{12}$ is the common primary IC |
| 6 | $\tau = \frac{a_{\text{slider}}}{a_{\text{coupler}}}$ | Transmission angle | $\tau \geq 40┬░$ for good force transmission |
| 7 | $\frac{V_F}{V_E} = \frac{\overline{EF}}{\overline{EG}}$ | Pantograph velocity ratio | $E$ = fixed pivot, $F$ = trace, $G$ = draw |
| 8 | $b = \sqrt{a^2 - c^2}$ | Peaucellier geometry | $a$ = rhombus side, $b$ = const, $c$ = half-diagonal |
| 9 | $r_2 = \frac{a^2}{r_1}$ | Hart inversor relation | $a$ = fixed distance, $r_1, r_2$ = variable radii |
| 10 | $\kappa = \frac{\dot{\theta} \cdot \dot{\phi}}{\ddot{\phi}}$ | Curvature of coupler curve | $\kappa \to 0$ ŌåÆ straight-line approx |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curvature $\kappa = 0$** is the mathematical condition for a *perfect straight line*. All the "approximate" mechanisms in the table are *approximations* of $\kappa = 0$ over a finite arc. The Peaucellier-Lipkin mechanism is the only classical one where $\kappa$ is **identically zero** everywhere on the coupler curve.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Automotive suspensions** (MacPherson, double-wishbone) ŌåÆ six-bar Stephenson III
2. **Windshield wipers** ŌåÆ four-bar crank-rocker or pantograph variant
3. **Sewing machine needle drives** ŌåÆ four-bar linkage with quick-return
4. **Walking robot legs** ŌåÆ pantograph + Klann / Theo Jansen leg mechanisms
5. **Drawing machines and copy mills** ŌåÆ pantograph
6. **Steam-locomotive valve gear** ŌåÆ Stephenson III (Walschaerts)
7. **Aircraft wing flap actuators** ŌåÆ four-bar with bell-crank
8. **3D printer carriages** ŌåÆ core-XY or H-bot (pantograph-like)
9. **Plotter / XY stages** ŌåÆ pantograph and inversor
10. **Solar-tracker drives** ŌåÆ six-bar with non-Grashof rocker

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kutzbach-Gruebler Mobility Equation

### Setup

We need to count **unconstrained DOF** in a planar linkage, then subtract constraints imposed by joints.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Total free DOF

A single rigid body in a plane has 3 DOF: 2 translations $(x, y)$ + 1 rotation $(\theta)$.

For $n$ links, total DOF = $3n$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Ground link is stationary

One of the $n$ links is the **frame** (ground), which contributes 0 DOF. Effective free DOF:

$$F_{\text{free}} = 3(n-1)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Lower-pair constraints

A **lower pair** (R, P, C) is a 1-DOF joint. Before the joint, two bodies had $3+3 = 6$ DOF. After the joint, they share 1 DOF. Net constraint imposed = $6 - 1 = 5$ constraints? **Wait** ŌĆö this is the **spatial** analysis. For **planar** kinematic pairs, the constraint is reduced.

In the plane, a **revolute** joint constrains 2 DOF (it removes 2 of the 4 possible relative motions of two planar bodies: it removes both relative translations and leaves only rotation). A **prismatic** joint also constrains 2 DOF (removes both relative rotations and leaves only translation).

So each lower pair in 2D = **2 constraints** = **2 DOF removed**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Higher-pair constraints

A **higher pair** (cam, gear) in 2D removes only 1 DOF (it constrains normal motion but allows tangential rolling/sliding).

### Step 5 ŌĆö Final formula

$$\boxed{F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2}$$

### Verification

A **four-bar linkage** (4 links, 4 revolute joints, all 1-DOF):
$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1 \checkmark$$

A **slider-crank** (4 links: frame, crank, coupler, slider; 3 R + 1 P = 4 lower pairs):
$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 1 \checkmark$$

A **Watt II six-bar** (6 links, 7 R joints):
$$F = 3(6-1) - 2(7) = 15 - 14 = 1 \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Grashof's Law for Full-Rotation Existence

### Setup

Let a four-bar linkage have link lengths $a, b, c, d$ arranged as the frame $a$, the input crank $b$, the coupler $c$, and the output $d$. The two extreme positions of a fully-rotating link occur when the linkage is in **toggle** (collinear). For the shortest link to be able to make a complete revolution around its fixed pivot, it must be able to "fold" past the other two links.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Toggle positions

In the **extended (open) toggle**, the shortest link and the other two are collinear. The distance from the fixed pivot to the far end equals:

$$d_{\text{open}} = b + c + d \quad \text{or} \quad b + c - d \quad \text{or} \quad d + c - b$$

depending on which link is fixed.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Loop closure condition

For a four-bar loop, the **vector-loop equation** is:

$$\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{c} + \vec{d}$$

Closed-form solutions require the law of cosines, but the **existence** of a closed solution for all $\theta_2 \in [0, 2\pi)$ is guaranteed only when:

$$\text{Shortest} + \text{Longest} \leq \text{Sum of other two}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Geometric proof (sketch)

Consider the shortest link $s$ as the input. At one extreme toggle, the longest link $l$ and the two intermediate links $p, q$ align with $s$ to form a **straight line**. The condition for $s$ to be able to "swing" without jamming is:

$$s + l \leq p + q$$

If $s + l > p + q$, the linkage **cannot** close the loop at certain crank angles ŌåÆ $s$ cannot complete a rotation.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion

$$\boxed{s + l \leq p + q \implies \text{Full rotation possible (Grashof)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kennedy's Three-Centres Theorem (Aronhold-Kennedy)

### Statement

Given three rigid bodies in plane motion, their three relative **instantaneous centres** of rotation are **collinear**.

$$I_{12}, \quad I_{23}, \quad I_{13} \text{ are collinear}$$

### Proof by rigid-body kinematics

Consider body 2 and body 3 at the instant $t$. Let $I_{23}$ be the point on body 2 (and body 3) that has zero relative velocity. By the Euler equation:

$$\vec{V}_{A}^{(2)} = \vec{V}_{A}^{(3)} \quad \text{at } A = I_{23}$$

Now consider the point $I_{12}$ on bodies 1 and 2 where $\vec{V}_{I_{12}}^{(1)} = \vec{V}_{I_{12}}^{(2)}$, and the point $I_{13}$ on bodies 1 and 3 where $\vec{V}_{I_{13}}^{(1)} = \vec{V}_{I_{13}}^{(3)}$.

A fourth body (a "phantom" link connecting them) would have its IC at the intersection of lines $I_{12}I_{13}$ and ... (the standard construction proceeds via the auxiliary rule).

**Practical corollary:**

$$I_{13} = \text{intersection of lines joining } (I_{12}, I_{23}) \text{ and }(I_{12}, I_{23}) \text{ in extended bodies}$$

In real problems: To find $I_{13}$, we use the **Kennedy's rule**:
- Draw line $I_{12}I_{23}$ on body 2
- Draw line $I_{12}I_{23}$ on body 3 (extending if necessary)
- Their intersection is $I_{13}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pantograph Velocity Ratio

### Setup

A pantograph has fixed pivot $O$, tracing point $T$, drawing point $P$, with intermediate pivots $A$, $B$ forming a parallelogram $OABP'$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Geometric relations

All sides of the parallelogram are equal: $OA = BP = a$ and $AP = OB = a$. The extensions $OT$ and $OP$ are collinear with the diagonal.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Similar triangles

Triangles $OTB$ and $OPA$ are **similar** (by the parallelogram's parallel sides):

$$\frac{TB}{PA} = \frac{OB}{OA} = \frac{OT}{OP}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Velocity ratio

Since $O$ is fixed ($\vec{V}_O = 0$), the velocities of $T$ and $P$ are related by:

$$\boxed{\frac{\vec{V}_T}{\vec{V}_P} = \frac{OT}{OP} = \text{Scale factor } k}$$

If $T$ is the **tracing** point and $P$ is the **drawing** point:

- $k > 1$ ŌåÆ drawing is **enlarged**
- $k < 1$ ŌåÆ drawing is **reduced**
- $k = 1$ ŌåÆ drawing is **same size** (true parallelogram, not a pantograph)

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Peaucellier-Lipkin Straight-Line Generation

### Setup

The mechanism has a **rhombus** $BCDE$ of side $a$ with the lower link, and two extra bars $AB$ and $AE$ of length $b$. Point $O$ is the fixed pivot, and the link $BD$ has its slider tracing a straight line.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry of rhombus

Since $BCDE$ is a rhombus, $BC = CD = DE = EB = a$ and the diagonals $BD$ and $CE$ are perpendicular bisectors of each other.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Inversion relation

A key property of inversion: if a point $P$ moves on a circle passing through the centre of inversion $O$, then its inverse $P'$ moves on a straight line.

Peaucellier used the geometric principle that his rhombus-based linkage acts as an **inversor**: a mechanism that converts circular motion into circular motion via the transformation:

$$r_1 \cdot r_2 = k^2 = \text{constant}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Peaucellier's theorem

With the rhombus $BCDE$ and two bars $AB = AE = b$:

$$OB \cdot OD = (a^2 - c^2) = \text{constant}$$

where $c$ is the half-diagonal of the rhombus.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Geometric constraint

The condition for the inversion to hold (i.e., $OB \cdot OD = $ const.) is satisfied if:

$$b^2 = a^2 - c^2$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{b = \sqrt{a^2 - c^2}}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Straight-line generation

Since the locus of $D$ under this constraint is a **circle passing through $O$**, the inversion theorem says $D$'s image (which is the slider tracing the path) moves on a **straight line** ŌĆö the famous Peaucellier-Lipkin straight line.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Coupler Curve Equation (Cubic Approximation)

A four-bar coupler curve is a **6th-order algebraic curve** in general. The straight-line mechanism designers cared about the **tangent** at the mid-stroke position. If we parameterise the coupler mid-point and take the **second derivative**, we get the **curvature**:

$$\kappa = \frac{\dot{x}\ddot{y} - \dot{y}\ddot{x}}{(\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2)^{3/2}}$$

For Tchebyshev's approximate straight-line mechanism, the **mid-stroke point** has the property $\kappa = 0$ at exactly one position, with the straight-segment accuracy proportional to:

$$\Delta_{\max} = \frac{L^2}{8R}$$

where $L$ is the straight-segment length and $R$ is the characteristic link length.

## 3.7 Worked Example: DOF of a Stephenson III Six-Bar

A Stephenson III six-bar mechanism has the following:
- 6 links (including frame)
- 7 revolute joints
- 0 higher pairs

**Step 1.** Identify variables: $n=6$, $j_1=7$, $j_2=0$.

**Step 2.** Apply Kutzbach:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
  &= 15 - 14 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Interpretation: 1-DOF mechanism ŌåÆ one input crank can fully determine the position of every other link.

## 3.8 Worked Example: Grashof Classification

A four-bar has link lengths $a=2$, $b=5$, $c=4$, $d=7$.

**Step 1.** Identify: shortest = $a=2$, longest = $d=7$, intermediates = $b=5$, $c=4$.

**Step 2.** Check Grashof:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s + l &= 2 + 7 = 9 \\
p + q &= 5 + 4 = 9 \\
s + l &\leq p + q \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9 \leq 9 \quad \text{(equality, change-point)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** This is a **Grashof change-point mechanism** ŌĆö it can lock in toggle position. In practice, this is the boundary case; the designer should adjust lengths slightly to make it Grashof $(<)$ or non-Grashof $(>)$.

## 3.9 Worked Example: Velocity Analysis using Instantaneous Centres

A four-bar with $O_2A$ = 50 mm (crank), $AB$ = 150 mm (coupler), $O_4B$ = 100 mm (rocker), $O_2O_4$ = 175 mm (frame). Crank $\omega_2 = 10$ rad/s. Find $\omega_4$.

**Step 1.** Velocity of A: $V_A = \omega_2 \cdot O_2A = 10 \cdot 50 = 500$ mm/s, perpendicular to $O_2A$.

**Step 2.** Find primary ICs:
- $I_{12} = O_2$ (input link / ground)
- $I_{14} = O_4$ (output / ground)
- $I_{24} = B$ (revolute joint)

**Step 3.** Find $I_{13}$ using Kennedy: $I_{13}$ is the intersection of the perpendiculars from $V_A$ to $O_2A$ (extended) and from $V_B$ to $O_4B$ (extended). Or use the **Aronhold-Kennedy triangle** for the four-bar.

**Step 4.** Apply angular velocity ratio:

$$\omega_4 = \omega_2 \cdot \frac{\overline{I_{12}I_{13}}}{\overline{I_{14}I_{13}}}$$

For numerical values, this is computed graphically or via vector-loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_B &= \vec{V}_A + \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{AB} \\
\vec{V}_B &= \vec{\omega}_4 \times \vec{r}_{O_4B}
\end{aligned}
$$

These two equations (solved for $\vec{\omega}_3$ and $\vec{\omega}_4$) yield the angular velocity of the rocker and the angular velocity of the coupler.

## 4.5 Pantograph Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["Tracing Point T input end"] -->|trace input curve| nodeP2["Point T moves with velocity V_T"]
    nodeP2["Point T moves with velocity V_T"] --> nodeP3["Parallelogram O-A-B-P"]
    nodeP3["Parallelogram O-A-B-P"] --> nodeP4["Fixed pivot O immobile"]
    nodeP3["Parallelogram O-A-B-P"] --> nodeP5["Drawing Point P output end"]
    nodeP5["Drawing Point P output end"] -->|draw output curve| nodeP6["Point P moves with velocity V_P"]
    nodeP6["Point P moves with velocity V_P"] --> nodeP7["Scale factor k equals OT divided by OP"]
    nodeP7["Scale factor k equals OT divided by OP"] --> nodeP8{"Check design intent"}
    nodeP8{"Check design intent"} -->|k greater than 1| nodeP9["Drawing is ENLARGED version"]
    nodeP8{"Check design intent"} -->|k less than 1| nodeP10["Drawing is REDUCED version"]
    nodeP8{"Check design intent"} -->|k equals 1| nodeP11["Drawing is IDENTICAL copy"]

    classDef trace fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef panto fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000

    class nodeP3,nodeP4,nodeP5,nodeP6,nodeP7 panto

```

## 4.6 Block Diagram ŌĆö Synthesis Procedure for Planar Complex Mechanisms

```mermaid

    nodeX2["Choose Link Count n greater than 4"] --> nodeX3["Choose Topology Stephenson or Watt"]

    nodeX4["Check Kutzbach F greater than 1"] --> nodeX5["Apply Grashof Rule s plus l less than p plus q"]
    nodeX5["Apply Grashof Rule s plus l less than p plus q"] --> nodeX6["Synthesize Link Lengths using Burmester or Freudenstein"]
    nodeX6["Synthesize Link Lengths using Burmester or Freudenstein"] --> nodeX7["Verify Transmission Angle tau greater than 40 deg"]
```
