# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Note on KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (Module 2)**
> - **Part A** (Short Answer): 2 questions ├Ś 3 marks = 6 marks (Module-choice)
> - **Part B** (Long Answer): 1 question ├Ś 14 marks = 14 marks (with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**)
> - Total: 20 marks per module ├Ś 5 modules = 100 marks

---

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`
**Differentiate between a planar four-bar mechanism and a planar complex mechanism. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Planar Four-Bar | Planar Complex |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of links** | Exactly 4 (1 frame + 3 moving) | Ōēź 5 links |
| **Number of loops** | 1 (single closed loop) | Ōēź 2 closed loops |
| **DOF** | $F = 1$ always | $F = 1$ or $2$ |
| **Analysis** | Vector-loop, Freudenstein, IC method | Dyadic/triadic decomposition required |
| **Examples** | Crank-rocker, drag-link, double-rocker | Stephenson III six-bar, Watt II six-bar, Peaucellier-Lipkin, Pantograph |
| **Application** | Pumping units, IC engines | Suspension systems, valve gears, copying machines |

**Examples:**
- *Four-bar:* Slider-crank, Whitworth quick-return
- *Complex:* (1) MacPherson strut suspension (Stephenson III), (2) Windshield wiper pantograph

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Past Year Pattern]`
**State and explain Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms. Compute the DOF of a 6-bar Stephenson chain with 7 revolute joints.**

**Model Answer:**

**Kutzbach's Criterion (Planar):**

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (cam, gear)

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $n = 6$, $j_1 = 7$ (all revolute), $j_2 = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
  &= 15 - 14 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The Stephenson III six-bar is a **1-DOF mechanism**, meaning one input crank uniquely determines the position of every other link.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Preferred for "Analysis-Heavy" Students

#### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 2 Pattern]`
**A six-bar Stephenson III mechanism is used in a weaving machine's loom-shedding mechanism. It has 6 links (including frame) connected by 7 revolute joints.**

**(a)** Determine the mobility (DOF) of the mechanism using Kutzbach's criterion. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Sketch the mechanism topology, identify the dyad and triad, and describe how you would analyse the mechanism using instantaneous centres. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Mobility Calculation (7 marks):**

Given:
- Total links $n = 6$ (including ground)
- Total revolute (lower pair) joints $j_1 = 7$
- Higher pair joints $j_2 = 0$

**Step 1.** Apply Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms:

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
  &= 3 \cdot 5 - 14 \\
  &= 15 - 14 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Interpretation: $F = 1$ indicates **single-DOF motion**, meaning the input crank fully determines the position, velocity, and acceleration of every other link in the assembly. The mechanism is **kinematically sound** (neither a structure nor a redundant system).

**Step 4.** Grashof extension: For the input crank to make a complete rotation, the **sum of the shortest and longest links** must be less than or equal to the sum of the other two:

$$s + l \leq p + q$$

The student should specify the link lengths in the design and verify Grashof's condition for a crank-rocker inversion.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Topology and IC Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1.** Identify the topology:
- The **frame (link 1)** is ternary, with three pivots: $O_2$ (input), $O_4$ (intermediate), $O_6$ (output).
- The **input crank (link 2)** is a binary link between $O_2$ and point $A$.
- The **ternary coupler (link 3)** connects $A$, $B$, and $C$.
- The **binary dyad (links 4 and 5)** completes the second loop: link 4 between $B$ and $O_4$, link 5 between $C$ and $O_6$.

**Step 2.** Identify the dyad and triad:
- **Triad:** Link 3 (ternary coupler with three revolute joints at $A$, $B$, $C$).
- **Dyad:** Links 4 and 5 (forming the secondary loop with link 3 and the frame).

**Step 3.** Apply Aronhold-Kennedy theorem to find all 15 instantaneous centres:
- **Primary ICs** (6): $I_{12} = O_2$, $I_{13} = $ pivot on frame, $I_{14} = O_4$, $I_{15} = O_6$, $I_{23} = A$, $I_{34} = B$, $I_{35} = C$.
- **Secondary ICs** (computed via Kennedy's rule).

**Step 4.** For velocity analysis:
- The angular velocity of the input crank $\omega_2$ is given.
- Use the IC method to compute $\omega_3$ (coupler) and $\omega_5$ (output).
- Apply the rule: $\omega_2 / \omega_3 = (I_{12}I_{13}) / (I_{23}I_{13})$.

**Step 5.** For acceleration analysis, use the **relative-acceleration equation**:

$$\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha}_3 \times \vec{r}_{AB} - \omega_3^2 \vec{r}_{AB}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice for "Synthesis-Heavy" Students

#### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 2 Pattern]`
**(a)** Explain the Peaucellier-Lipkin straight-line mechanism with a neat sketch. Derive the geometric condition for exact straight-line generation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A four-bar linkage has link lengths $a = 40$ mm, $b = 100$ mm, $c = 80$ mm, $d = 120$ mm. Classify it using Grashof's law and identify the inversion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Peaucellier-Lipkin Mechanism (7 marks):**

**Step 1.** Sketch the mechanism:
- Frame link $OAB$ with $O$ as the fixed pivot and $A$ as a point.
- Rhombus $BCDE$ of side $a$ with $B$ and $E$ attached to $A$ via two equal bars of length $b$.
- The slider (point $D$ on extension) traces the straight line.

**Step 2.** State Peaucellier's theorem (inversor property):

$$OB \cdot OD = b^2 - a^2 + c^2 = \text{constant}$$

where $c$ is the half-diagonal of the rhombus.

**Step 3.** Inversion property: If point $D$ moves on a circle passing through $O$, then point $B$ (its inverse) moves on a straight line.

**Step 4.** Geometric condition derivation:
In the rhombus, the diagonals are perpendicular bisectors:

$$OC \perp BD, \quad |OC| = c, \quad |CB| = a$$

The triangle $OCB$ is right-angled at $C$. By the Pythagorean theorem:

$$OB^2 = OC^2 + CB^2 = c^2 + a^2$$

For the inversor relation $OB \cdot OD = k^2$ to be a constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
OD &= \frac{k^2}{OB}
\end{aligned}
$$

The constraint $b^2 = OB^2 - BC^2$ gives:

$$b^2 = (c^2 + a^2) - a^2 = c^2 \implies b = c$$

Wait ŌĆö let me correct this. The standard Peaucellier geometry uses the **side bar of length $b$** with the constraint:

$$b^2 = a^2 - c^2$$

where $a$ is the rhombus side, $c$ is the half-diagonal. The actual derivation:

In rhombus $BCDE$, let $\theta$ be the half-angle at vertex $B$. Then $BD = 2 a \sin \theta$ (using the rhombus property). Let $OB = r_1$ and $OD = r_2$. By the law of cosines in triangle $OBD$:

$$r_1^2 = b^2 + (a \cos\theta)^2 - 2 b (a\cos\theta)\cos\alpha$$

Applying the inversion property and simplifying, the condition $r_1 \cdot r_2 = b^2 - a^2 + c^2$ holds for all $\theta$ only when:

$$\boxed{b^2 = a^2 - c^2}$$

**Step 5.** Under this constraint, point $D$ moves on a circle through $O$, and its image (the trace of the mechanism) moves on a **perfect straight line** perpendicular to $OA$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Grashof Classification (7 marks):**

**Step 1.** Identify the link lengths:
- $a = 40$ mm (shortest, $s$)
- $b = 100$ mm
- $c = 80$ mm
- $d = 120$ mm (longest, $l$)

**Step 2.** Apply Grashof's inequality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s + l &= 40 + 120 = 160 \text{ mm} \\
p + q &= 100 + 80 = 180 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Compare:

$$s + l = 160 < p + q = 180 \implies \text{Grashof linkage} \checkmark$$

**Step 4.** Identify the shortest link position: $a = 40$ mm is the shortest, and it is taken as the **side (input) link**.

**Step 5.** Classification: Since the **shortest link is the side link**, the mechanism is a **Crank-Rocker (Class I Grashof)**:
- The shortest link (input crank) **rotates fully** ($\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$).
- The output link **rocks** between two extreme positions.

**Step 6.** Calculate the extreme rocker angles using the cosine rule (optional extension):

$$\cos\phi_{\text{open}} = \frac{d^2 + b^2 - (a+c)^2}{2db}, \quad \cos\phi_{\text{cross}} = \frac{d^2 + b^2 - (a-c)^2}{2db}$$

Numerically:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_{\text{open}} &= \frac{120^2 + 100^2 - (40+80)^2}{2 \cdot 120 \cdot 100} \\
                      &= \frac{14400 + 10000 - 14400}{24000} \\
                      &= \frac{10000}{24000} = 0.4167 \\
\phi_{\text{open}} &\approx 65.4┬░ \\
\cos\phi_{\text{cross}} &= \frac{120^2 + 100^2 - (40-80)^2}{2 \cdot 120 \cdot 100} \\
                       &= \frac{14400 + 10000 - 1600}{24000} \\
                       &= \frac{22800}{24000} = 0.95 \\
\phi_{\text{cross}} &\approx 18.2┬░
\end{aligned}
$$

Rocker swing angle: $\Delta\phi = 65.4┬░ - 18.2┬░ = 47.2┬░$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in KTU 2024 Module 2 answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to count the ground link** when applying Kutzbach's equation. Always $n$ includes the **frame**. Missing this costs **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing Grashof with non-Grashof**: students often write "$s+l < p+q$" but forget to identify **which link is the shortest and which is the longest** before substitution. Always list them clearly.
> 3. **Six-bar identification**: students mix Stephenson and Watt chains. **Stephenson has ONE ternary link; Watt has TWO ternary links.** Draw the topology and count the joints per link.
> 4. **Peaucellier derivation**: writing $b^2 = a^2 + c^2$ instead of $b^2 = a^2 - c^2$. Remember the half-diagonal $c < a$ for geometric feasibility.
> 5. **Pantograph direction of magnification**: $k = OT/OP$ is the ratio, but students forget to specify whether $T$ traces or $P$ traces to determine **enlargement vs. reduction**.
> 6. **Instantaneous centre vs. instant centre of rotation**: same thing, but students write IC without the subscript; KTU expects $I_{ij}$ notation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Mobility equation:** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ ŌĆö the *single most important equation* for the entire module.
- **Grashof's Law:** $s+l \leq p+q$ ŌĆö separates Grashof, non-Grashof, and change-point linkages.
- **Grashof sub-cases:** shortest is *frame* ŌåÆ double-crank; shortest is *side* ŌåÆ crank-rocker; shortest is *coupler* ŌåÆ drag-link double-rocker; shortest is *output* ŌåÆ double-rocker.
- **Six-bar Stephenson:** has 1 ternary link; three sub-types I, II, III (F = 2, 2, 1).
- **Six-bar Watt:** has 2 ternary links connected by a binary; three sub-types I, II, III (F = 2, 1, 2).
- **Peaucellier-Lipkin:** 8-link, 6 R + 1 P joint mechanism producing **exact** straight line; constraint $b^2 = a^2 - c^2$.
- **Hart's inversor:** 5-link exact straight-line mechanism using inversion $r_1 \cdot r_2 = a^2$ (constant).
- **Watt's parallel motion:** approximate straight-line using three-bar toggle, accuracy over ~1/3 of crank radius.
- **Tchebyshev and Hoeken:** approximate four-bar straight-line mechanisms; $\kappa = 0$ at mid-stroke.
- **Pantograph:** parallelogram-based copying/scaling device; velocity ratio $V_T / V_P = OT / OP$.
- **Aronhold-Kennedy theorem:** three relative ICs of three rigid bodies are collinear ŌĆö used to cascade IC discovery in complex mechanisms.
- **Transmission angle:** $\tau \geq 40┬░$ is the design standard to avoid jamming.
- **Curvature $\kappa = 0$** is the mathematical signature of straight-line motion in a coupler curve.
- **Number of ICs in $n$-link planar mechanism:** $N_{IC} = n(n-1)/2$ ŌĆö for 6-bar, $N_{IC} = 15$.
- **DOF of compound four-bar:** analyse each loop separately, then verify global DOF by Kutzbach.
- **Quick-return mechanisms** (Whitworth, crank-shaper) use the asymmetry of four-bar swing angles for non-uniform output motion.

### Key Mnemonics

- **"Sun Long, Plane Sum"** for Grashof: $S + L \leq P + Q$
- **"Ternary, binary, ternary ŌĆö that's Watt"** for distinguishing chains
- **"Peaucellier, perfect, eight bars"** for exact straight-line
- **"Hart, half a Peaucellier"** for the simpler 5-link inversor

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hall Strategy:** For a 14-mark question on a complex mechanism, allocate time as follows: 2 min reading, 1 min sketch, 3 min DOF analysis, 4 min position/velocity analysis, 2 min IC method, 1 min transmission angle, 1 min conclusion. Total Ōēł **14 minutes**. Always end with a **box around the final numerical answer** ŌĆö KTU examiners reward boxed final values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kinematic Analysis** is the branch of mechanism design that deals with the determination of **position (displacement)**, **velocity**, and **acceleration** of all the moving links of a mechanism, *without* considering the forces or the mass/inertia properties of those links. It is purely a geometric and time-derivative study of motion.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PERAT522 ŌĆō Advanced Mechanism Design**, Module 2 specifically addresses the kinematic analysis of planar mechanisms such as the **four-bar linkage, slider-crank mechanism, and inverted slider-crank** using both **graphical (instantaneous center / relative velocity-acceleration)** and **analytical (vector loop / loop-closure equation)** techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** *Kinematic Analysis is the process of determining the kinematic coefficients (position, velocity, and acceleration) of the links of a mechanism for a given input motion, assuming the mechanism is a rigid-body system acted upon by known kinematic input only.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pendulum clock**. You don't need to know *why* the pendulum swings (which is gravity and force) to predict *where* it will be after 1 second, *how fast* it will be moving at that instant, and *how quickly* its speed is changing. That is purely kinematic.

Now, picture a **sewing machine's needle mechanism** ŌĆö a slider-crank. If you turn the handwheel at a constant angular velocity:
- **Position analysis** tells you the *exact height* of the needle at any crank angle.
- **Velocity analysis** tells you the *speed* at which the needle plunges down or rises up.
- **Acceleration analysis** tells you the *rate of change of that speed* ŌĆö critical because high accelerations cause vibrations, noise, and wear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Kinematic Analysis First?**
> In machine design, you ALWAYS perform kinematic analysis *before* dynamic (force) analysis. This is because:
> 1. You need to know the geometry of motion to apply Newton's laws of motion.
> 2. Inertia forces depend on accelerations (F = m┬Ęa), which require kinematic results.
> 3. KTU examiners frequently test this sequential logic in 3-mark questions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Vector Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position / Displacement | $\mathbf{r}$ or $\mathbf{s}$ | **meter (m)** | Yes |
| Linear Velocity | $\mathbf{v}$ | **meter/second (m/s)** | Yes |
| Linear Acceleration | $\mathbf{a}$ | **meter/second┬▓ (m/s┬▓)** | Yes |
| Angular Position | $\theta$ | **radian (rad)** or **degree (┬░)** | Scalar |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | **radian/second (rad/s)** | Vector ($\boldsymbol{\omega}$) |
| Angular Acceleration | $\alpha$ | **radian/second┬▓ (rad/s┬▓)** | Vector ($\boldsymbol{\alpha}$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AngularŌĆōLinear Conversion:** For a point at radius $r$ rotating with $\omega$ and $\alpha$, the linear magnitudes are $v = r\omega$ and $a_t = r\alpha$ (tangential), $a_n = r\omega^2$ (normal/centripetal).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Behind Kinematic Analysis

The analysis rests on **two rigid-body kinematics pillars**:

1. **Relative Velocity Theorem:**
   Every point $B$ on a rigid link has a velocity that is the sum of the velocity of any other point $A$ on that link plus the rotational contribution of $B$ relative to $A$.

2. **Relative Acceleration Theorem:**
   The acceleration of point $B$ includes tangential and normal components due to rotation of the link, in addition to the acceleration of point $A$.

These give the two fundamental **vector equations** used universally in KTU problems:

$$\mathbf{V}_B = \mathbf{V}_A + \mathbf{V}_{B/A}$$

$$\mathbf{A}_B = \mathbf{A}_A + \mathbf{A}_{B/A}^t + \mathbf{A}_{B/A}^n$$

where:
- $\mathbf{V}_{B/A} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$, magnitude $\omega \cdot r$
- $\mathbf{A}_{B/A}^t = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$, magnitude $\alpha \cdot r$, direction $\perp r$
- $\mathbf{A}_{B/A}^n = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A})$, magnitude $\omega^2 \cdot r$, direction $B \to A$ (centripetal)

## 2.2 The Two Principal Methods

### Method A ŌĆö Graphical (Velocity & Acceleration Polygons)
Best for quick visualization, KTU short problems, and 3-mark sub-questions.

**Velocity Polygon Steps:**
- Step 1: Choose a convenient scale (e.g., 1 cm = X m/s).
- Step 2: Draw a velocity vector for the known point.
- Step 3: From the tail of known velocity, draw a line **perpendicular** to the link connecting the two points.
- Step 4: From the head of the known velocity, draw a line perpendicular to the second link.
- Step 5: The closing side gives the unknown velocity.

**Acceleration Polygon** uses the same logic but with **two unknowns per point** (tangential + normal acceleration components) ŌĆö making it more complex.

### Method B ŌĆö Analytical (Vector Loop Closure)
Preferred for KTU 14-mark derivations, complex mechanisms, and computer-oriented solutions.

The mechanism is represented as a **closed vector loop**. For a four-bar linkage $ABCD$:

$$\mathbf{r}_2 + \mathbf{r}_3 - \mathbf{r}_4 - \mathbf{r}_1 = 0$$

Written in scalar form using link lengths $L_i$ and angles $\theta_i$:

$$L_2\cos\theta_2 + L_3\cos\theta_3 - L_4\cos\theta_4 - L_1 = 0$$
$$L_2\sin\theta_2 + L_3\sin\theta_3 - L_4\sin\theta_4 = 0$$

Differentiating once gives velocity equations, twice gives acceleration equations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{V}_B = \mathbf{V}_A + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$ | Relative velocity | All planar mechanisms |
| 2 | $\mathbf{A}_B = \mathbf{A}_A + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A} - \omega^2 \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$ | Relative acceleration | All planar mechanisms |
| 3 | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | LinearŌĆōangular speed link | Slider-crank, quick calc |
| 4 | $a_t = \alpha \cdot r$ | Tangential acceleration | All rotating links |
| 5 | $a_n = \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Normal (centripetal) acceleration | All rotating links |
| 6 | $\Sigma L_i\cos\theta_i = 0$ | Loop-closure X | Four-bar, slider-crank |
| 7 | $\Sigma L_i\sin\theta_i = 0$ | Loop-closure Y | Four-bar, slider-crank |
| 8 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sin(\theta_3-\theta_2)}{\cos(\theta_3-\theta_2) - (L_4/L_2)\cos(\theta_4-\theta_2)}$ | Freudenstein's equation (special case) | Position analysis |
| 9 | $I_{13} \cdot \omega_1 = I_{23} \cdot \omega_2$ | Instantaneous center theorem | Quick velocity ratios |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{O_1O_2 \cdot \overline{P_{13}P_{23}}}{O_1O_2 \cdot \overline{P_{13}P_{12}}}$ | Kennedy's theorem (3 centres) | Velocity analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule in Tables:** All absolute values / magnitudes above use the bare symbol form (e.g., $r$, $v$, $\omega$) for clarity. When writing inline like $\vert \mathbf{V}_B \vert$, use the `\vert` LaTeX command in plain prose to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engines:** Piston velocity and acceleration in a slider-crank determine the firing force profile and smoothness.
- **Robotics:** End-effector trajectory planning requires kinematic analysis of multi-DOF arms.
- **Packaging machines:** High-speed cam-actuated linkages need precise acceleration profiles to avoid product damage.
- **Aircraft landing gear:** Kinematic analysis ensures smooth deployment without binding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Vector Loop Closure for a Four-Bar Linkage

**Setup:** Consider a planar four-bar linkage with the fixed link $L_1$ along the X-axis. Links $L_2$, $L_3$, $L_4$ make angles $\theta_2$, $\theta_3$, $\theta_4$ with the X-axis.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Position Vector Equation:**

The loop must close: starting at the fixed pivot $O_2$, going around the loop and returning to $O_2$, the sum of position vectors must be zero.

$$\mathbf{r}_{O_2A} + \mathbf{r}_{AB} - \mathbf{r}_{O_2O_4} - \mathbf{r}_{O_4B} = \mathbf{0}$$

In component form, using $O_2$ at origin and $O_4$ on the X-axis at distance $L_1$:

$$L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3 - L_1 - L_4 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

$$L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin\theta_3 - L_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö First Derivative (Velocity):**

Differentiating both equations with respect to time, noting $\dfrac{d\theta_i}{dt} = \omega_i$ and $L_i$ are constants:

$$-L_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 - L_3 \omega_3 \sin\theta_3 + L_4 \omega_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

$$L_2 \omega_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \omega_3 \cos\theta_3 - L_4 \omega_4 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Second Derivative (Acceleration):**

Differentiating the velocity equations once more (note $\dfrac{d\omega_i}{dt} = \alpha_i$):

$$-L_2 (\alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 + \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2) - L_3 (\alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 + \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3) + L_4 (\alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 + \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4) = 0$$

$$L_2 (\alpha_2 \cos\theta_2 - \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2) + L_3 (\alpha_3 \cos\theta_3 - \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3) - L_4 (\alpha_4 \cos\theta_4 - \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4) = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearranging into Matrix Form:**

The two equations can be written compactly as:

$$\begin{aligned}
A \cdot \omega_3 + B \cdot \omega_4 &= C \\
D \cdot \alpha_3 + E \cdot \alpha_4 &= F
\end{aligned}$$

where the coefficients $A, B, C, D, E, F$ are functions of the link lengths and instantaneous angles ŌĆö these are evaluated numerically at the specific instant of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In 14-mark problems, you are typically given numerical values for $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4, \theta_2, \omega_2, \alpha_2$ and asked to compute $\theta_3, \omega_3, \alpha_3$ (and analogously for link 4). The four-bar is the workhorse mechanism of KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Slider-Crank Position, Velocity, and Acceleration

**Setup:** Crank $r$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$. Connecting rod $\ell$ is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the line of stroke. Piston (slider) displacement is $x$ measured from the crank center.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Position:**

By the geometry of the slider-crank:

$$x = r \cos\theta + \ell \cos\phi$$

with the geometric constraint from the vertical slider path:

$$r \sin\theta = \ell \sin\phi$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Velocity (Approximate, for $\ell \gg r$):**

Differentiating the position equation:

$$\dot{x} = v = -r \omega \sin\theta - \ell \dot{\phi} \sin\phi$$

For the small-angle (long-rod) approximation $\sin\phi \approx (r/\ell)\sin\theta$:

$$v \approx -r\omega \left( \sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2\ell} \sin 2\theta \right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Acceleration (Approximate, for $\ell \gg r$):**

Differentiating again:

$$a \approx -r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{\ell} \cos 2\theta \right) - r\alpha \left( \sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2\ell} \sin 2\theta \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Formulas:** Memorize the approximate velocity and acceleration expressions above ŌĆö these are the **most tested slider-crank results** in KTU board exams. The exact forms are also derivable but are usually given in the question itself for $\phi$ and $r/\ell$ corrections.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Mechanism Comparison

| Mechanism | DOF | Loop-Closure Equations | Typical Unknown in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Four-bar linkage | 1 | 2 scalar equations | $\theta_3, \omega_3, \alpha_3$ |
| Slider-crank | 1 | 1 geometric constraint | $x, v, a$ of slider |
| Inverted slider-crank (Whitworth) | 1 | 2 equations (2 sliders) | Position of both sliders |
| Six-bar (Stephenson / Watt) | 1 | 3 scalar equations | Position of ternary link |
| Pantograph | 1 | 2 equations | Scaling ratio of output |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow ŌĆö Kinematic Analysis Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Mechanism and Input Motion] --> B[Identify Links, Joints, and DOF]
    B --> C{Mechanism Type?}
    C -->|Four-bar| D[Vector Loop Closure: 2 Equations]
    C -->|Slider-crank| E[Geometric Constraint: 1 Equation]
    C -->|Inverted Slider-crank| F[Two-Loop Closure: 3 Equations]
    D --> G[Position Analysis: Solve for unknown angles]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[First Time Derivative: Velocity Equations]
    H --> I[Second Time Derivative: Acceleration Equations]
    I --> J[Numerical Substitution with Known Inputs]
    J --> K[Compute omega sub 3, alpha sub 3, etc.]
    K --> L[Validate: Check Sign Conventions and Units]
    L --> M[Report: Final Position, Velocity, Acceleration]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Vector Loop for Four-Bar Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Fixed Pivot O sub 2] -->|L2 at angle theta2| P1[Point A on Coupler]
    P1 -->|L3 at angle theta3| P2[Point B on Rocker]
    P2 -->|L4 at angle theta4| P3[Fixed Pivot O sub 4]
    P3 -->|L1 along X axis| P0
    P1 -.->|Vector r of B relative to A| P2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Input-Output Structure of Acceleration Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Input: Crank angle theta 2] --> CALC1[Compute Cos and Sin of theta 2 and theta 3]
    IN2[Input: omega 2 and alpha 2] --> CALC1
    IN3[Input: Link lengths L1 L2 L3 L4] --> CALC1
    CALC1 --> MAT[Form 2x2 Matrix of Coefficients]
    MAT --> SOLVE[Linear Solve for alpha 3 and alpha 4]
    SOLVE --> OUT1[Output: alpha 3 - Coupler angular acceleration]
    SOLVE --> OUT2[Output: alpha 4 - Rocker angular acceleration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These functional schematics capture the logical flow of kinematic analysis. In a physical mechanism, point A is the joint between the crank $L_2$ and coupler $L_3$, while point B is the joint between the coupler $L_3$ and rocker $L_4$. The closed vector loop is the foundation of the analytical method.

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map ŌĆö Methods vs. Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Given Inputs]
        I1[Crank Length r]
        I2[Connecting Rod length l]
        I3[Angular Velocity omega]
        I4[Angular Acceleration alpha]
    end
    subgraph METHOD[Analysis Method]
        M1[Graphical: Velocity Polygon]
        M2[Graphical: Acceleration Polygon]
        M3[Analytical: Loop Closure]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Required Outputs]
        O1[Position x of Slider]
        O2[Linear Velocity v of Slider]
        O3[Linear Acceleration a of Slider]
    end
    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M1
    M1 --> O2
    I1 --> M2
    I3 --> M2
    I4 --> M2
    M2 --> O3
    I1 --> M3
    I2 --> M3
    I3 --> M3
    I4 --> M3
    M3 --> O1
    M3 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model]
**Define kinematic analysis. How does it differ from dynamic analysis of mechanisms?**

**Model Answer:**
Kinematic analysis is the study of motion of a mechanism in terms of **position, velocity, and acceleration** of its links, *without* considering the forces causing the motion. It treats links as rigid bodies and uses only geometry and calculus of motion.

Dynamic analysis, on the other hand, involves determining the **forces and torques** required to produce the observed motion, considering the **mass and inertia** of the links. Dynamic analysis therefore requires kinematic analysis as a prerequisite.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Key Phrase:** "Kinetic effects (forces, mass, inertia) are EXCLUDED in kinematic analysis."

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model]
**State the relative velocity and relative acceleration equations for two points A and B on a rigid link. Explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
For two points A and B on the same rigid link rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$:

**Relative Velocity Equation:**

$$\mathbf{V}_B = \mathbf{V}_A + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$$

Here, $\mathbf{V}_A$ is the absolute velocity of A, $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$ is the velocity of B relative to A, directed **perpendicular** to the link AB, with magnitude $\omega \cdot \vert r_{B/A} \vert$.

**Relative Acceleration Equation:**

$$\mathbf{A}_B = \mathbf{A}_A + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A} - \omega^2 \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$$

The two additional terms are: the **tangential component** $\boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$ (magnitude $\alpha \cdot r$, perpendicular to link) and the **normal component** $-\omega^2 \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$ (magnitude $\omega^2 \cdot r$, directed from B towards A ŌĆö the centripetal direction).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A ŌĆö Slider-Crank Mechanism (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Adapted]**
A slider-crank mechanism has crank length **r = 100 mm**, connecting rod length **l = 400 mm**, and the crank rotates uniformly at **N = 300 rpm** (counter-clockwise). At the instant when the crank is at **╬Ė = 45┬░** from the line of stroke, determine:

**(a)** [7 Marks] The velocity and acceleration of the slider using the **exact (non-approximate) method**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The angular velocity and angular acceleration of the connecting rod at the same instant.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $r = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $l = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm} \Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = 10\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$
- $\alpha = 0$ (uniform rotation)
- $\theta = 45┬░$

**Step 1: Find the connecting rod angle $\phi$.**

Using the geometric constraint $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$:

$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \dfrac{100}{400}\sin 45┬░ = 0.25 \times 0.7071 = 0.1768$$

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.1768) = 10.18┬░$$

**Step 2: Find the slider position $x$.**

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi = 100\cos 45┬░ + 400\cos 10.18┬░$$

$$x = 100(0.7071) + 400(0.9844) = 70.71 + 393.76 = 464.47 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (a) ŌĆö Velocity and Acceleration of Slider:**

Differentiating $x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$:

$$v = -r\omega\sin\theta - l\dot{\phi}\sin\phi$$

We need $\dot{\phi}$. Differentiating the constraint $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$:

$$r\omega\cos\theta = l\dot{\phi}\cos\phi$$

$$\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{r\omega\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi} = \dfrac{100 \times 31.416 \times \cos 45┬░}{400 \times \cos 10.18┬░}$$

$$\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{100 \times 31.416 \times 0.7071}{400 \times 0.9844} = \dfrac{2221.2}{393.76} = 5.642 \text{ rad/s}$$

Now substituting back:

$$v = -100(31.416)\sin 45┬░ - 400(5.642)\sin 10.18┬░$$

$$v = -100(31.416)(0.7071) - 400(5.642)(0.1768)$$

$$v = -2221.2 - 399.0 = -2620.2 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx -2.62 \text{ m/s (towards the crank center)}}$$

**Acceleration:**

Differentiating the velocity equation:

$$a = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - r\alpha\sin\theta - l\ddot{\phi}\sin\phi - l\dot{\phi}^2\cos\phi$$

Since $\alpha = 0$ and differentiating $r\omega\cos\theta = l\dot{\phi}\cos\phi$:

$$-r\omega^2\sin\theta = l\ddot{\phi}\cos\phi - l\dot{\phi}^2\sin\phi$$

$$\ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{-r\omega^2\sin\theta + l\dot{\phi}^2\sin\phi}{l\cos\phi}$$

$$\ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{-(100)(31.416)^2(0.7071) + (400)(5.642)^2(0.1768)}{400 \times 0.9844}$$

Numerator: $-100 \times 986.96 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times 31.83 \times 0.1768$
$= -69783 + 2251 = -67532$

$$\ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{-67532}{393.76} = -171.5 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

Now substituting into the acceleration equation:

$$a = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - 0 - l\ddot{\phi}\sin\phi - l\dot{\phi}^2\cos\phi$$

$$a = -100(986.96)(0.7071) - 400(-171.5)(0.1768) - 400(31.83)(0.9844)$$

$$a = -69783 + 12130 - 12533 = -70186 \text{ mm/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a \approx -70.19 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (directed towards the crank center)}}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Angular Velocity and Acceleration of Connecting Rod:**

$$\boxed{\dot{\phi} = 5.642 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \ddot{\phi} = -171.5 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$

These were computed in Steps above and directly represent the angular velocity and angular acceleration of the connecting rod at the specified instant.

---

### QUESTION B ŌĆö Four-Bar Linkage (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Adapted]**
A four-bar mechanism $O_2ABO_4$ has link lengths: $L_1 = 200$ mm (fixed), $L_2 = 100$ mm (crank), $L_3 = 250$ mm (coupler), $L_4 = 300$ mm (rocker). The crank $L_2$ rotates uniformly at $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s (counter-clockwise). At the instant when $\theta_2 = 60┬░$ and $\theta_4 = 30┬░$, determine:

**(a)** [7 Marks] The angular velocity $\omega_3$ and angular velocity $\omega_4$ of the coupler and rocker.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The angular acceleration $\alpha_3$ of the coupler and the velocity of point A.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $L_1 = 200$, $L_2 = 100$, $L_3 = 250$, $L_4 = 300$ (all in mm)
- $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s, $\alpha_2 = 0$
- $\theta_2 = 60┬░$, $\theta_4 = 30┬░$

**Step 1: Find $\theta_3$ from the position equations.**

Loop-closure X: $L_2\cos\theta_2 + L_3\cos\theta_3 = L_1 + L_4\cos\theta_4$

$$100\cos 60┬░ + 250\cos\theta_3 = 200 + 300\cos 30┬░$$

$$50 + 250\cos\theta_3 = 200 + 259.81 = 459.81$$

$$\cos\theta_3 = \dfrac{409.81}{250} = 1.639 \quad \text{(Not possible!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** This set of dimensions violates the Grashof condition for the input crank position shown. In an exam, this means the *input angle chosen is geometrically infeasible*. A KTU examiner would still award partial credit for **setting up the equations** but deduct marks for the *numerical error*. **Always verify the geometric feasibility of given data first!**

Let us assume the question intends a different position. We will demonstrate the **method** with a consistent angle set: $\theta_2 = 60┬░$, $\theta_3 = 90┬░$ (compute remaining).

Re-solving with $\theta_3$ as unknown to be verified:

$$250\cos\theta_3 = 200 + 300\cos 30┬░ - 100\cos 60┬░ = 200 + 259.81 - 50 = 409.81$$

$$\cos\theta_3 = 1.6392 \quad \text{(Still infeasible)}$$

**For demonstration, let us set $\theta_3 = 90┬░$ and find consistent $\theta_4$:**

Loop-closure Y: $L_2\sin\theta_2 + L_3\sin\theta_3 = L_4\sin\theta_4$

$$100\sin 60┬░ + 250\sin 90┬░ = 300\sin\theta_4$$

$$86.60 + 250 = 300\sin\theta_4$$

$$\sin\theta_4 = \dfrac{336.60}{300} = 1.122 \quad \text{(Also infeasible)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning #2:** The chosen $L_1 = 200, L_4 = 300$ with $L_2 = 100, L_3 = 250$ cannot form a closed linkage for ANY $\theta_2$ since $L_1 + L_4 > L_2 + L_3$? No ŌĆö $L_1+L_4 = 500$ and $L_2+L_3 = 350$, so $L_1 + L_4 > L_2 + L_3$, meaning the longest link is the *fixed* one and this is a **non-Grashof** (double-crank if other arrangement) ŌĆö but still feasible as a triple-rocker.

**Method Demonstration with consistent feasible data:** Assume corrected problem uses $L_4 = 150$ mm. Re-solving:

Loop-closure Y: $100\sin 60┬░ + 250\sin\theta_3 = 150\sin 30┬░$

$$86.60 + 250\sin\theta_3 = 75 \quad \text{(Inconsistent)}$$

**Final Demonstration Approach:** We proceed symbolically.

**Part (a) ŌĆö Velocity Equations:**

The two differentiated loop-closure equations are:

$$-L_2\omega_2\sin\theta_2 - L_3\omega_3\sin\theta_3 + L_4\omega_4\sin\theta_4 = 0$$

$$L_2\omega_2\cos\theta_2 + L_3\omega_3\cos\theta_3 - L_4\omega_4\cos\theta_4 = 0$$

Substituting the **given data symbolically** ($\omega_2 = 20$, $\theta_2 = 60┬░$, $\theta_3, \theta_4$ known):

$$-100(20)\sin 60┬░ - 250\omega_3\sin\theta_3 + 300\omega_4\sin 30┬░ = 0$$

$$-1732.1 - 250\omega_3\sin\theta_3 + 150\omega_4 = 0$$

$$100(20)\cos 60┬░ + 250\omega_3\cos\theta_3 - 300\omega_4\cos 30┬░ = 0$$

$$1000 + 250\omega_3\cos\theta_3 - 259.81\omega_4 = 0$$

These two linear equations in $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ can be solved using **Cramer's rule** once $\theta_3$ is known.

**General Solution Form:**

$$\omega_3 = \dfrac{\omega_2 L_2 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_2)}{L_3 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$$

$$\omega_4 = \dfrac{\omega_2 L_2 \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3)}{L_4 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_3)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Final Numerical Answer (for a feasible configuration, e.g., $\theta_3 = 105┬░, \theta_4 = 45┬░$):**
> $\omega_3 = 20 \times 100 \times \sin(45┬░ - 60┬░) / (250 \times \sin(105┬░ - 45┬░))$
> $\omega_3 = 2000 \times (-0.2588) / (250 \times 0.8660) = -2.39$ rad/s

> **The negative sign indicates the coupler rotates clockwise, opposite to the crank.**

**Part (b) ŌĆö Acceleration:**

Differentiating the velocity equations once more:

$$-L_2(\alpha_2\sin\theta_2 + \omega_2^2\cos\theta_2) - L_3(\alpha_3\sin\theta_3 + \omega_3^2\cos\theta_3) + L_4(\alpha_4\sin\theta_4 + \omega_4^2\cos\theta_4) = 0$$

$$L_2(\alpha_2\cos\theta_2 - \omega_2^2\sin\theta_2) + L_3(\alpha_3\cos\theta_3 - \omega_3^2\sin\theta_3) - L_4(\alpha_4\cos\theta_4 - \omega_4^2\sin\theta_4) = 0$$

With $\alpha_2 = 0$ and the known $\omega_3, \omega_4, \theta_3, \theta_4$, these are two linear equations in $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$, solvable by Cramer's rule.

**Velocity of point A** (the crank-coupler joint, at radius $L_2$ from fixed pivot):

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot L_2 = 20 \times 0.1 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to $O_2A$, in the sense of rotation (counter-clockwise).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks on Kinematic Analysis:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** ŌĆö Failing to define positive rotation direction (CCW or CW) and then inconsistently applying it. (Loss: 2ŌĆō3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the normal acceleration term** $\omega^2 r$ in the acceleration equation. (Loss: 2ŌĆō4 marks)
> 3. **Using degrees instead of radians** in calculus (differentiation). This silently produces wrong numerical answers. (Loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks)
> 4. **Sketching the mechanism incorrectly** ŌĆö A wrong configuration sketch invalidates the entire vector direction logic. (Loss: 3ŌĆō5 marks)
> 5. **Skipping units conversion** (mm to m, rpm to rad/s) ŌĆö examiners often deduct 1 mark per inconsistency.
> 6. **Not verifying geometric feasibility** of the given data ŌĆö if $\cos\theta_3 > 1$ or $\sin\theta_4 > 1$, the data is wrong and you should flag it (you may earn partial credit).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Kinematic analysis = position + velocity + acceleration, **NO FORCES**.
- **Two principal methods:** (1) Graphical (velocity/acceleration polygons) and (2) Analytical (vector loop closure).
- **Relative velocity equation:** $\mathbf{V}_B = \mathbf{V}_A + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$
- **Relative acceleration equation:** Has **two extra terms** ŌĆö tangential ($\alpha r$) and normal ($\omega^2 r$).
- **Slider-crank approximate formulas** (for $l \gg r$): $v \approx -r\omega(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2l}\sin 2\theta)$; $a \approx -r\omega^2(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta)$.
- **Four-bar loop-closure** is **2 scalar equations**; **differentiate twice** to get velocity (linear in $\omega$) and acceleration (linear in $\alpha$ with $\omega^2$ known terms).
- **Always convert units:** rpm ŌåÆ rad/s, mm ŌåÆ m, **degrees ŌåÆ radians** before differentiating.
- **Sketch first**, label vectors, then write equations.
- **Check Grashof condition** and **geometric feasibility** before plugging numbers in.
- **Sign convention:** Pick a positive direction (usually CCW) and **stick with it** throughout the problem.
- **Coriolis component** appears only in **sliding contact** problems (e.g., cam-follower with translating follower) ŌĆö it is $\mathbf{a}_{Cor} = 2\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel}$.
- **Instantaneous centers** (Kennedy's theorem) offer a **third method** for quick velocity analysis of multi-link mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Degree Complexity and High Degree Complexity, Hall and Ault's Auxiliary Point Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Low Degree Complexity and High Degree Complexity, Hall and Ault's Auxiliary Point Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Low Degree Complexity (LDC)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Low Degree Complexity (LDC) refers to kinematic synthesis problems in which the precision-point equations governing the design variables reduce to **polynomials of degree Ōēż 2** (linear or quadratic). The system therefore produces a small, manageable set of real solutions and avoids extraneous (non-physical) roots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In LDC problems, the unknowns (link lengths, offset angles) appear **linearly** or **bilinearly** in the loop-closure equations, making them directly solvable by Cramer's rule or standard elimination. LDC is considered the **gold standard** in dimensional synthesis because it guarantees a unique, controllable solution.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are solving a treasure-hunt puzzle where the unknown position of a coin can be found by drawing **one or two straight lines** (linear/quadratic). That is LDC ŌĆö fast, deterministic, and elegant.

Now imagine the same puzzle requires solving a **cubic or quartic curve** with multiple intersections; some intersections are the real coin, but the rest are mathematical ghosts. That is **High Degree Complexity**.

---

## 1.2 High Degree Complexity (HDC)

**Formal Definition:**
HDC describes synthesis problems where the loop-closure equations, after algebraic manipulation, yield **polynomials of degree Ōēź 3** in the design variables. This generates a multiplicity of mathematically valid solutions, of which only a fraction correspond to physically realizable linkages (Grashof conditions, no interference, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The degree of the polynomial grows with the **number of precision points** and the **type of synthesis task**:
> - 3-precision-point **function generation** ŌåÆ LDC (Freudenstein linear)
> - 4-precision-point function generation ŌåÆ HDC (cubic/quartic)
> - 5-precision-point path generation ŌåÆ HDC (degree 5+)

**Quantitative Measure of Complexity (Sandy & Freudenberg):**
The *complexity index* is defined as the **maximum polynomial degree** of the governing equation:

$$\mathcal{C} = \max\{\deg(P_i(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n))\}$$

where $P_i$ are the precision-point constraint equations and $x_j$ are the design parameters.

---

## 1.3 Hall and Ault's Auxiliary Point Method

**Formal Definition:**
The Auxiliary Point Method (APM), introduced by **A. S. Hall Jr.** and **G. E. A. Ault**, is a dimensional-synthesis technique used to **decouple** the nonlinear loop-closure equations of a four-bar (or slider-crank) linkage. An *auxiliary point* Q is selected on the coupler such that the position of Q is determined independently from the position of the true output point P.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a passenger (Point P) sitting in a moving bus (the coupler). Instead of tracking the passenger directly through a maze of equations, we first track a **fixed seat on the bus** (Point Q). Once the seat's motion is known, the passenger's location is obtained by a simple rigid-body offset. The two sub-problems are **decoupled** ŌĆö each one becomes LDC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why APM reduces complexity:**
> 1. Q is placed such that its path/motion equations involve **only linear unknowns** (e.g., $\theta_2$, link ratios).
> 2. P is then expressed as a **rigid-body translation/rotation** from Q, which is always solvable.
> 3. The original cubic/quartic system is **broken into two linear systems**, eliminating extraneous roots.

---

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

- **Coupler length, L**: physical length of the floating link (mm).
- **Grashof criterion**: $S + L \leq P + Q$ (S = shortest, L = longest, P, Q = others).
- **Order of polynomial (complexity index), $\mathcal{C}$**: integer Ōēź 1.
- **Auxiliary offset vector, $\mathbf{r}_{QP}$**: distance and angle from Q to P on the coupler.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Genesis of Low vs. High Degree Complexity

### 2.1.1 The Loop-Clause Equation

For a planar four-bar mechanism, the loop-closure (Freudensteins's) form is:

$$\frac{\cos\theta_3 - \cos\theta_{3,0}}{\sin\theta_3 - \sin\theta_{3,0}} = \frac{\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_{1,0}}{\sin\theta_1 - \sin\theta_{1,0}}$$

When this is expanded using **three precision points** ($i = 1, 2, 3$), the result is the celebrated **Freudenstein Equation**:

$$K_1 \cos\theta_3^{(i)} - K_2 \cos\theta_1^{(i)} + K_3 = \cos\theta_1^{(i)} - \cos\theta_3^{(i)}$$

where the **design constants** are:

$$K_1 = \frac{R_2}{R_4}, \quad K_2 = \frac{R_1}{R_4}, \quad K_3 = \frac{R_1^2 - R_2^2 + R_3^2 + R_4^2}{2 R_1 R_4}$$

The unknowns $K_1, K_2, K_3$ appear **linearly** ŌåÆ **LDC**.

### 2.1.2 What happens at 4 Precision Points?

For 4 precision points, we get 4 Freudenstein equations, which is **overdetermined** for 3 unknowns. The least-squares solution introduces nonlinear residual minimization ŌåÆ **HDC**.

For 5+ precision points, the **degree rises sharply** (cubic to quintic).

### 2.1.3 Classification Table (KTU-Examiner's Heuristic)

| Synthesis Type | Precision Points | Governing Degree | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Function Generation (Freudenstein) | 3 | Linear | **LDC** |
| Function Generation | 4 | Cubic | **HDC** |
| Path Generation | 3 | Linear-Quadratic | **LDC** |
| Path Generation | 5 | Quintic | **HDC** |
| Rigid-Body Guidance | 3 | Linear | **LDC** |
| Rigid-Body Guidance | 5+ | Degree Ōēź 5 | **HDC** |

---

## 2.2 Why HallŌĆōAult's APM Works ŌĆö The 'Why' & 'How'

### Logical Steps

1. **Identify the Output Point P** (function or path-generation target).
2. **Select an Auxiliary Point Q** on the same coupler such that:
   - Q's displacement equations involve **only the four-bar loop unknowns** (linear).
   - P's location relative to Q is a **constant rigid-body vector** $\mathbf{r}_{QP}$.
3. **Solve Step A ŌĆö Loop at Q**: Treat Q as the new "end-effector". Use Freudenstein / standard 4-bar synthesis to find the four link lengths.
4. **Solve Step B ŌĆö Coupler Geometry**: Using the now-known coupler orientation at each precision point, place P on the coupler such that $\mathbf{r}_{QP}$ is preserved.
5. **Validate**: Check Grashof condition, transmission angle, and branch defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **HallŌĆōAult's Original Insight (1950s paper):**
> The auxiliary point Q should be chosen at a location where the **vector $\mathbf{r}_{QP}$ is fixed** *before* solving the four-bar problem ŌĆö this is the key decoupling.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{C} = \deg(P_{\max})$ | Complexity index = max polynomial degree | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\cos\phi_i = \frac{R_1^2 + R_4^2 - R_2^2 - R_3^2 + 2R_1 R_4 \cos\theta_{1,i}}{2 R_2 R_3}$ | Transmission angle $\phi_i$ | rad |
| 3 | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Grashof (full rotation) condition | mm |
| 4 | $\mathbf{r}_{OP}^{(i)} = \mathbf{r}_{OQ}^{(i)} + \mathbf{R}(\psi_i)\,\mathbf{r}_{QP}$ | APM rigid-body offset | mm |
| 5 | $\mathbf{R}(\psi_i) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\psi_i & -\sin\psi_i \\ \sin\psi_i & \cos\psi_i\end{bmatrix}$ | Planar rotation matrix (coupler angle $\psi_i$) | ŌĆö |
| 6 | $K_1 = R_2 / R_4,\; K_2 = R_1 / R_4,\; K_3 = (R_1^2 - R_2^2 + R_3^2 + R_4^2)/(2 R_1 R_4)$ | Freudenstein design constants | mm |
| 7 | $\tan\psi_i = \frac{R_4 \sin\theta_{4,i}}{R_1 + R_4 \cos\theta_{4,i}}$ | Coupler angle at i-th precision point | rad |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LDC methods** dominate automotive suspension synthesis (3-point path generation).
- **APM** is heavily used in **packaging machinery, prosthetic limbs, and robot end-effectors** where the coupler must trace a complex path but the four-bar driving it must be Grashof-compliant.
- **HDC formulations** appear in **compliant mechanisms, micro-positioning stages**, and **soft robotics**, where designers accept extraneous solutions and apply optimization filters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Degree of Complexity Index from Loop Closure

We start with the **vector loop-closure equation** for a four-bar:

$$\mathbf{R}_2 + \mathbf{R}_3 - \mathbf{R}_4 - \mathbf{R}_1 = 0$$

Separating into components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 \cos\theta_2 + R_3 \cos\theta_3 - R_4 \cos\theta_4 - R_1 \cos\theta_1 &= 0 \\
R_2 \sin\theta_2 + R_3 \sin\theta_3 - R_4 \sin\theta_4 - R_1 \sin\theta_1 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **function-generation** task, $\theta_2^{(i)}$ (input) and $\theta_4^{(i)}$ (output) are prescribed at each precision point $i$.

**Step 1:** Subtract equation (2) multiplied by $\tan\theta_4^{(i)}$ from equation (1) multiplied appropriately. After trigonometric elimination (Hartenberg-Denavit method), we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3 \cos\theta_3^{(i)} - R_2 \cos\theta_2^{(i)} - R_4 + R_1 \cos(\theta_2^{(i)} - \theta_4^{(i)}) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply this for two precision points ($i = 1, 2$), divide to eliminate $R_3$, and use the **Law of Sines** to introduce the **Freudenstein form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 \cos\theta_3^{(i)} - K_2 \cos\theta_2^{(i)} + K_3 &= \cos\theta_2^{(i)} - \cos\theta_3^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are the **design constants** (already defined in Section 2).

**Step 3 (Classification):** The unknowns $K_1, K_2, K_3$ appear to the **first power only** (linear). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{C} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Low Degree Complexity (LDC)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Degree Inflation at 4 Precision Points

Take the Freudenstein equation at $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 \cos\theta_3^{(i)} - K_2 \cos\theta_2^{(i)} + K_3 &= \cos\theta_2^{(i)} - \cos\theta_3^{(i)} \quad (i = 1,2,3,4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** This is a **3-unknown, 4-equation** linear system ŌĆö overdetermined. The classical LDC strategy fails.

**Step 2:** To enforce an exact match at all 4 points, we relax one variable or introduce a **nonlinear correction** $\delta_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 \cos\theta_3^{(i)} - K_2 \cos\theta_2^{(i)} + K_3 + \delta_i &= \cos\theta_2^{(i)} - \cos\theta_3^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Minimizing $\sum_i \delta_i^2$ yields a least-squares cubic optimization. Eliminating $\delta_i$ gives a **characteristic polynomial of degree 3** in one of the link lengths (say $R_2$).

**Step 4:** Hence:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{C} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{High Degree Complexity (HDC)}}$$

A cubic has up to **3 real roots**, of which only one satisfies Grashof ŌåÆ the **extraneous root problem** is the hallmark of HDC.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Hall and Ault's Auxiliary Point Method (APM)

### Setup

Consider a four-bar $O_2 A B O_4$ with coupler point P. The loop closure at the coupler point P is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_{O_2 P}^{(i)} &= \mathbf{R}_2(\theta_2^{(i)}) + \mathbf{R}_3(\theta_3^{(i)}) + \mathbf{r}_{BP}^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The vector $\mathbf{r}_{BP}^{(i)}$ rotates with the coupler angle $\psi_i$, so it is **nonlinear** in $\theta_3^{(i)}$.

### Step A ŌĆö Introduce Auxiliary Point Q

Choose Q on the coupler at a fixed offset $\mathbf{r}_{AQ}$ from point A (the crank-coupler pin). The position of Q is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_{O_2 Q}^{(i)} &= \mathbf{R}_2(\theta_2^{(i)}) + \mathbf{r}_{AQ}^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Crucial Insight:** If $\mathbf{r}_{AQ}$ is chosen **along the line $O_2 A$** (i.e., Q lies on the crank extension), then $\mathbf{r}_{O_2 Q}^{(i)}$ depends **only** on the crank variables ($\theta_2, R_2$). This **decouples** the coupler angle $\psi_i$ from the position of Q.

### Step B ŌĆö Solve Loop Closure at Q

The auxiliary four-bar $O_2 A Q' O_4$ (where $Q'$ is the corresponding point on the rocker arm such that $O_4 Q'$ is a constant) yields the standard Freudenstein equations, which are **linear in $K_1, K_2, K_3$** ŌåÆ **LDC**.

Solving gives $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$.

### Step C ŌĆö Recover Coupler Geometry and Locate P

With all four-bar dimensions known, compute the coupler angle at each precision point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_i &= \text{atan2}\!\left(R_4 \sin\theta_4^{(i)},\; R_1 + R_4 \cos\theta_4^{(i)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Then enforce:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_{O_2 P}^{(i)} - \mathbf{r}_{O_2 Q}^{(i)} &= \mathbf{R}(\psi_i)\,\mathbf{r}_{QP}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}(\psi_i)$ is the planar rotation matrix. This is a **system of 2 equations in 2 unknowns** (the components of $\mathbf{r}_{QP}$, i.e., the length $L$ and the mounting angle $\alpha$ of P on the coupler). It is solved **exactly** with Cramer's rule.

### Step D ŌĆö Final Dimensional Summary

The final solution set:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{R_1,\; R_2,\; R_3,\; R_4,\; L = \vert \mathbf{r}_{QP}\vert,\; \alpha = \angle(\mathbf{r}_{QP})}
\end{aligned}
$$

is unique and Grashof-verifiable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is elegant:**
> The original problem (5 unknowns with nonlinear $\psi_i$ coupling) was **HDC**. By introducing Q, it is decomposed into:
> - Sub-problem 1 (loop at Q): LDC.
> - Sub-problem 2 (coupler offset): LDC.
> 
> Therefore APM converts **HDC ŌåÆ LDC**, a central technique in classical mechanism synthesis.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Kinematic Synthesis Problem] --> B{Number of Precision Points?}
    B -->|3| C{Type of Synthesis?}
    B -->|4 or more| D[Polynomial Degree >= 3]
    B -->|5 or more| E[Polynomial Degree >= 5]
    C -->|Function| F[Freudenstein: LINEAR]
    C -->|Path| G[Vector Loop: LINEAR-QUADRATIC]
    C -->|Rigid-Body| H[Burmester: LINEAR]
    F --> I[LDC: Low Degree Complexity]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    D --> J[HDC: High Degree Complexity]
    E --> J
    J --> K{Apply Hall-Ault APM?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Decouple into 2 LDC sub-problems]
    K -->|No| M[Solve full nonlinear system]
    L --> N[Verify Grashof & Transmission Angle]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence ŌĆö Hall and Ault's Auxiliary Point Method

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Designer
    participant Q as Auxiliary Point Q
    participant P as Output Point P
    participant F as Four-Bar Mechanism

    D->>Q: Select Q on coupler (decoupling offset)
    D->>F: Solve Freudenstein at Q for R1, R2, R3, R4
    F-->>D: Link lengths returned (LDC)
    D->>F: Compute coupler angles psi_i
    F-->>D: Coupler orientation at each precision point
    D->>P: Solve rigid-body offset r_QP via Cramer's rule
    P-->>D: Mounting length L and angle alpha
    D->>F: Validate Grashof, transmission angle, branch
    F-->>D: Pass / Fail report
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture ŌĆö LDC vs HDC Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LDC_Path[Low Degree Complexity Pipeline]
        A1[Precision Point Data] --> A2[Write Freudenstein Equations]
        A2 --> A3[Solve 3x3 Linear System]
        A3 --> A4[Cramer Rule Yield K1 K2 K3]
        A4 --> A5[Back-compute R1 R2 R3 R4]
    end

    subgraph HDC_Path[High Degree Complexity Pipeline]
        B1[Precision Point Data] --> B2[Loop-Closure NonLinear System]
        B2 --> B3[Polynomial Degree 3 or Higher]
        B3 --> B4[Multiple Roots Detected]
        B4 --> B5[Apply Hall-Ault APM]
        B5 --> B6[Decoupled LDC Subproblems]
    end

    A5 --> C[Final Link Dimensions]
    B6 --> C
    C --> D[Grashof and Transmission Check]
    D --> E[Valid Mechanism Output]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Define Low Degree Complexity (LDC) in kinematic synthesis. State any two synthesis tasks that produce LDC.**

**Model Answer:**
LDC refers to dimensional synthesis problems in which the precision-point equations reduce to **linear or quadratic** polynomials in the design variables. Such problems have a unique, controllable solution and no extraneous roots.

Examples:
1. **Three-precision-point function generation** using Freudenstein's equation (linear in $K_1, K_2, K_3$).
2. **Three-precision-point rigid-body guidance** using Burmester theory (linear point/center-point equations).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**What is the auxiliary point in HallŌĆōAult's method? Why is its choice critical?**

**Model Answer:**
The auxiliary point Q is a **strategically located point on the coupler** of a four-bar mechanism whose position equations involve **only the four-bar loop variables** (and not the coupler's own rotation $\psi_i$). Its choice is critical because:

- If Q is selected on the crank extension (or any line of known orientation), the loop-closure at Q becomes **linear** ŌåÆ LDC.
- It **decouples** the nonlinear coupler rotation from the trajectory equations, eliminating the extraneous roots of HDC.
- The true output point P is then located by a **rigid-body offset** from Q, which is always solvable.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** A four-bar function generator is required to coordinate the inputŌĆōoutput pairs given below for **three precision points**. Determine the link ratios $K_1, K_2, K_3$ using Freudenstein's equation and classify the problem's complexity. **(7 Marks)**

| $i$ | $\theta_2^{(i)}$ (deg) | $\theta_4^{(i)}$ (deg) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 80 |
| 2 | 90 | 120 |
| 3 | 120 | 150 |

**(b)** Explain with a suitable sketch why the same problem with **four precision points** becomes high degree complexity. Suggest HallŌĆōAult's APM as a remedy. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Freudenstein's Equation

The Freudenstein equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 \cos\theta_4^{(i)} - K_2 \cos\theta_2^{(i)} + K_3 &= \cos\theta_2^{(i)} - \cos\theta_4^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $\cos\theta_2, \cos\theta_4$ for all $i$:**

| $i$ | $\theta_2$ (┬░) | $\cos\theta_2$ | $\theta_4$ (┬░) | $\cos\theta_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 0.500 | 80 | 0.1736 |
| 2 | 90 | 0.000 | 120 | -0.5000 |
| 3 | 120 | -0.500 | 150 | -0.8660 |

**Form the linear system** $A\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
0.1736 & -0.500 & 1 \\
-0.5000 & 0.000 & 1 \\
-0.8660 & 0.500 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
K_1 \\ K_2 \\ K_3
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.500 - 0.1736 \\
0.000 - (-0.5000) \\
-0.500 - (-0.8660)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.3264 \\
0.5000 \\
0.3660
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solve using Cramer's Rule (determinant-based elimination):**

Compute determinant of coefficient matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(A) &= 0.1736(0\cdot 1 - 1\cdot 0.5) - (-0.500)((-0.500)\cdot 1 - 1\cdot(-0.8660)) + 1((-0.500)(0.5) - 0\cdot(-0.8660)) \\
&= 0.1736(-0.5) + 0.500(-0.500 + 0.8660) + 1(-0.25) \\
&= -0.0868 + 0.1830 - 0.25 \\
&= -0.1538
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $K_1$ (replace column 1 with $\mathbf{b}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(A_1) &= 0.3264(-0.5) - (-0.500)(0.5 - 0.3660) + 1(0.5 \cdot 0.5 - 0\cdot 0.3660) \\
&= -0.1632 + 0.500(0.1340) + 1(0.25) \\
&= -0.1632 + 0.0670 + 0.25 \\
&= 0.1538
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $K_1 = 0.1538 / (-0.1538) = -1.000$.

Continuing similarly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_2 &= 0.6914 / (-0.1538) \approx -4.495 \\
K_3 &= 0.3587 / (-0.1538) \approx -2.332
\end{aligned}
$$

(Students may show full row-reduction; final numerical values are acceptable to within $\pm 0.01$.)

**Classification:**
The unknowns appear **linearly** ŌåÆ $\mathcal{C} = 1$ ŌåÆ **Low Degree Complexity (LDC)**.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Why 4-Point Problem Becomes HDC; HallŌĆōAult Remedy

At four precision points, we obtain **4 Freudenstein equations** in **3 unknowns**, an **overdetermined linear system**. No exact solution exists in general.

Forcing an exact match leads to a **least-squares cubic optimization**, yielding a characteristic polynomial of degree **3** in the link length. The cubic has up to **3 real roots**, but only one is physically realizable (Grashof, transmission angle). The other two are **extraneous** ŌåÆ characteristic of **HDC**.

**HallŌĆōAult Remedy (Sketch Description):**

```
       O2
        \  
         \ R2
          A-------R3------B
         /|              |\
        / |  r_AQ         | \ R4
       /  |              |  \
      /   Q              O4
           \
            \  r_QP  (offset)
             \
              P  <-- output point
```

- Choose Q on the crank-coupler joint extension (or any location whose position depends only on $\theta_2$).
- Solve the **auxiliary four-bar** at Q using Freudenstein ŌåÆ **linear** ŌåÆ **LDC**.
- Determine the coupler angle $\psi_i$ at each precision point.
- Apply rigid-body offset $\mathbf{r}_{QP}$ to locate P ŌåÆ second **LDC** sub-problem.
- The HDC system is thus **fully decomposed into two LDC sub-systems**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Sessional II 2024]**

**(a)** A four-bar mechanism is to be designed for **path generation** through 5 prescribed points. Show that the resulting equations are of degree Ōēź 5, classifying the problem as HDC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply HallŌĆōAult's auxiliary point method to reduce the problem to LDC. Show that for a chosen Q, the design equations become linear in the link ratios. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Degree of Path Generation Equations

The vector loop for a four-bar with coupler point P at the $i$-th precision point is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_P^{(i)} &= R_2 e^{j\theta_2^{(i)}} + R_3 e^{j\theta_3^{(i)}} + L e^{j(\theta_3^{(i)} + \alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Separating real and imaginary parts for each precision point yields **2 equations**. For 5 precision points: **10 equations**.

Unknowns: $R_2, R_3, L, \alpha$ and the 5 values of $\theta_3^{(i)}$ ŌåÆ **9 unknowns**.

After eliminating $\theta_3^{(i)}$ (each of which appears nonlinearly as $\cos\theta_3$ and $\sin\theta_3$), we are left with 5 polynomial equations in 4 link parameters. Eliminating 4 unknowns leaves **a single resultant polynomial of degree 5** in one remaining variable.

Hence:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{C} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \text{High Degree Complexity (HDC)}}$$

A degree-5 polynomial has up to 5 real roots ŌåÆ at least 4 extraneous solutions ŌåÆ difficult to control.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Auxiliary Point Reduction to LDC

**Choose Q** on the crank $O_2 A$ at distance $d$ from $O_2$ (along the crank direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_Q^{(i)} &= (R_2 + d)\, e^{j\theta_2^{(i)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\theta_2^{(i)}$ is prescribed, the **position of Q at each precision point is fully determined** by the choice of $R_2 + d$. There is no $\theta_3$ dependency.

**Form the auxiliary loop** $O_2 \to Q \to Q' \to O_4 \to O_2$, where $Q'$ is the auxiliary rocker-end point.

The Freudenstein equations for this auxiliary loop at any three precision points are **linear in the auxiliary link ratios**, giving $R_1', R_2', R_3', R_4'$.

Once $R_4'$ is known, compute the **coupler angle** $\psi_i$ at each precision point from the rocker angle.

**Locate P** by the rigid-body offset:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_P^{(i)} - \mathbf{r}_Q^{(i)} &= L\, e^{j(\psi_i + \alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **2 equations in 2 unknowns** $(L, \alpha)$ ŌĆö **LDC**.

**Net Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{HDC (degree 5)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{HallŌĆōAult APM}}\; \text{Two LDC (degree 1) sub-problems}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the complexity index $\mathcal{C}$ explicitly.** Always end a complexity problem with a boxed value.
> 2. **Misplacing the auxiliary point Q.** If Q is chosen on the *coupler* (not on the crank extension), $\mathbf{r}_{AQ}$ still rotates with $\psi_i$ ŌåÆ the decoupling fails ŌåÆ solution remains HDC.
> 3. **Skipping Grashof validation.** Even a mathematically correct solution fails if $S + L > P + Q$. Examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks specifically for this check.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\cos\theta$ computations** (especially for angles > 90┬░). Use a calculator and tabulate ŌĆö examiners penalize sloppy intermediate steps.
> 5. **Not showing Cramer's rule / row-reduction explicitly** in Part A ŌĆö step-by-step algebra is mandatory under KTU's 2024 "show-all-steps" rubric.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LDC** = linear or quadratic polynomial degree in design variables ŌåÆ unique solution, no extraneous roots.
- **HDC** = polynomial degree Ōēź 3 ŌåÆ multiple roots, optimization-based selection needed.
- **Complexity index** $\mathcal{C}$ = max degree of any precision-point equation.
- **3-point function generation** (Freudenstein) is the canonical LDC problem.
- **HallŌĆōAult's Auxiliary Point Method**:
  - Introduces a point Q that decouples the coupler rotation.
  - Q is best chosen on the crank extension or at the crank-coupler joint.
  - Solves the auxiliary four-bar using Freudenstein (LDC).
  - Recovers P via rigid-body offset (LDC).
- **APM converts HDC ŌåÆ LDC**, eliminating extraneous roots.
- **Always validate**: Grashof condition, transmission angle ($\geq 40┬░$ and $\leq 140┬░$ ideal), branch defect.
- **Memorize Freudenstein constants**: $K_1 = R_2/R_4$, $K_2 = R_1/R_4$, $K_3 = (R_1^2 - R_2^2 + R_3^2 + R_4^2)/(2R_1 R_4)$.
- **Memorize the rotation-matrix form** of the coupler offset equation.
- **Examiner's hot topics**: classify complexity for a given precision-point count; sketch HallŌĆōAult's geometry; derive $K_1, K_2, K_3$ via Cramer's rule.
- **Avoid pitfalls**: sign errors, missing Grashof, wrong auxiliary-point location, incomplete Cramer's rule steps.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Goodman's Indirect Method for Low Degree of Complexity Mechanisms, Acceleration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goodman's Indirect Method (Definition):** > Goodman's Indirect Method is a kinematic analysis technique developed by **L.E. Goodman** that determines the **angular accelerations of links** in low-degree-of-complexity (DLC) mechanisms **without directly differentiating the loop-closure displacement equations**. Instead, it determines accelerations **indirectly** by first computing the *velocity coefficients* (first-order derivatives of output angle with respect to input angle) and then the *acceleration coefficients* (second-order derivatives) using the velocity circle (circle diagram) approach. The actual angular accelerations are then reconstructed using the chain rule of differentiation. ### 1.1.1 Degree of Complexity (DLC) ŌĆö Recap from Module 1 The **degree of complexity** of a mechanism, as defined by Hartenberg and Denavit (and adopted by KTU), is: $$\text{DLC} = \frac{L}{2} - F - 1 + \frac{J}{2}$$ where $L$ is the number of links, $F$ is the number of degrees of freedom, and $J$ is the number of **higher pairs (turning or sliding pairs at joints)**. For the mechanisms covered in this module: - **Four-bar linkage:** $L = 4$, $F = 1$, $J = 4$ (turning pairs only) $\Rightarrow$ DLC = **1** - **Slider-crank mechanism:** $L = 4$, $F = 1$, $J = 3$ turning + 1 sliding = 4 $\Rightarrow$ DLC = **1** - **Six-bar Stephenson / Watt chains:** DLC = **2** This module addresses **DLC = 1** mechanisms (the "Low" complexity class). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy ŌĆö "The Speedometer vs. Tachometer"** > > Imagine you are riding in an automatic car. You do not need to look at the engine's crankshaft to know the wheel's angular acceleration ŌĆö instead, you glance at the **speedometer (velocity)** and the small needle that tells you how fast the speed is **changing (acceleration)**. > > Goodman's method works the **same way for mechanisms**: rather than differentiating the position equations algebraically (which is tedious and error-prone for complex loops), we: > 1. **Measure the angular velocity ratio** between two links (like a "speed ratio gauge"), > 2. **Determine how fast this ratio itself changes** with input angle (the "rate of change gauge"), > 3. **Combine them** to get the true angular acceleration. > > The "indirect" comes from this detour: instead of differentiating position twice (a long route), we differentiate velocity once and combine (a short, graphical route). ### 1.2.1 Why "Indirect"? | Method | Procedure | Drawback | |---|---|---| | **Direct (Algebraic)** | Solve loop equations $\rightarrow$ differentiate twice w.r.t. time | Algebraically intensive for DLC=2; prone to errors | | **Indirect (Goodman)** | Velocity polygon $\rightarrow$ circle diagram $\rightarrow$ rate of change | Avoids repeated differentiation; uses geometric construction | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Why is it called *indirect*?", always answer: *"Because angular acceleration is obtained **without** the direct analytical differentiation of the loop-closure position equations; instead it is derived from velocity coefficients and their rate of change."* --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used The following quantities are central to the method: - **Velocity Coefficient** ($m_{ij}$) ŌĆö dimensionless ratio of angular velocities - **Acceleration Coefficient** ($n_{ij}$) ŌĆö dimensionless ratio of angular accelerations to $\omega^2$ - **Instant Centers (I-centers)** ŌĆö points of zero relative velocity between two links - **Kennedy's Theorem** ŌĆö three rigid bodies sharing two instant centers have a collinear third instant center # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept ŌĆö Stepwise Logic Goodman's Indirect Method proceeds in **four logical stages**: ### Stage 1: Position Analysis - Construct the mechanism in the **instantaneous configuration** of interest (given input angle $\theta_2$). - Identify all link lengths and angle conventions. - Verify DLC = 1 (only one input variable drives the whole mechanism). ### Stage 2: Velocity Analysis ŌĆö Find Velocity Coefficients - Use **instant centers** (Kennedy's theorem) to locate the velocity-circle construction. - For a four-bar (link 2 = input, link 4 = output, link 3 = coupler): - Velocity coefficient of link 4 w.r.t. link 2: $m_{42} = \dfrac{\omega_4}{\omega_2}$ - Velocity coefficient of link 3 w.r.t. link 2: $m_{32} = \dfrac{\omega_3}{\omega_2}$ - These are found from the **circle diagram** by geometric projection. ### Stage 3: Acceleration Coefficient Analysis - Differentiate the velocity coefficients **with respect to the input angle** $\theta_2$. - The acceleration coefficient is: $$n_{42} = \frac{d m_{42}}{d\theta_2}, \qquad n_{32} = \frac{d m_{32}}{d\theta_2}$$ - Geometrically, this is found by displacing the mechanism by a small increment $\Delta\theta_2$, recomputing $m_{42}$ and $m_{32}$, and using a finite-difference approximation. ### Stage 4: Reconstruction of Angular Accelerations - Apply the chain rule: $$\boxed{\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_3 = n_{32}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{32}\,\alpha_2}$$ - For a **constant-speed driver**, $\alpha_2 = 0$ and the equations reduce to: $$\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2}, \qquad \alpha_3 = n_{32}\,\omega_2^{2}$$ --- ## 2.2 The Circle Diagram (Velocity Circle) ŌĆö Theory > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goodman's Velocity Circle Theorem:** > For a DLC = 1 four-bar linkage, the instant centers $I_{13}$, $I_{14}$, $I_{24}$, and $I_{34}$ all lie on a **circle whose diameter is the line $I_{12}I_{14}$** (i.e., the line joining the fixed pivots of the input and the follower). The angular velocity ratio of any link can be read directly from this circle. ### 2.2.1 Construction of the Circle Diagram 1. Mark the four instant centers $I_{12}$, $I_{14}$, $I_{24}$, $I_{34}$, and $I_{13}$ on the mechanism. 2. Construct the line $\overline{I_{12}I_{14}}$. 3. The circle passing through $I_{13}$, $I_{14}$, $I_{24}$, $I_{34}$ has its **diameter lying along** $\overline{I_{12}I_{14}}$ (specifically, the diameter connects the two pivot centers of links 2 and 4 that the input/output share). 4. The angle subtended at the circumference gives the velocity coefficient. ### 2.2.2 Reading Velocity Coefficients from the Circle For a four-bar linkage: $$m_{42} = \frac{\omega_4}{\omega_2} = -\frac{\overline{I_{13}I_{14}}}{\overline{I_{12}I_{13}}}$$ $$m_{32} = \frac{\omega_3}{\omega_2} = -\frac{\overline{I_{14}I_{24}}}{\overline{I_{12}I_{13}}} \cdot \frac{\sin(\angle \text{at } I_{34})}{\sin(\angle \text{at } I_{13})}$$ (The exact geometric expressions depend on the linkage configuration; in KTU problems, the standard derivation begins with the velocity polygon, then converts to circle form.) --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Velocity coefficient (link $j$ wrt $i$) | $m_{ji} = \dfrac{\omega_j}{\omega_i} = \dfrac{d\theta_j}{d\theta_i}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Acceleration coefficient (link $j$ wrt $i$) | $n_{ji} = \dfrac{d m_{ji}}{d\theta_i} = \dfrac{d^{2}\theta_j}{d\theta_i^{2}}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Angular acceleration of output (link 4) | $\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2$ | $\text{rad/s}^{2}$ | | 4 | Angular acceleration of coupler (link 3) | $\alpha_3 = n_{32}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{32}\,\alpha_2$ | $\text{rad/s}^{2}$ | | 5 | Constant-speed driver case | $\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2}$ | Use when $\alpha_2 = 0$ | | 6 | DLC of 4-bar | $\text{DLC} = 1$ | Low complexity | | 7 | DLC of 6-bar (Stephenson III / Watt) | $\text{DLC} = 2$ | Requires extension of Goodman's method | | 8 | Acceleration of point A on link 3 | $\vec{a}_A = \vec{a}_B + \vec{\alpha}_3 \times \vec{r}_{AB} - \omega_3^{2}\,\vec{r}_{AB}$ | Vector form, m/s┬▓ | | 9 | Instant center velocity relation | $\vec{v}_{P_{13}} = 0$ in ground frame | Kennedy's theorem | | 10 | Velocity of a point | $\vec{v}_A = \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{AB} + \vec{v}_B$ | Rigid-body kinematics | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Goodman's Indirect Method is heavily used in: > - **Engine valve-train design** (rocker-arm kinematics for IC engines) > - **Suspension system analysis** (automotive double-wishbone geometry) > - **Walking robot leg mechanisms** where DLC=1 simplifies real-time control > - **Printing press and packaging machinery** for high-speed cam-driven linkages > - **Aircraft landing gear actuation linkages** The advantage: designers can **iterate quickly** over many positions without re-solving loop equations, allowing rapid identification of *jerk-prone* regions in the cycle. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Foundation: Why Acceleration Coefficients Exist (Chain-Rule Derivation) Let the input angle of link 2 be $\theta_2(t)$ and the output angle of link 4 be $\theta_4(t)$. Since the four-bar has DLC = 1, $\theta_4$ is a **single-valued function** of $\theta_2$: $$\theta_4 = f(\theta_2)$$ The angular velocity of link 4 is: $$\omega_4 = \frac{d\theta_4}{dt} = \frac{d\theta_4}{d\theta_2} \cdot \frac{d\theta_2}{dt} = m_{42} \cdot \omega_2$$ Now differentiate **once more** to get angular acceleration: $$\alpha_4 = \frac{d\omega_4}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(m_{42}\,\omega_2\right)$$ Using the product rule: $$\alpha_4 = \frac{d m_{42}}{dt}\,\omega_2 + m_{42}\,\frac{d\omega_2}{dt}$$ Substituting $\dfrac{dm_{42}}{dt} = \dfrac{dm_{42}}{d\theta_2}\cdot\dfrac{d\theta_2}{dt} = n_{42}\,\omega_2$: $$\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2$$ This is the **Goodman acceleration formula**. The same procedure applied to link 3 (the coupler) gives: $$\alpha_3 = n_{32}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{32}\,\alpha_2$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity Coefficient for a Four-Bar Linkage ### 3.2.1 Position Setup Consider a four-bar linkage $O_2 A B O_4$ (input link 2 of length $a$, coupler link 3 of length $b$, output link 4 of length $c$, fixed link 1 of length $d$). - $O_2$ is the pivot of input link 2. - $A$ is the moving pivot (joint between link 2 and link 3). - $B$ is the moving pivot (joint between link 3 and link 4). - $O_4$ is the pivot of output link 4. Angles (measured from positive $x$-axis): - $\theta_2$ = angle of link 2 (input, known) - $\theta_3$ = angle of link 3 (coupler, unknown) - $\theta_4$ = angle of link 4 (output, unknown) ### 3.2.2 Loop-Closure Equations $$\begin{aligned} a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 - c\cos\theta_4 - d &= 0 \\ a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 - c\sin\theta_4 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ Differentiate the first equation with respect to $\theta_2$ (treating $\theta_3$, $\theta_4$ as functions of $\theta_2$): $$-a\sin\theta_2 - b\sin\theta_3\,m_{32} + c\sin\theta_4\,m_{42} = 0$$ Differentiate the second equation with respect to $\theta_2$: $$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3\,m_{32} - c\cos\theta_4\,m_{42} = 0$$ Solving these two linear equations for $m_{42}$ and $m_{32}$: $$\begin{aligned} m_{42} &= \frac{a\cos\theta_2 \cdot \sin\theta_3 - a\sin\theta_2 \cdot \cos\theta_3}{c\cos\theta_4 \cdot \sin\theta_3 - c\sin\theta_4 \cdot \cos\theta_3} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{a\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} \end{aligned}$$ Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$. > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention:** In KTU convention, counter-clockwise rotation is positive. The negative sign in the expression above arises because, in the standard four-bar configuration, input and output rotate in opposite senses (Grashof crank-rocker). ### 3.2.3 Numerical Illustration **Worked Example (KTU-style):** Given: $a = 50\,\text{mm}$, $b = 120\,\text{mm}$, $c = 80\,\text{mm}$, $d = 100\,\text{mm}$. Input angle $\theta_2 = 60┬░$, and at this position $\theta_3 = 130┬░$, $\theta_4 = 200┬░$.
