Compute $m_{42}$: $$\begin{aligned} m_{42} &= -\frac{a\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{50 \cdot \sin(130┬░ - 60┬░)}{80 \cdot \sin(130┬░ - 200┬░)} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{50 \cdot \sin(70┬░)}{80 \cdot \sin(-70┬░)} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{50 \cdot 0.9397}{80 \cdot (-0.9397)} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{46.985}{-75.176} \\ m_{42} &= +0.6250 \end{aligned}$$ This means the output rotates in the **same direction** as the input with about 62.5% of the speed ŌĆö consistent with the geometry. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Acceleration Coefficient (Differentiate Again) Differentiate the two loop equations (now already differentiated once) a second time w.r.t. $\theta_2$: $$\begin{aligned} -a\cos\theta_2 - b\cos\theta_3\,m_{32}^{2} - b\sin\theta_3\,n_{32} + c\cos\theta_4\,m_{42}^{2} + c\sin\theta_4\,n_{42} &= 0 \\ -a\sin\theta_2 - b\sin\theta_3\,m_{32}^{2} + b\cos\theta_3\,n_{32} + c\sin\theta_4\,m_{42}^{2} - c\cos\theta_4\,n_{42} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving these (matrix form) for $n_{42}$ and $n_{32}$ yields the acceleration coefficients. ### 3.3.1 General Closed-Form Expression Using Cramer's rule: $$\begin{aligned} n_{42} &= \frac{a\,\cos\theta_2 - a\,\cos\theta_2\,(\ldots) + \ldots}{c\,(\ldots)} \end{aligned}$$ The classical Hartenberg-Denavit form is: $$n_{42} = \frac{a^{2} - b^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2} - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2bc\,m_{42}\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}{c^{2}} + m_{42}\,\cot(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$$ **Key insight:** $n_{42}$ depends on the link lengths, the input angle, and the *current* velocity coefficient. It is dimensionless. --- ## 3.4 Goodman's Method for the Acceleration Coefficient (Geometric Form) Rather than carrying out the algebraic differentiation, Goodman proposed the following **numerical-graphical procedure**: 1. Construct the mechanism at the **current position** $\theta_2$. 2. Find the instant centers $I_{13}$, $I_{24}$, $I_{14}$, $I_{34}$ using **Kennedy's theorem**. 3. Construct the **velocity circle** on diameter $\overline{I_{12}I_{14}}$. 4. Read the velocity coefficients $m_{42}$ and $m_{32}$ from the circle. 5. Move the input crank by a **small increment** $\Delta\theta_2$ (typically $1┬░$ to $5┬░$). 6. Repeat steps 2ŌĆō4 for the displaced position to find $m'_{42}$ and $m'_{32}$. 7. Compute the acceleration coefficient by finite difference: $$n_{42} \approx \frac{m'_{42} - m_{42}}{\Delta\theta_2}, \qquad n_{32} \approx \frac{m'_{32} - m_{32}}{\Delta\theta_2}$$ 8. Apply the chain-rule reconstruction: $$\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full Problem) **Problem (KTU pattern):** A four-bar mechanism has link lengths $a = 30\,\text{mm}$, $b = 100\,\text{mm}$, $c = 70\,\text{mm}$, $d = 90\,\text{mm}$. At the instant when $\theta_2 = 45┬░$, the velocity coefficient $m_{42} = -0.520$ and the acceleration coefficient $n_{42} = -0.183$. The input crank rotates at $\omega_2 = 20\,\text{rad/s}$ (constant). Find $\alpha_4$. **Solution:** Since the input is at constant speed, $\alpha_2 = 0$. $$\begin{aligned} \alpha_4 &= n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2 \\ \alpha_4 &= (-0.183)(20)^{2} + (-0.520)(0) \\ \alpha_4 &= (-0.183)(400) \\ \alpha_4 &= -73.2\,\text{rad/s}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ The negative sign indicates **clockwise angular acceleration** (in the convention where CCW is positive). The magnitude is **73.2 rad/s┬▓**. --- ## 3.6 Extension: Slider-Crank Mechanism (DLC = 1) For a slider-crank, the position of the slider $x$ is a function of the crank angle $\theta$: $$x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}$$ - Velocity coefficient: $m_x = dx/d\theta$ - Acceleration coefficient: $n_x = d^{2}x/d\theta^{2}$ - Slider acceleration: $\ddot{x} = n_x\,\dot{\theta}^{2} + m_x\,\ddot{\theta}$ > [!TIP] > The slider-crank acceleration coefficients are tabulated in standard textbooks (Hartenberg & Denavit) and are commonly asked in KTU Part-B problems. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Summary of All Key Equations | Quantity | Symbolic Form | Geometric (Goodman) Form | |---|---|---| | Velocity coeff. (4-bar) | $m_{42} = -\dfrac{a\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$ | From circle diagram chord ratio | | Velocity coeff. (3-bar, coupler) | $m_{32} = -\dfrac{a\sin(\theta_4 - \theta_2)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$ | From circle diagram chord ratio | | Acceleration coeff. | $n_{42} = \dfrac{dm_{42}}{d\theta_2}$ | Finite difference via displaced position | | Angular accel. (output) | $\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2$ | Chain-rule reconstruction | | Angular accel. (coupler) | $\alpha_3 = n_{32}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{32}\,\alpha_2$ | Chain-rule reconstruction | | Slider acceleration | $\ddot{x} = n_x\,\dot{\theta}^{2} + m_x\,\ddot{\theta}$ | Slider-crank case | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Goodman's Method The following flow diagram illustrates the procedural architecture of Goodman's Indirect Method from inputs to outputs. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Data: Link lengths a, b, c, d
Input angle theta2
Input omega2 and alpha2] --> B[Stage 1: Position Analysis
Solve loop equations for theta3, theta4] B --> C[Stage 2: Locate Instant Centers
Kennedys Theorem to find I13, I24, I34] C --> D[Stage 3: Construct Velocity Circle
Diameter = line I12-I14] D --> E[Stage 4: Read Velocity Coefficients
m42 and m32 from circle chords] E --> F[Stage 5: Displace Mechanism
Apply small delta-theta2] F --> G[Stage 6: Recompute m42 and m32
at displaced position] G --> H[Stage 7: Finite Difference
n42 = delta-m42 / delta-theta2] H --> I[Stage 8: Chain Rule Reconstruction
alpha4 = n42 times omega2 squared plus m42 times alpha2] H --> J[Stage 8b: Coupler Acceleration
alpha3 = n32 times omega2 squared plus m32 times alpha2] I --> K[Final Output: alpha4 in rad per s squared] J --> L[Final Output: alpha3 in rad per s squared] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 Instant-Center Topology for a Four-Bar (DLC = 1) This diagram shows the four-bar mechanism with all instant centers labeled (per Kennedy/Aronhold notation $I_{ij}$). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FOURBAR[Four-Bar Linkage Topology] O2[O2 = I12
Fixed pivot of input] A[A = I23
Moving pivot input-coupler] B[B = I34
Moving pivot coupler-output] O4[O4 = I14
Fixed pivot of output] I24[I24
Input-Output IC] I13[I13
Coupler-Ground IC] O2 --- A A --- B B --- O4 O2 --- O4 A -.->|velocity zero| I13 B -.->|velocity zero| I24 end style O2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style O4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style I24 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style I13 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Velocity Coefficients as a "Ratio Gauge" ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT[Input Crank Link 2
theta2, omega2, alpha2] -->|drives| MECH[Four-Bar Mechanism
DLC = 1] MECH -->|coupler kin| COUPLER[Coupler Link 3
m32 governs omega3] MECH -->|output kin| OUTPUT[Output Link 4
m42 governs omega4] MECH -.->|velocity coeff| VC[Velocity Coefficients
m42 and m32] VC -.->|displace and differentiate| AC[Acceleration Coefficients
n42 and n32] AC -->|reconstruct alpha| OUTACC[Angular Accelerations
alpha4 and alpha3] style INPUT fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style VC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style AC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px style OUTACC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparison Block: Direct vs. Indirect (Goodman) Method ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DIRECT[Direct Algebraic Method] D1[Loop Closure Equations] --> D2[Differentiate Once w.r.t. t] D2 --> D3[Solve Linear System for omega] D3 --> D4[Differentiate Again w.r.t. t] D4 --> D5[Solve Linear System for alpha] end subgraph INDIRECT[Goodman Indirect Method] I1[Loop Closure Equations] --> I2[Compute theta3, theta4 numerically] I2 --> I3[Velocity Circle Diagram or Velocity Polygon] I3 --> I4[Read Velocity Coefficients m42, m32] I4 --> I5[Displace by delta-theta2 and recompute] I5 --> I6[Finite-Difference Acceleration Coefficients n42, n32] I6 --> I7[Chain Rule Reconstruction alpha4 and alpha3] end style D1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style I1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style I7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "velocity coefficient" as used in Goodman's method for acceleration analysis of mechanisms.** **Model Answer:** > The velocity coefficient $m_{ji}$ of link $j$ with respect to link $i$ is defined as the ratio of the angular velocity of link $j$ to that of link $i$, expressed as the derivative of the output angle with respect to the input angle: > $$m_{ji} = \frac{\omega_j}{\omega_i} = \frac{d\theta_j}{d\theta_i}$$ > It is a **dimensionless quantity** that captures the instantaneous speed ratio between two links in a DLC = 1 mechanism. Geometrically, in Goodman's method, it is obtained from chord ratios on the **velocity circle diagram** without requiring explicit differentiation of the loop-closure equations. (3 marks) --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain the significance of Kennedy's theorem in Goodman's indirect method.** **Model Answer:** > Kennedy's theorem states that **three rigid bodies sharing two instant centers have their third instant center collinear with the other two on a single line**. In the context of Goodman's method for a DLC = 1 four-bar linkage, Kennedy's theorem allows systematic construction of all six instant centers ($I_{12}, I_{13}, I_{14}, I_{23}, I_{24}, I_{34}$) from the four primary ones. Once located, these instant centers enable construction of the **velocity circle (circle diagram)** on which the velocity coefficients $m_{42}$ and $m_{32}$ are read directly as chord ratios. Without Kennedy's theorem, Goodman's indirect graphical procedure would not be feasible. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > - **Sign convention errors:** Many students drop the negative sign in $m_{42}$ for a standard crank-rocker, leading to wrong direction of $\alpha_4$. Always verify the rotation sense by sketching the velocity polygon. > - **Forgetting the $\omega_2^{2}$ term:** A common mistake is writing $\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2$ instead of $n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2}$. This loses 2 marks. > - **Mis-identifying DLC = 1 vs DLC = 2:** Goodman's indirect method (single velocity coefficient) only applies to DLC = 1. For DLC = 2 (six-bar), the method must be extended with multiple input angles ŌĆö students often erroneously apply the four-bar formula to a six-bar. > - **Confusing $m$ and $n$:** Velocity coefficient $m$ has units of "ratio"; acceleration coefficient $n$ has units of "per radian." Marking is strict on this distinction. --- ### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **In a four-bar mechanism $O_2 A B O_4$, the link lengths are $a = 40\,\text{mm}$, $b = 150\,\text{mm}$, $c = 90\,\text{mm}$, $d = 120\,\text{mm}$.** **(a)** *At the instant when the input crank angle $\theta_2 = 75┬░$, the coupler angle is $\theta_3 = 145┬░$ and the output rocker angle is $\theta_4 = 215┬░$. Compute the velocity coefficient $m_{42}$ using the analytical formula.* **[7 Marks]** **(b)** *If the velocity coefficient at the same instant is $m_{42} = -0.642$ and the acceleration coefficient obtained from a displaced-position analysis is $n_{42} = -0.215$, determine the angular acceleration $\alpha_4$ of the output link when the input crank rotates at $\omega_2 = 25\,\text{rad/s}$ with constant angular acceleration $\alpha_2 = 5\,\text{rad/s}^{2}$.* **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution: **Part (a):** Using the standard four-bar velocity coefficient formula: $$m_{42} = -\frac{a\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$$ Compute the angle differences: - $\theta_3 - \theta_2 = 145┬░ - 75┬░ = 70┬░$ - $\theta_3 - \theta_4 = 145┬░ - 215┬░ = -70┬░$ Substitute: $$\begin{aligned} m_{42} &= -\frac{40 \cdot \sin(70┬░)}{90 \cdot \sin(-70┬░)} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{40 \cdot 0.9397}{90 \cdot (-0.9397)} \\ m_{42} &= -\frac{37.588}{-84.573} \\ m_{42} &= +0.4445 \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $m_{42} \approx +0.444$ (output rotates in the same direction as the input at this configuration). **[7 Marks]** **Part (b):** Apply the chain-rule reconstruction (Goodman's acceleration formula): $$\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2$$ Substitute the given values: $$\begin{aligned} \alpha_4 &= (-0.215)(25)^{2} + (-0.642)(5) \\ \alpha_4 &= (-0.215)(625) + (-3.210) \\ \alpha_4 &= -134.375 + (-3.210) \\ \alpha_4 &= -137.585\,\text{rad/s}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $\alpha_4 \approx -137.6\,\text{rad/s}^{2}$ (negative sign indicates clockwise angular acceleration in the CCW-positive convention). **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` **A slider-crank mechanism has crank length $r = 50\,\text{mm}$, connecting rod length $L = 200\,\text{mm}$, and the slider moves along a horizontal axis through the crank pivot. The crank rotates at constant $\omega = 30\,\text{rad/s}$.** **(a)** *Derive the expressions for the velocity coefficient $m_x = dx/d\theta$ and acceleration coefficient $n_x = d^{2}x/d\theta^{2}$ of the slider position $x$.* **[7 Marks]** **(b)** *At the crank angle $\theta = 60┬░$, evaluate $m_x$ and $n_x$, and hence compute the slider acceleration $\ddot{x}$ at this instant.* **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution: **Part (a):** Slider position from crank pivot: $$x = r\cos\theta + L\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}$$ For $r/L \ll 1$, expand the square root using Taylor series: $$\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta} \approx 1 - \frac{k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2} - \frac{k^{4}\sin^{4}\theta}{8} - \ldots$$ where $k = r/L$. Differentiating once: $$\begin{aligned} m_x = \frac{dx}{d\theta} &= -r\sin\theta + L \cdot \frac{-2k^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta}{2\sqrt{1-k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \\ m_x &= -r\sin\theta - \frac{r^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{L^{2}-r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \end{aligned}$$ Differentiating again: $$n_x = \frac{dm_x}{d\theta} = -r\cos\theta - \frac{d}{d\theta}\left[\frac{r^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{L^{2}-r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}}\right]$$ Carrying out the differentiation (product and chain rules): $$\begin{aligned} n_x &= -r\cos\theta - \frac{r^{2}(\cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta)}{\sqrt{L^{2}-r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \\ &\quad - \frac{r^{4}\sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta}{(L^{2}-r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta)^{3/2}} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** The derived expressions for $m_x$ and $n_x$ are above. **[7 Marks]** **Part (b):** With $r = 50\,\text{mm}$, $L = 200\,\text{mm}$, $\theta = 60┬░$: Compute auxiliary quantities: - $\sin 60┬░ = 0.8660$, $\cos 60┬░ = 0.5000$ - $\sin^{2} 60┬░ = 0.7500$ - $L^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta = 40000 - 2500 \cdot 0.75 = 40000 - 1875 = 38125\,\text{mm}^{2}$ - $\sqrt{38125} = 195.26\,\text{mm}$ - $r^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta = 2500 \cdot 0.8660 \cdot 0.5000 = 1082.5\,\text{mm}^{2}$ Compute $m_x$: $$\begin{aligned} m_x &= -50 \cdot 0.8660 - \frac{1082.5}{195.26} \\ m_x &= -43.30 - 5.544 \\ m_x &= -48.84\,\text{mm/rad} \end{aligned}$$ Compute $n_x$: $$\begin{aligned} n_x &= -50 \cdot 0.5000 - \frac{2500 \cdot (0.25 - 0.75)}{195.26} - \frac{(50)^{4} \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.25}{(38125)^{1.5}} \\ n_x &= -25.0 - \frac{2500 \cdot (-0.50)}{195.26} - \frac{6.25 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.1875}{7.43 \times 10^{6}} \\ n_x &= -25.0 + 6.403 - 0.158 \\ n_x &= -18.755\,\text{mm/rad}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ Since $\alpha = 0$ (constant-speed crank), slider acceleration: $$\ddot{x} = n_x \cdot \omega^{2} + m_x \cdot \alpha = (-18.755)(30)^{2} + (-48.84)(0)$$ $$\ddot{x} = -18.755 \cdot 900 = -16879.5\,\text{mm/s}^{2} = -16.88\,\text{m/s}^{2}$$ **Answer:** Slider acceleration is $\ddot{x} \approx -16.88\,\text{m/s}^{2}$ (decelerating in the +$x$ direction at this instant). **[7 Marks]** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Goodman's Indirect Method** ### Core Concepts - ŌśÉ Goodman's method finds acceleration **indirectly** through velocity coefficients and their rates of change. - ŌśÉ It applies to **DLC = 1** mechanisms (four-bar, slider-crank). - ŌśÉ The two key quantities are the **velocity coefficient** $m_{ji}$ and the **acceleration coefficient** $n_{ji}$. - ŌśÉ Both are dimensionless: $m_{ji} = d\theta_j/d\theta_i$ and $n_{ji} = d^{2}\theta_j/d\theta_i^{2}$. ### Key Formulas (Must Memorize) - ŌśÉ $\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2} + m_{42}\,\alpha_2$ ŌĆö central chain-rule reconstruction. - ŌśÉ For constant-speed input: $\alpha_4 = n_{42}\,\omega_2^{2}$. - ŌśÉ Velocity coefficient for four-bar: $m_{42} = -\dfrac{a\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$. - ŌśÉ Acceleration coefficients are obtained by **displacing the mechanism** by $\Delta\theta_2$ and applying finite differences. ### Geometric Construction (Goodman Specific) - ŌśÉ The **velocity circle** has its diameter on the line joining the two fixed pivots ($O_2$ and $O_4$). - ŌśÉ Velocity coefficients are read as **chord ratios** on this circle. - ŌśÉ **Kennedy's theorem** is the foundation for instant-center construction. ### Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!) - ŌśÉ Forgetting the **square** on $\omega_2$: must be $\omega_2^{2}$, not $\omega_2$. - ŌśÉ Mixing up **velocity and acceleration coefficients** ($m$ vs. $n$). - ŌśÉ Wrong **sign convention** for crank-rocker mechanisms (CCW positive). - ŌśÉ Applying four-bar formulas to **DLC = 2 six-bar** mechanisms. - ŌśÉ Missing the $m_{42}\,\alpha_2$ term when input is accelerating. ### Engineering Applications - ŌśÉ Used in **engine valve trains, suspension linkages, cam-driven machinery, walking robots.** - ŌśÉ Preferred over direct differentiation for **rapid graphical iteration** during design. ### Examiner's Last Word - ŌśÉ Always **draw the velocity polygon or circle diagram** before computing ŌĆö visual grounding catches sign errors. - ŌśÉ State units explicitly: $\alpha$ in rad/s┬▓, $m$ dimensionless, $n$ in radŌü╗┬╣. - ŌśÉ End your answer with a **sanity check**: a Grashof crank-rocker should have output reversing direction at dead centers, so $m_{42}$ should change sign there.

topics.title: Analytical Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analytical Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Analytical Methods in Mechanism Design? **Analytical Methods** in Advanced Mechanism Design refer to the **closed-form, equation-based mathematical procedures** used to determine the **position, velocity, acceleration, and force characteristics** of planar and spatial mechanisms without relying on graphical construction or numerical iteration. These methods translate the geometry of linkages into a set of **non-linear algebraic, transcendental, and differential equations** which are then solved analytically to obtain exact kinematic relationships. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, the term "Analytical Methods" specifically encompasses: 1. **Loop Closure Equations (Vector Loop Method)** ŌĆō expressing loop geometry as vector sums 2. **Freudenstein's Equation** ŌĆō direct inputŌĆōoutput position synthesis 3. **Velocity & Acceleration Analysis via Jacobian** ŌĆō differential form of loop equations 4. **EulerŌĆōSavary Equation** ŌĆō curvature analysis for path and motion generation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 demands mastery of *deriving* loop closure equations, *applying* Freudenstein's equation to a four-bar linkage, and *computing* the velocity/acceleration vector through **analytical (Jacobian) differentiation** ŌĆö NOT graphical methods. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Compass & Map" Concept Imagine you are a hiker lost in a forest with only a **compass and a topographic map** (no GPS, no satellite). To find your position, you cannot just *draw* your trail ŌĆö you must set up **trigonometric equations** relating your bearings, distances, and known landmarks, then solve them exactly. Similarly, in analytical methods: - The **links of the mechanism** are the "landmarks" with known lengths. - The **joint angles** are the unknown bearings. - The **loop closure equation** is the closed chain of bearings that must "return to zero." - Solving it **algebraically** gives the exact configuration ŌĆö analogous to triangulating your position on the map. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and notation conventions are used throughout Module 2: - **Number of links in a planar mechanism:** $n$ - **Lower pairs** (1 DOF): revolute ($R$), prismatic ($P$) - **Higher pairs** (2 DOF): camŌĆōfollower, gear mesh - **Gruebler's mobility criterion:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ where $j_1$ is the number of full joints and $j_2$ half joints - **Standard angle convention:** Counter-clockwise (CCW) measured from positive x-axis is **positive**; all angles expressed in **radians** for differentiation > [!NOTE] > **Why "Analytical" over "Graphical"?** Graphical methods (velocity polygons, instant centers) are quick but limited to one configuration at a time. Analytical methods yield **closed-form relationships** ŌĆö the same equation works for *every* configuration, making them indispensable for **computer-aided design (CAD), simulation, and optimization**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Vector Loop Closure Method ŌĆö The Heart of Analytical Kinematics ### 2.1.1 Conceptual Foundation Every closed kinematic chain (four-bar, slider-crank, inverted slider-crank, etc.) can be represented as a **vector polygon**. As the mechanism moves, the lengths of the links remain constant, but the orientations change. The loop closure equation enforces the geometric reality that **starting at one point and traversing all links must return to the same point** ŌĆö the vector sum of all link vectors must equal **zero**. ### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Steps 1. **Identify the closed loop(s)** in the mechanism. For a simple four-bar, there is exactly **one independent loop**. 2. **Assign a reference frame** (typically the fixed link) and an **input angle** $\theta_2$ which varies with time. 3. **Express each link vector** in complex exponential or $(x, y)$ component form: $$ \vec{r}_i = r_i e^{j\theta_i} = r_i(\cos\theta_i + j\sin\theta_i) $$ 4. **Write the loop equation** ensuring the sum of vectors around the loop equals zero. 5. **Separate into real and imaginary components** to obtain two scalar equations. 6. **Differentiate with respect to time** to get velocity equations; differentiate again for acceleration. 7. **Solve the resulting linear system** for unknown joint rates and accelerations. ### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility - **Automotive suspension analysis** ŌĆö closed-form equations allow rapid optimization of bushing angles. - **Robotic manipulators** ŌĆö Jacobian matrices for serial chains are direct extensions of loop closure. - **Compliant mechanisms** ŌĆö analytical models predict deflection in MEMS devices. - **Earth-moving equipment** ŌĆö loader linkages designed via Freudenstein's equation for predictable bucket trajectories. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Module 2 Analytical Methods | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables / Notes** | |:-----:|:------------|:------------|:----------------------| | 1 | Gruebler Mobility (planar) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links; $j_1$ = full joints; $j_2$ = half joints | | 2 | Kutzbach Criterion (spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $j_k$ = joint with $k$ DOF | | 3 | Complex Form Loop Equation | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} r_i e^{j\theta_i} = 0$ | Sum of link vectors in a closed loop | | 4 | Real-Imaginary Split | $\sum r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \; ; \quad \sum r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Two scalar equations per loop | | 5 | Velocity Equation | $\sum r_i \dot{\theta}_i (-\sin\theta_i) = 0$ | Differentiate real part w.r.t. time | | 6 | Acceleration Equation | $\sum r_i \ddot{\theta}_i (-\sin\theta_i) - r_i \dot{\theta}_i^2 (\cos\theta_i) = 0$ | Differentiate velocity equation | | 7 | Freudenstein's Equation | $K_1 \cos\theta_3 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$ | For four-bar; constants given below | | 8 | Freudenstein's Constants | $K_1 = d/a \; ; \quad K_2 = d/c \; ; \quad K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$ | $a$ = input crank, $b$ = coupler, $c$ = output, $d$ = ground | | 9 | EulerŌĆōSavary Equation | $\frac{1}{(r-u)} + \frac{1}{(r'-u)} = \frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin\phi}$ | Curvature of path; $u$ = pole, $r$ & $r'$ = circle of curvature distances | | 10 | Inflection Circle (four-bar) | $u_{inf} = \frac{(d-a)(d+a)}{(d-a)+(d+a)\cos\phi}$ | Pole location at which $R \to \infty$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the Freudenstein's equation in the form **$K_1 \cos\theta_3 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$**. This single equation is worth up to **7 marks** in KTU ESE questions on four-bar synthesis. ## 2.3 Why These Methods Are Essential The analytical framework converts mechanism analysis into a **solvable, deterministic algebraic problem**. This enables: - **Synthesis** ŌĆö designing dimensions $a, b, c, d$ to achieve desired motion. - **Optimization** ŌĆö sweeping input angles programmatically. - **Sensitivity analysis** ŌĆö measuring how output angle $\theta_3$ responds to dimensional tolerances. - **Dynamic simulation** ŌĆö feeding accelerations into Newton's equations to find joint forces. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Loop Closure Equation for a Four-Bar Linkage ### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup Consider a planar four-bar mechanism $O_2 A B O_4$ with: - Ground link $O_2 O_4$ of length $d$ - Input crank $O_2 A$ of length $a$ at angle $\theta_2$ - Coupler $AB$ of length $b$ at angle $\theta_3$ - Output crank $O_4 B$ of length $c$ at angle $\theta_4$ The vector loop traversed in CCW direction from $O_2 \to A \to B \to O_4 \to O_2$ must close. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation Starting from $O_2$ and traversing the loop back to $O_2$: $$ \vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_4 - \vec{r}_1 = \vec{0} $$ where $\vec{r}_1 = d\, e^{j\theta_1}$ (ground), $\vec{r}_2 = a\, e^{j\theta_2}$ (input), $\vec{r}_3 = b\, e^{j\theta_3}$ (coupler), $\vec{r}_4 = c\, e^{j\theta_4}$ (output). Taking $\theta_1 = 0$ (ground along x-axis), and rearranging: $$ a\, e^{j\theta_2} + b\, e^{j\theta_3} = c\, e^{j\theta_4} + d $$ Now expanding using Euler's formula $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$ and separating real & imaginary parts: **Real Component:** $$ a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = c\cos\theta_4 + d $$ **Imaginary Component:** $$ a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 $$ These are the **two scalar loop closure equations** governing the four-bar's geometry. ### 3.1.3 Solving for the Output Angle $\theta_4$ (Freudenstein's Form) From the real equation: $b\cos\theta_3 = c\cos\theta_4 + d - a\cos\theta_2$ Squaring both real and imaginary equations, adding them, and using $\sin^2\theta_4 + \cos^2\theta_4 = 1$: $$ b^2 = c^2 + d^2 + a^2 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 + 2c(d - a\cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_4 - 2ac\sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_4 $$ Rearranging to **eliminate $b$** between the equations leads to Freudenstein's classic form. The standard route uses the algebraic identity: $$ \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4 $$ After substitution and rearrangement: $$ \frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2 + \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) $$ Defining constants: $$ K_1 = \frac{d}{a} \quad ; \quad K_2 = \frac{d}{c} \quad ; \quad K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} $$ We obtain the **Freudenstein Equation**: $$ K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on notation:** Many textbooks (and the KTU model answer scheme) swap $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ ŌĆö using $\theta_3$ for the output crank. Either convention is acceptable as long as it is **stated clearly**. ## 3.2 Velocity Analysis via Jacobian Differentiation Differentiate the loop equation with respect to time $t$: $$ j\dot{\theta}_2 a\, e^{j\theta_2} + j\dot{\theta}_3 b\, e^{j\theta_3} - j\dot{\theta}_4 c\, e^{j\theta_4} = 0 $$ Separating into real and imaginary: **Real:** $$ -a\dot{\theta}_2\sin\theta_2 - b\dot{\theta}_3\sin\theta_3 + c\dot{\theta}_4\sin\theta_4 = 0 $$ **Imaginary:** $$ a\dot{\theta}_2\cos\theta_2 + b\dot{\theta}_3\cos\theta_3 - c\dot{\theta}_4\cos\theta_4 = 0 $$ In **matrix form**, with $\omega_2 = \dot{\theta}_2$ as the known input angular velocity: $$ \begin{bmatrix} -b\sin\theta_3 & c\sin\theta_4 \\ b\cos\theta_3 & -c\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{bmatrix} = \omega_2 \begin{bmatrix} a\sin\theta_2 \\ -a\cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Solving this 2├Ś2 linear system (e.g., by Cramer's rule) yields $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ analytically. ## 3.3 Acceleration Analysis Differentiating the velocity equation once more: **Real Component:** $$ -a(\ddot{\theta}_2\sin\theta_2 + \dot{\theta}_2^{\,2}\cos\theta_2) - b(\ddot{\theta}_3\sin\theta_3 + \dot{\theta}_3^{\,2}\cos\theta_3) + c(\ddot{\theta}_4\sin\theta_4 + \dot{\theta}_4^{\,2}\cos\theta_4) = 0 $$ **Imaginary Component:** $$ a(\ddot{\theta}_2\cos\theta_2 - \dot{\theta}_2^{\,2}\sin\theta_2) + b(\ddot{\theta}_3\cos\theta_3 - \dot{\theta}_3^{\,2}\sin\theta_3) - c(\ddot{\theta}_4\cos\theta_4 - \dot{\theta}_4^{\,2}\sin\theta_4) = 0 $$ In matrix form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} -b\sin\theta_3 & c\sin\theta_4 \\ b\cos\theta_3 & -c\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_3 \\ \alpha_4 \end{bmatrix} = \omega_2^{\,2} \begin{bmatrix} a\cos\theta_2 \\ a\sin\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b\dot{\theta}_3^{\,2}\cos\theta_3 - c\dot{\theta}_4^{\,2}\cos\theta_4 \\ b\dot{\theta}_3^{\,2}\sin\theta_3 - c\dot{\theta}_4^{\,2}\sin\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} $$ The same **2├Ś2 coefficient matrix** (call it $\mathbf{J}$) appears as in velocity analysis ŌĆö this is the **Jacobian** of the mechanism. Once $\omega_3, \omega_4$ are known, the right-hand side is fully determined, and $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$ follow by simple matrix inversion. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Analytical Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mechanism Geometry Definition
a b c d and theta2] --> B[Formulate Loop Closure Equation
sum of vectors equals zero] B --> C[Separate Real and Imaginary
Two Scalar Equations] C --> D[Position Analysis
Solve for theta3 and theta4] D --> E[Differentiation w.r.t. time
Velocity Equations] E --> F[Form 2x2 Jacobian Matrix J] F --> G[Invert Jacobian
Solve Linear System] G --> H[Velocity Outputs
omega3 and omega4] E --> I[Second Differentiation
Acceleration Equations] I --> J[Substitute omega3 omega4
Known Velocities] J --> K[Acceleration Outputs
alpha3 and alpha4] H --> L[Dynamic Force Analysis
Newtons Equations] K --> L L --> M[Final Outputs
Joint Forces and Torques] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow for Freudenstein-Based Synthesis ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Design Specification
Desired Motion theta4 vs theta2] --> Q[Compute K1 K2 K3
from Link Lengths] Q --> R[Apply Freudenstein Equation
K1 cos theta3 minus K2 cos theta2 plus K3 equals cos theta2 minus theta3] R --> S[Solve Transcendental Equation
for theta3] S --> T[Iterate over theta2 Range
full cycle 0 to 2pi] T --> U[Compare with Desired Motion
Minimize Error] U --> V{Error Acceptable?} V -->|No| W[Adjust Link Lengths a b c d] W --> Q V -->|Yes| X[Final Link Dimensions] ``` ## 4.3 Loop Closure ŌĆö Vector Polygon Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Link 1: Ground d
Vector r1 along x axis] --> L2[Link 2: Input crank a
Vector r2 at angle theta2] L2 --> L3[Link 3: Coupler b
Vector r3 at angle theta3] L3 --> L4[Link 4: Output crank c
Vector r4 at angle theta4] L4 --> L1 SUM[Vector Sum Condition
r2 plus r3 minus r4 minus r1 equals zero] --> L2 ``` ## 4.4 Analytical vs. Graphical ŌĆö Method Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Traditional G1[Sketch Mechanism] --> G2[Draw Velocity Polygon] --> G3[Measure with Scale] end subgraph Analytical A1[Define Link Vectors] --> A2[Form Loop Closure Equation] --> A3[Differentiate Analytically] --> A4[Solve Linear System] end G3 --> CMP[Comparison] A4 --> CMP CMP --> R1[Analytical Wins for
Parametric Studies] CMP --> R2[Graphical Wins for
Quick Single Configuration] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** All diagrams above are presented as **block-level functional architecture** because the physical geometric drawing of a four-bar requires precise CAD tooling. The flowcharts faithfully capture the *information flow* of analytical methods as tested in the KTU ESE. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** *State Freudenstein's equation for a four-bar linkage. Define each constant in terms of the link lengths.* **Model Answer:** Freudenstein's equation for a four-bar linkage $O_2ABO_4$ with input crank $a$, coupler $b$, output $c$, and ground $d$ is: $$ K_1\cos\theta_3 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) $$ where the dimensionless constants are: - $K_1 = \dfrac{d}{a}$ - $K_2 = \dfrac{d}{c}$ - $K_3 = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$ It directly relates the input angle $\theta_2$ and output angle $\theta_3$ **without computing the coupler angle**, making it the most efficient tool for **function generation synthesis**. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024]** *Differentiate between loop closure equations and Freudenstein's equation.* **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Loop Closure Equation** | **Freudenstein's Equation** | |:-----------|:--------------------------|:----------------------------| | Form | Vector or complex exponential | Trigonometric scalar | | Variables involved | All link vectors and angles | Only input and output angles | | Coupler angle | Required as $\theta_3$ | Eliminated | | Primary use | Position, velocity, acceleration analysis | Function generation synthesis | | Output | All joint angles | Output angle $\theta_3$ directly | --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]** *For a four-bar linkage with link lengths $a = 50\text{ mm}$, $b = 150\text{ mm}$, $c = 80\text{ mm}$, $d = 120\text{ mm}$, and input crank angle $\theta_2 = 60┬░$:* **(a)** *Derive the loop closure equation in vector form and determine the output angle $\theta_3$ using Freudenstein's equation. (7 marks)* **(b)** *If the input crank rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega_2 = 10\text{ rad/s}$, determine the angular velocities $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ of the coupler and output crank. (7 marks)* #### Part (a) Model Solution **Loop Closure Equation Derivation:** $$ a\,e^{j\theta_2} + b\,e^{j\theta_3} = c\,e^{j\theta_4} + d $$ **Freudenstein's Constants:** $$ K_1 = \frac{d}{a} = \frac{120}{50} = 2.4 $$ $$ K_2 = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{120}{80} = 1.5 $$ $$ K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} = \frac{2500 - 22500 + 6400 + 14400}{2(50)(80)} = \frac{800}{8000} = 0.1 $$ **Substituting $\theta_2 = 60┬░$:** $$ 2.4\cos\theta_3 - 1.5\cos 60┬░ + 0.1 = \cos(60┬░ - \theta_3) $$ $$ 2.4\cos\theta_3 - 0.75 + 0.1 = 0.5\cos\theta_3 + 0.866\sin\theta_3 $$ $$ 1.9\cos\theta_3 - 0.866\sin\theta_3 = 0.65 $$ Solving by the auxiliary angle method: Let $R = \sqrt{1.9^2 + 0.866^2} = \sqrt{3.61 + 0.75} = \sqrt{4.36} \approx 2.088$ $$ \tan\phi = \frac{0.866}{1.9} \Rightarrow \phi \approx 24.5┬░ $$ $$ 2.088\cos(\theta_3 + 24.5┬░) = 0.65 $$ $$ \cos(\theta_3 + 24.5┬░) = 0.311 $$ $$ \theta_3 + 24.5┬░ = 71.9┬░ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_3 \approx 47.4┬░ $$ (Open configuration chosen; the crossed branch gives $\theta_3 \approx -96.4┬░$.) #### Part (b) Model Solution **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\theta_4$ from the real loop equation:** $$ a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = c\cos\theta_4 + d $$ $$ 50(0.5) + 150\cos 47.4┬░ = 80\cos\theta_4 + 120 $$ $$ 25 + 150(0.677) = 80\cos\theta_4 + 120 $$ $$ 25 + 101.55 = 80\cos\theta_4 + 120 $$ $$ 80\cos\theta_4 = 6.55 \Rightarrow \cos\theta_4 = 0.082 \Rightarrow \theta_4 \approx 85.3┬░ $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Form the velocity Jacobian system:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} -b\sin\theta_3 & c\sin\theta_4 \\ b\cos\theta_3 & -c\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{bmatrix} = \omega_2 \begin{bmatrix} a\sin\theta_2 \\ -a\cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Substituting numerical values ($\theta_3 = 47.4┬░$, $\theta_4 = 85.3┬░$, $\omega_2 = 10$ rad/s): $$ \begin{bmatrix} -150(0.736) & 80(0.997) \\ 150(0.677) & -80(0.082) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{bmatrix} = 10 \begin{bmatrix} 50(0.866) \\ -50(0.5) \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} -110.4 & 79.76 \\ 101.55 & -6.56 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 433 \\ -250 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Solve by Cramer's rule:** Determinant: $$ \Delta = (-110.4)(-6.56) - (79.76)(101.55) = 724.2 - 8099.6 = -7375.4 $$ For $\omega_3$: $$ \omega_3 = \frac{(433)(-6.56) - (79.76)(-250)}{-7375.4} = \frac{-2840.5 + 19940}{-7375.4} = \frac{17099.5}{-7375.4} \approx -2.32 \text{ rad/s} $$ For $\omega_4$: $$ \omega_4 = \frac{(-110.4)(-250) - (433)(101.55)}{-7375.4} = \frac{27600 - 43971}{-7375.4} = \frac{-16371}{-7375.4} \approx 2.22 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Final Answer:** $\omega_3 \approx -2.32$ rad/s (clockwise); $\omega_4 \approx 2.22$ rad/s (CCW). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024 Alternative]** *(a) Derive the EulerŌĆōSavary equation for curvature analysis of a four-bar mechanism. Explain the significance of the inflection circle. (7 marks) (b) A four-bar has links $a = 40$ mm, $b = 100$ mm, $c = 60$ mm, $d = 90$ mm. Using Gruebler's criterion, verify the mobility. For input crank angle $\theta_2 = 45┬░$, compute the Freudenstein constant $K_3$ and identify whether the mechanism is in the Grashof or non-Grashof class. (7 marks)* #### Part (a) Model Solution **Derivation of EulerŌĆōSavary Equation:** For a point on the coupler undergoing general plane motion, the center of curvature traces a relationship involving the **pole** $P$ (instant center of the coupler link), the **circle of curvature** of the path, and the **circle of curvature of the fixed centrode**. Let: - $u$ = distance from pole to circle of curvature of fixed centrode - $r$ = distance from pole to point on coupler - $r'$ = distance from pole to center of curvature of the path at that point - $R$ = radius of curvature of fixed centrode - $\phi$ = pressure angle The EulerŌĆōSavary equation is: $$ \left(\frac{1}{r - u}\right) - \left(\frac{1}{r' - u}\right) = \frac{1}{R \sin\phi} $$ Equivalent classical form: $$ \frac{1}{(r-u)} + \frac{1}{(r'-u)} = \frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin\phi} $$ **Inflection Circle:** The inflection circle is the locus of points on the moving plane whose path has **infinite radius of curvature** ($R_{path} \to \infty$), meaning the path has an **inflection point** (transition between concave and convex). Its radius from the pole is: $$ u_{inf} = \frac{(d-a)(d+a)}{(d-a)+(d+a)\cos\phi} $$ **Significance:** - Used in **path generation synthesis** to ensure smooth, cusp-free paths. - Critical in **cam design** where inflection points lead to high dynamic loads. - Determines whether a follower motion is "well-behaved" for high-speed applications. #### Part (b) Model Solution **Step 1 ŌĆö Gruebler's Mobility:** For a four-bar: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (four revolute joints). $$ M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 9 - 8 = 1 $$ The mechanism has **1 degree of freedom** ŌĆö consistent with a four-bar. **Step 2 ŌĆö Freudenstein's $K_3$:** $$ K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} = \frac{40^2 - 100^2 + 60^2 + 90^2}{2(40)(60)} = \frac{1600 - 10000 + 3600 + 8100}{4800} = \frac{3300}{4800} = 0.6875 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Grashof Classification:** Grashof's law: $s + l \le p + q$, where $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p, q$ = the other two. Here: $s = a = 40$, $l = b = 100$, $p = c = 60$, $q = d = 90$. Check: $s + l = 40 + 100 = 140$; $p + q = 60 + 90 = 150$. Since $140 \le 150$, **Grashof condition is satisfied**. Because the shortest link $a$ is the **input crank (adjacent to ground)**, this is a **Grashof crank-rocker mechanism** ŌĆö the input link $a$ can rotate fully ($360┬░$), while the output link $c$ oscillates. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Sign of $\omega$**: Students often forget to apply the negative sine correctly during Jacobian differentiation ŌĆö one sign slip produces wrong rotational sense. > 2. **Crossed vs. Open Configuration**: For the same $\theta_2$, two values of $\theta_3$ exist (open and crossed). Always **state the branch chosen**; otherwise 2 marks will be deducted. > 3. **Units in Freudenstein**: The constants $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are **dimensionless** ŌĆö if numerical $K$ values emerge with units, a link has been mis-assigned. > 4. **Grashof Misdiagnosis**: Students confuse $s+l$ comparison. Always sort the four links: shortest ($s$), longest ($l$), then the middle pair ($p, q$). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Ō£ģ **Loop closure equation** is the **starting point** of every analytical kinematic analysis. Express it in complex form $a e^{j\theta_2} + b e^{j\theta_3} - c e^{j\theta_4} - d = 0$. - Ō£ģ **Freudenstein's equation** eliminates the coupler angle and gives a **direct relationship** between input and output angles ŌĆö the workhorse of function generation. - Ō£ģ **Velocity analysis** involves differentiating the loop equation and solving a 2├Ś2 **linear system** in matrix form (the Jacobian). - Ō£ģ **Acceleration analysis** uses the **same Jacobian matrix** as velocity analysis but with additional $\omega^2$ centripetal terms on the right-hand side. - Ō£ģ **EulerŌĆōSavary equation** governs the **curvature of the path** traced by any point on the coupler and is fundamental to **path generation** and **cusp avoidance**. - Ō£ģ **Grashof's criterion** $s + l \le p + q$ classifies four-bars into **crank-rocker, double-crank, double-rocker, or change-point** mechanisms ŌĆö must be checked before any synthesis. - Ō£ģ **Angular sign convention**: CCW positive. For a vector rotating at $\omega$, its tip velocity is $j\omega\vec{r}$. - Ō£ģ **KTU Board Favorite Question Pattern**: "Given link lengths and $\theta_2$, find $\theta_3$ via Freudenstein and $\omega_3, \omega_4$ via Jacobian." - Ō£ģ Always **draw the loop in one consistent direction** (CCW or CW) ŌĆö inconsistency produces a sign error in the final equation. - Ō£ģ **Numerical sanity check**: After solving, substitute $\theta_3, \theta_4$ back into both real and imaginary loop equations and verify they are satisfied to within rounding error.

topics.title: Chase Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chase Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Chase Solution** (also called the **Chase Method** or **Iterative Position-Solving Method**) is a numerical-analytical procedure used in planar mechanism analysis to determine the unknown angular positions of links in a multi-loop kinematic chain when the input link position is prescribed. The technique systematically "chases" through the kinematic chain, applying the **vector loop-closure equation** at each joint to propagate the known motion and resolve the unknown link positions through a converging sequence of estimates.

In the context of **four-bar, slider-crank, and multi-loop mechanisms**, the chase solution becomes essential when:
- Closed-form (direct) analytical solutions like the **Freudenstein equation** become unwieldy due to non-linear transcendental terms.
- The mechanism has multiple degrees of freedom, making direct inversion impractical.
- Synthesis problems require checking if a particular Burmester point is reachable for a given dyad configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Chase Solution is treated as a numerical position-analysis tool in Module 2 (Kinematic Synthesis & Analysis), where students must demonstrate proficiency in formulating loop-closure equations, performing the iterative chase, and verifying convergence for planar mechanisms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large warehouse and given a rope of fixed length, with one end tied to a fixed pole on the floor. You are told: *"The rope is currently attached to a cart at coordinates (xŌéü, yŌéü). I am pulling the cart with another rope of fixed length, and the far end of that rope slides on a horizontal track."* 

You want to know **where the cart is** after I pull the rope by an angle of 30┬░.

You cannot see anything, so you cannot measure angles directly. What do you do?

You **guess** the cart's position, calculate how much the angles *should* have changed based on the rope lengths, compare with the actual change, and **adjust your guess**. You keep doing this until your guess is "close enough." That iterative refinement ŌĆö *guess ŌåÆ check ŌåÆ correct ŌåÆ repeat* ŌĆö is exactly what the **Chase Solution** does for a four-bar mechanism.

> **Real-World Analogy:** A person navigating in fog by trial-and-error using a compass and known distances ŌĆö each step refines the estimate of position.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Link length | $r$ or $a$ | **mm** (or **m**) |
| Link angle | $\theta$ | **radians** (rad) or **degrees** (┬░) |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Convergence tolerance | $\epsilon$ | typically **0.001 rad** |
| Loop count | $L$ | dimensionless integer |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard **convergence tolerance** for KTU board numerical problems is **$\epsilon \le 0.01┬░$** for angular precision unless otherwise specified in the question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle ŌĆö Vector Loop Closure

Every planar mechanism must satisfy the **Vector Loop-Closure Equation** at every instant. For an $n$-loop mechanism, the chase method solves these equations one loop at a time.

For a simple **four-bar mechanism** (single loop), the loop-closure equation is:

$$\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_3$$

Where:
- $\vec{r}_1$ = vector of the input link (crank)
- $\vec{r}_2$ = vector of the coupler link
- $\vec{r}_3$ = vector of the output link (rocker)
- $\vec{r}_4$ = vector of the fixed link (ground)

Resolving into scalar $x$ and $y$ components:

$$r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_2 \cos\theta_2 - r_3 \cos\theta_3 - r_4 = 0$$

$$r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \sin\theta_3 = 0$$

These are **two non-linear transcendental equations** in two unknowns ($\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$) when $\theta_1$ is given as input.

## 2.2 Why Direct Solution Fails ŌĆö The "Why" of the Chase Method

The scalar equations are **non-linear** in the angles (because of $\cos$ and $\sin$ terms). A closed-form solution is possible (giving the famous Freudenstein equation), but:
1. It requires the elimination of variables, which is algebraically messy for complex chains.
2. Multiple solutions (assembly configurations) require case-by-case handling.
3. For **multi-loop mechanisms** (like six-bar Stephenson or Watt chains), the algebra becomes unmanageable.

**The Chase Method** bypasses these problems by:
- **Rearranging** the loop equations so each unknown is isolated on one side.
- **Iterating** with an initial guess and updating using the previous estimate.
- **Converging** to the correct root within a few iterations.

## 2.3 The Three Step-by-Step Phases of the Chase

### Phase 1 ŌĆö Setup
Identify all links, their lengths, and the input angle(s). Write the vector loop-closure equation for each independent loop.

### Phase 2 ŌĆö Rearrangement
Rearrange the equation so that **one unknown vector** is expressed in terms of known quantities. For the four-bar, isolate $\vec{r}_2$:

$$\vec{r}_2 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_1$$

The magnitude of $\vec{r}_2$ is **fixed** ($|\vec{r}_2| = r_2$ = constant), which is the **constraint** that the iterative method enforces.

### Phase 3 ŌĆö Iteration
1. Make an **initial guess** for $\theta_3$ (e.g., using the previous time-step value or a graphical estimate).
2. Compute $\vec{r}_2$ from the right-hand side.
3. Calculate the **error**: $e = |\vec{r}_2|_{computed} - r_2$.
4. Adjust $\theta_3$ using a gradient-based update (NewtonŌĆōRaphson style): 

$$\theta_3^{(k+1)} = \theta_3^{(k)} - \frac{e}{\partial e / \partial \theta_3}$$

5. Repeat until $|e| < \epsilon$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{r}_i = 0$ | Vector loop-closure equation for a single loop | dimensionless |
| 2 | $r_1\cos\theta_1 + r_2\cos\theta_2 = r_3\cos\theta_3 + r_4$ | $x$-component scalar equation | mm |
| 3 | $r_1\sin\theta_1 + r_2\sin\theta_2 = r_3\sin\theta_3$ | $y$-component scalar equation | mm |
| 4 | $K_1 = r_1\cos\theta_1 - r_4$ | Freudenstein coefficient (constant for a given $\theta_1$) | mm |
| 5 | $K_2 = r_1\sin\theta_1$ | Freudenstein coefficient | mm |
| 6 | $\theta_3^{(k+1)} = \theta_3^{(k)} - \dfrac{F(\theta_3^{(k)})}{F'(\theta_3^{(k)})}$ | NewtonŌĆōRaphson update (chase iteration) | rad |
| 7 | $\epsilon = \vert \theta_3^{(k+1)} - \theta_3^{(k)} \vert$ | Convergence test (typically $\le 0.01┬░$) | rad |
| 8 | $e = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | Position error for vector magnitude check | mm |
| 9 | $F(\theta_3) = (K_1 + r_3\cos\theta_3)^2 + (K_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3)^2 - r_2^2$ | Non-linear scalar function to be zeroed | mm┬▓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, **Freudenstein's equation** is the analytical equivalent of the chase solution for a four-bar. Students often confuse the two. **Freudenstein = direct closed-form; Chase = iterative numerical.** Both solve the same geometry.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Chase Solution is the **backbone of Computer-Aided Mechanism Design** software like:
- **ADAMS** (Automatic Dynamic Analysis of Mechanical Systems)
- **Working Model 2D**
- **MATLAB SimMechanics**

Every time these tools "solve" a mechanism for a given input angle, they internally use a chase-method-like iterative solver (often **NewtonŌĆōRaphson** on the loop-closure residual). Understanding the chase method is therefore **fundamental for robotics, automotive suspension design, prosthetic limbs, and cam-actuated packaging machinery.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Scalar Loop-Closure Equations

We start with the **vector equation** for a four-bar mechanism with ground link $\vec{r}_4$ along the positive $x$-axis, and the crank $\vec{r}_1$ at input angle $\theta_1$:

$$\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_4 = 0$$

Substituting vector components $\vec{r}_i = r_i(\cos\theta_i, \sin\theta_i)$:

$$r_1(\cos\theta_1, \sin\theta_1) + r_2(\cos\theta_2, \sin\theta_2) - r_3(\cos\theta_3, \sin\theta_3) - r_4(1, 0) = (0, 0)$$

**Separating the $x$ and $y$ components:**

**x-direction:**
$$r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_2 \cos\theta_2 - r_3 \cos\theta_3 - r_4 = 0$$

**y-direction:**
$$r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \sin\theta_3 = 0$$

These two equations constitute the **non-linear constraint set** that the chase method must solve.

## 3.2 Rearrangement into Iterative Form

**Step 1** ŌĆö Isolate $r_2 \cos\theta_2$ and $r_2 \sin\theta_2$ on the left side:

$$r_2 \cos\theta_2 = r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3$$

$$r_2 \sin\theta_2 = -r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_3 \sin\theta_3$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Square both equations and add them. This uses the **Pythagorean identity** $\cos^2\theta_2 + \sin^2\theta_2 = 1$, eliminating $\theta_2$ entirely:

$$r_2^2(\cos^2\theta_2 + \sin^2\theta_2) = (r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 + (-r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_3 \sin\theta_3)^2$$

$$r_2^2 = (K_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 + (K_2 - r_3 \sin\theta_3)^2$$

where we have defined:
$$K_1 = r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1$$
$$K_2 = r_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Define the scalar function $F(\theta_3)$ whose root we seek:

$$F(\theta_3) = (K_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 + (K_2 - r_3 \sin\theta_3)^2 - r_2^2 = 0$$

**This is the canonical Freudenstein form**, and the chase method finds its root iteratively.

## 3.3 The NewtonŌĆōRaphson Iteration (The Heart of the Chase)

To iterate, we need the **derivative** of $F$ with respect to $\theta_3$:

$$\frac{dF}{d\theta_3} = 2(K_1 + r_3\cos\theta_3)(-r_3 \sin\theta_3) + 2(K_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3)(-r_3 \cos\theta_3)$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{dF}{d\theta_3} = -2r_3 \left[ (K_1 + r_3\cos\theta_3)\sin\theta_3 + (K_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3)\cos\theta_3 \right]$$

The **NewtonŌĆōRaphson update rule** is then:

$$\theta_3^{(k+1)} = \theta_3^{(k)} - \frac{F(\theta_3^{(k)})}{F'(\theta_3^{(k)})}$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Choose an **initial guess** $\theta_3^{(0)}$. For continuous tracking of input motion, the previous time-step value $\theta_3^{(t-\Delta t)}$ is used. For a fresh start, a graphical estimate or the value at $\theta_1 = 0$ works.

**Step 5** ŌĆö Iterate until convergence:

$$\left\vert \theta_3^{(k+1)} - \theta_3^{(k)} \right\vert \le \epsilon$$

Once $\theta_3$ is known, **back-substitute** to find $\theta_2$:

$$\theta_2 = \arctan\left( \frac{-r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_3 \sin\theta_3}{r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3} \right)$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** Four-bar mechanism with $r_1 = 50$ mm, $r_2 = 150$ mm, $r_3 = 100$ mm, $r_4 = 200$ mm. Input angle $\theta_1 = 60┬░$. Initial guess $\theta_3^{(0)} = 90┬░$.

**Compute the Freudenstein coefficients:**

$$K_1 = 200 - 50 \cos(60┬░) = 200 - 25 = 175 \text{ mm}$$

$$K_2 = 50 \sin(60┬░) = 50 \times 0.8660 = 43.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Iteration 1** ŌĆö At $\theta_3^{(0)} = 90┬░$:

$$F(90┬░) = (175 + 100 \cdot 0)^2 + (43.30 - 100 \cdot 1)^2 - 150^2$$

$$F(90┬░) = (175)^2 + (-56.70)^2 - 22500$$

$$F(90┬░) = 30625 + 3214.89 - 22500 = 11339.89 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Compute the derivative at $\theta_3^{(0)} = 90┬░$:**

$$F'(90┬░) = -2(100)\left[ (175 + 0)(1) + (-56.70)(0) \right] = -2(100)(175) = -35000 \text{ mm}^2/\text{rad}$$

**Update $\theta_3$:**

$$\theta_3^{(1)} = 90┬░ - \frac{11339.89}{-35000} \times \frac{180┬░}{\pi}$$

$$\theta_3^{(1)} = 90┬░ + 18.55┬░ \times \frac{180┬░}{\pi} \approx 90┬░ + 5.91┬░ \times \text{conversion factor}$$

Let me recalculate carefully: $\dfrac{11339.89}{35000} = 0.3240$ rad $= 18.57┬░$.

$$\theta_3^{(1)} = 90┬░ + 18.57┬░ = 108.57┬░$$

**Iteration 2** ŌĆö At $\theta_3^{(1)} = 108.57┬░$, recompute $F$:

$$\cos(108.57┬░) = -0.3174, \quad \sin(108.57┬░) = 0.9483$$

$$F(108.57┬░) = (175 + 100 \cdot (-0.3174))^2 + (43.30 - 100 \cdot 0.9483)^2 - 22500$$

$$F(108.57┬░) = (143.26)^2 + (-51.53)^2 - 22500$$

$$F(108.57┬░) = 20523.4 + 2655.3 - 22500 = 678.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Derivative at $\theta_3^{(1)} = 108.57┬░$:**

$$F'(108.57┬░) = -200 \left[ (143.26)(0.9483) + (-51.53)(-0.3174) \right]$$

$$F'(108.57┬░) = -200 \left[ 135.86 + 16.36 \right] = -200(152.22) = -30444 \text{ mm}^2/\text{rad}$$

**Update:**

$$\Delta\theta_3 = \frac{678.7}{30444} = 0.02230 \text{ rad} = 1.278┬░$$

$$\theta_3^{(2)} = 108.57┬░ + 1.28┬░ = 109.85┬░$$

**Convergence check:** $\vert 109.85┬░ - 108.57┬░ \vert = 1.28┬░ > 0.01┬░$ ŌåÆ continue.

**Iteration 3** would reduce this to $\theta_3 \approx 109.93┬░$ ŌĆö well within tolerance.

> **Final Answer:** $\theta_3 \approx 109.9┬░$ for $\theta_1 = 60┬░$ using the chase method with tolerance $\epsilon = 0.01┬░$.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which Method

| Method | Best Suited For | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Freudenstein Equation** | Single-loop 4-bar, fixed link along x-axis | Closed-form, one equation in one unknown | Requires specific reference frame |
| **Chase Method (NewtonŌĆōRaphson)** | Multi-loop, general geometry, transient analysis | Flexible, robust, programmable | Needs good initial guess, may diverge |
| **Graphical Method** | Quick design sketches, concept verification | Visual, no math | Low precision, not for 14-mark problems |
| **MATLAB `fsolve`** | Complex multi-DOF systems | Automated, handles large systems | "Black box" ŌĆö examiners may deduct marks for lack of manual steps |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö The Chase Method Algorithm Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete iterative logic of the chase method as applied to a four-bar mechanism:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Input: r1, r2, r3, r4, theta1, epsilon/]
    B --> C[/Compute K1 and K2/]
    C --> D[/Initial guess theta3_k = theta3_old/]
    D --> E[/Compute F theta3_k using loop closure/]
    E --> F[/Compute dF/dtheta3 at theta3_k/]
    F --> G{Is dF/dtheta3 zero?}
    G -- Yes --> H[/Stop: Method fails, choose new guess/]
    G -- No --> I[/Update: theta3_new = theta3_k - F/Fprime/]
    I --> J{Is error less than epsilon?}
    J -- No --> K[/Set theta3_k = theta3_new/]
    K --> E
    J -- Yes --> L[/Back-substitute to find theta2/]
    L --> M[/Output: theta2, theta3/]
    M --> N([End])
    H --> N
```

## 4.2 System-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Multi-Loop Mechanism Solver

For multi-loop mechanisms (e.g., a six-bar Stephenson chain), the chase method is applied **loop by loop** in a cascaded architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LoopOne [Independent Loop 1]
        L1A[Crank theta1 known] --> L1B[Chase Solver 1]
        L1B --> L1C[Output theta3]
    end
    subgraph LoopTwo [Independent Loop 2]
        L2A[theta3 from Loop 1] --> L2B[Chase Solver 2]
        L2B --> L2C[Output theta5]
    end
    subgraph LoopThree [Independent Loop 3]
        L3A[theta3 and theta5] --> L3B[Chase Solver 3]
        L3B --> L3C[Output theta7]
    end
    L1C --> L2A
    L2C --> L3A
    L1B -.uses.-> F1[Vector Loop-Closure Equation]
    L2B -.uses.-> F2[Vector Loop-Closure Equation]
    L3B -.uses.-> F3[Vector Loop-Closure Equation]
    L1A[theta1 input from driver] --> L1B
```

## 4.3 Free-Body Schematic of the Four-Bar (Text Description)

Since a true mechanism drawing is best done physically, the textual schematic is:

```
        B (joint)
       / \
      /   \  r2 (coupler)
     /     \
    A-------C
   r1        \  r3 (output)
   (crank)    \
   theta1      \
                D (fixed pivot)
   ----r4---- (ground, along x-axis)
```

- **A** = fixed pivot of crank (origin)
- **B** = moving pivot between crank and coupler
- **C** = moving pivot between coupler and rocker
- **D** = fixed pivot of rocker
- Vector loop: $\vec{AB} + \vec{BC} = \vec{AD} + \vec{DC}$, i.e., $\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board answers, always **draw a labelled figure** of the four-bar showing all four link lengths and the input angle $\theta_1$ before starting the algebraic chase. Marks are awarded for the figure even in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Q: Define the "Chase Solution" in the context of planar mechanism analysis. State two situations where it is preferred over the closed-form Freudenstein equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Chase Solution** is an iterative numerical procedure used to solve the vector loop-closure equations of a planar mechanism for the unknown link angles, given the input angle. It proceeds by isolating one unknown, making an initial guess, and updating it using a NewtonŌĆōRaphson type iteration until the position-error residual drops below a specified tolerance.

**Two situations where it is preferred:**
1. **Multi-loop mechanisms** (e.g., six-bar Stephenson or Watt chains) where the Freudenstein elimination becomes algebraically prohibitive.
2. **General reference frames** where the ground link is not aligned with the $x$-axis, making the Freudenstein form inconvenient.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the two situations.)*

---

### Question A2: Algorithmic Recall `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the four essential steps of the Chase Method in their correct order.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Setup:** Identify the input angle and write the vector loop-closure equation.
2. **Isolate:** Rearrange to express one unknown vector (e.g., $\vec{r}_2$) in terms of the others.
3. **Iterate:** Use NewtonŌĆōRaphson to update the guess until the magnitude error falls below tolerance $\epsilon$.
4. **Back-substitute:** Once the intermediate variable converges, recover the remaining unknowns ($\theta_2$ here).

*(3 marks: 1 for each of the first three steps, partial credit if order is shuffled.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö Full Numerical Chase `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**A 4-bar planar mechanism has link lengths $r_1 = 40$ mm, $r_2 = 120$ mm, $r_3 = 80$ mm, and $r_4 = 160$ mm. The input crank rotates at $60┬░$ from the ground link. Using the Chase Method, determine the output rocker angle $\theta_3$ to within a tolerance of $0.1┬░$. Also find the coupler angle $\theta_2$.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Set up the scalar loop-closure equation and derive the Freudenstein-style scalar function $F(\theta_3)$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vector Loop Closure:**

$$\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_3$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Resolving into $x$ and $y$ components:**

$$r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_2 \cos\theta_2 = r_4 + r_3 \cos\theta_3 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_2 \sin\theta_2 = r_3 \sin\theta_3 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rearrange to isolate $\vec{r}_2$ components:**

$$r_2 \cos\theta_2 = r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3$$

$$r_2 \sin\theta_2 = -r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_3 \sin\theta_3$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Square and add to eliminate $\theta_2$:**

$$r_2^2 = (r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 + (-r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_3 \sin\theta_3)^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute $K_1 = r_4 - r_1 \cos\theta_1$ and $K_2 = r_1 \sin\theta_1$:**

$$F(\theta_3) = (K_1 + r_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 + (K_2 - r_3 \sin\theta_3)^2 - r_2^2 = 0$$

With $\theta_1 = 60┬░$:

$$K_1 = 160 - 40 \cdot 0.5 = 140 \text{ mm}, \quad K_2 = 40 \cdot 0.8660 = 34.64 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{F(\theta_3) = (140 + 80\cos\theta_3)^2 + (34.64 - 80\sin\theta_3)^2 - 14400 = 0}$$

*(7 marks: 1 for figure, 1 for vector equation, 2 for scalar components, 2 for isolation and squaring, 1 for the final scalar function.)*

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Perform two iterations of the NewtonŌĆōRaphson chase starting from $\theta_3^{(0)} = 75┬░$, and report the converged value. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 0 ŌåÆ 1** at $\theta_3^{(0)} = 75┬░$:

$\cos 75┬░ = 0.2588$, $\sin 75┬░ = 0.9659$.

$$F(75┬░) = (140 + 80 \cdot 0.2588)^2 + (34.64 - 80 \cdot 0.9659)^2 - 14400$$

$$F(75┬░) = (160.70)^2 + (-42.63)^2 - 14400 = 25824.5 + 1817.3 - 14400 = 13241.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Derivative:**

$$F'(\theta_3) = -2r_3\left[(K_1 + r_3\cos\theta_3)\sin\theta_3 + (K_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3)\cos\theta_3\right]$$

$$F'(75┬░) = -160\left[(160.70)(0.9659) + (-42.63)(0.2588)\right]$$

$$F'(75┬░) = -160\left[155.22 - 11.03\right] = -160 \cdot 144.19 = -23070.4 \text{ mm}^2/\text{rad}$$

**Update:**

$$\Delta\theta_3 = \frac{13241.8}{-23070.4} = -0.5739 \text{ rad} = -32.88┬░$$

$$\theta_3^{(1)} = 75┬░ - (-32.88┬░) = 107.88┬░$$

**Iteration 1 ŌåÆ 2** at $\theta_3^{(1)} = 107.88┬░$:

$\cos 107.88┬░ = -0.3040$, $\sin 107.88┬░ = 0.9527$.

$$F(107.88┬░) = (140 + 80 \cdot (-0.3040))^2 + (34.64 - 80 \cdot 0.9527)^2 - 14400$$

$$F(107.88┬░) = (115.68)^2 + (-41.58)^2 - 14400 = 13382.0 + 1728.9 - 14400 = 710.9 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Derivative:**

$$F'(107.88┬░) = -160\left[(115.68)(0.9527) + (-41.58)(-0.3040)\right]$$

$$F'(107.88┬░) = -160\left[110.21 + 12.64\right] = -160 \cdot 122.85 = -19656 \text{ mm}^2/\text{rad}$$

**Update:**

$$\Delta\theta_3 = \frac{710.9}{-19656} = -0.03617 \text{ rad} = -2.07┬░$$

$$\theta_3^{(2)} = 107.88┬░ + 2.07┬░ = 109.95┬░$$

**Convergence check:** After one more iteration the value stabilizes at $\theta_3 \approx 109.97┬░$, well within $\epsilon = 0.1┬░$.

**Back-substitute for $\theta_2$:**

$$r_2 \cos\theta_2 = 140 + 80 \cdot \cos(109.97┬░) = 140 - 26.80 = 113.20 \text{ mm}$$

$$r_2 \sin\theta_2 = 34.64 - 80 \cdot \sin(109.97┬░) = 34.64 - 75.41 = -40.77 \text{ mm}$$

$$\theta_2 = \arctan\left(\frac{-40.77}{113.20}\right) = \arctan(-0.3602) = -19.81┬░$$

$$\boxed{\theta_3 \approx 109.97┬░, \quad \theta_2 \approx -19.81┬░}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for iteration 1, 2 for iteration 2, 1 for convergence verification, 2 for back-substitution and final answer.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö Conceptual + Multi-Loop `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**A six-bar Stephenson III mechanism has two independent loops. Explain how the Chase Method would be applied sequentially to determine all unknown angles when the input crank angle is specified.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Draw the schematic of the Stephenson III chain and write the loop-closure equations for both independent loops. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic Description:**

A Stephenson III six-bar mechanism consists of:
- A four-bar primary loop: links $r_1$ (crank), $r_2$ (coupler), $r_3$ (output), $r_4$ (ground), with a ternary link $r_5$ attached to the coupler $r_2$ at point $P$.
- A secondary loop: links $r_5$, $r_6$, and an additional ground pivot.

**Independent Loop 1 (primary four-bar):**

$$\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_3$$

Resolving components:

$$r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_2 \cos\theta_2 = r_4 + r_3 \cos\theta_3$$

$$r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_2 \sin\theta_2 = r_3 \sin\theta_3$$

**Independent Loop 2 (secondary chain through the ternary link):**

$$\vec{r}_2' + \vec{r}_5 + \vec{r}_6 = \vec{r}_4' + \vec{r}_3'$$

where $\vec{r}_2'$ is the vector from point $P$ on the coupler to the secondary pivot.

Resolving:

$$r_5 \cos\theta_5 + r_6 \cos\theta_6 = r_4' + r_3' \cos\theta_3'$$

$$r_5 \sin\theta_5 + r_6 \sin\theta_6 = r_3' \sin\theta_3'$$

*(7 marks: 2 for schematic description, 2 for Loop 1 equations, 3 for Loop 2 equations with proper identification of the ternary link.)*

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Describe the sequential chase procedure: which loop is solved first, how the result feeds into the second loop, and the convergence criterion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Solve Loop 1 first (the simpler four-bar).**
With $\theta_1$ specified, apply the chase iteration to find $\theta_3$. Form the scalar function:

$$F_1(\theta_3) = (K_1 + r_3\cos\theta_3)^2 + (K_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3)^2 - r_2^2 = 0$$

Iterate using NewtonŌĆōRaphson until $\vert F_1 \vert < \epsilon$ and recover $\theta_2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Determine the position of the ternary point $P$ on the coupler.**
With $\theta_2$ known from Step 1, the position of point $P$ is fully determined:

$$x_P = r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_2'\cos\theta_2, \quad y_P = r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_2'\sin\theta_2$$

where $r_2'$ is the distance from the crank pivot to point $P$ along the coupler.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve Loop 2 using the known position of $P$.**
Loop 2 is now a four-bar sub-problem with the position of one end of $r_5$ fixed. Apply the chase iteration to find $\theta_5$ and $\theta_6$:

$$F_2(\theta_6) = (K_3 + r_6 \cos\theta_6)^2 + (K_4 - r_6 \sin\theta_6)^2 - r_5^2 = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convergence Criterion:**
For multi-loop problems, **convergence is checked independently** for each loop:

$$\vert F_1(\theta_3^{(k+1)}) - F_1(\theta_3^{(k)}) \vert < \epsilon_1, \quad \vert F_2(\theta_6^{(k+1)}) - F_2(\theta_6^{(k)}) \vert < \epsilon_2$$

with $\epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = 0.01┬░$ typically.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stopping the overall chase:**
The procedure terminates when **all loop residuals are below tolerance simultaneously**, and a final position check is performed to ensure the assembly-mode branch is consistent.

*(7 marks: 1 for the order of solution, 2 for the ternary point calculation, 2 for the second-loop chase, 2 for the convergence criterion and stopping rule.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Chase Solution Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the figure (1ŌĆō2 marks lost).** Even in numerical problems, a labelled 4-bar sketch with link lengths and angles is mandatory. Examiners explicitly allocate marks for the diagram.
> 2. **Sign errors in the Freudenstein coefficients.** The most common is writing $K_1 = r_4 + r_1\cos\theta_1$ instead of $K_1 = r_4 - r_1\cos\theta_1$. Always derive from the **vector loop** in the correct orientation; never copy from memory.
> 3. **Confusing degrees and radians in the NewtonŌĆōRaphson update.** The derivative $F'(\theta_3)$ is naturally in **rad**. If $\theta_3$ is in degrees, either convert at the end or multiply the update by $180┬░/\pi$.
> 4. **Not checking assembly mode.** A four-bar has two configurations (open and crossed). The chase will converge to *whichever* the initial guess points to. State the assembly mode explicitly.
> 5. **Stopping too early.** KTU expects **at least 2 iterations** with a convergence check shown. A single iteration with no $\epsilon$ test loses the "convergence" marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to back-substitute for $\theta_2$.** A 14-mark chase problem that stops at $\theta_3$ is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chase Solution = iterative numerical solver for loop-closure equations**, most commonly implemented via NewtonŌĆōRaphson.
- The **vector loop-closure equation** $\sum \vec{r}_i = 0$ is the foundation; everything else follows from it.
- For a **four-bar**, the chase reduces to finding the root of $F(\theta_3) = (K_1 + r_3\cos\theta_3)^2 + (K_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3)^2 - r_2^2 = 0$.
- **$K_1 = r_4 - r_1\cos\theta_1$** and **$K_2 = r_1\sin\theta_1$** are the **Freudenstein coefficients** that depend only on the input.
- The **update rule** is $\theta_3^{(k+1)} = \theta_3^{(k)} - F(\theta_3^{(k)}) / F'(\theta_3^{(k)})$.
- **Convergence** is declared when $\vert \theta_3^{(k+1)} - \theta_3^{(k)} \vert \le \epsilon$, typically $0.01┬░$.
- A **good initial guess** (e.g., the previous time-step value) is critical ŌĆö bad guesses can cause divergence.
- For **multi-loop mechanisms**, the chase is applied **sequentially loop by loop**, with intermediate results feeding the next loop.
- The chase method is the **practical backbone** of mechanism simulation software (ADAMS, MATLAB, Working Model).
- **Always draw a labelled four-bar diagram** at the start of any KTU chase problem.
- The chase method is **equivalent to Freudenstein** for a single four-bar with ground along the $x$-axis ŌĆö chase is the iterative twin, Freudenstein is the direct closed-form.
- **Watch the units** ŌĆö $\Delta\theta$ from NewtonŌĆōRaphson is in radians; convert to degrees before reporting.
- **Back-substitution** at the end is non-negotiable: once $\theta_3$ converges, use the $x$ and $y$ component equations to recover $\theta_2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instant Centre of Acceleration, Euler-Savory Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instant Centre of Acceleration, Euler-Savory Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Instant Centre of Zero Acceleration (IŌéÉ or J)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Instant Centre of Zero Acceleration** (also denoted as **IŌéÉ** or **J**) is a point on a rigid body (or its rigid extension) in plane motion that has **zero linear acceleration** at a given instant. At this precise instant, although the point is not necessarily at rest, its acceleration vector is identically zero, meaning it has no tangential and no normal (centripetal) acceleration components.

This is fundamentally different from the **Instant Centre of Zero Velocity (IßĄź or I)**, where the point is momentarily at rest. At **IŌéÉ**, the point is moving with some finite velocity, but its acceleration happens to vanish. The body still rotates about the true ICV (instantaneously), so every point on the body ŌĆö including **IŌéÉ** ŌĆö has a centripetal acceleration of magnitude $\omega^2 r$ directed toward the ICV. The only way a point on the body can have **zero total acceleration** is if its centripetal acceleration is exactly cancelled by the tangential acceleration arising from the angular acceleration of the body.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **giant spinning merry-go-round** (the rigid body) that is also slowing down or speeding up. Every rider feels a *centripetal pull toward the center* (because of $\omega$) AND a *tangential push/pull* (because of $\alpha$, the change in spin rate).

- The **Instant Centre of Velocity (I)** is the exact point on the merry-go-round (or its extension) that is momentarily stationary ŌĆö like a point on the rim that, for a single instant, is moving neither forward nor backward.
- The **Instant Centre of Acceleration (J)** is a totally different point: it is the unique point where these two acceleration components **perfectly cancel out** ŌĆö the centripetal pull toward I is exactly balanced by the tangential pull from angular acceleration. The net acceleration is zero, even though the point is whizzing around at some speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** In general, the points **IßĄź** and **IŌéÉ** are *not* the same. They coincide **only** for a body undergoing pure translation (where $\omega = 0$ and $\alpha = 0$). The famous **EulerŌĆōSavory Equation** precisely relates the locations of IßĄź, IŌéÉ, and the body's angular kinematics.

## 1.2 EulerŌĆōSavory Equation ŌĆö The Kinematic Link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **EulerŌĆōSavory Equation** is the fundamental kinematic relationship that geometrically and analytically connects the **Instant Centre of Zero Velocity (I)**, the **Instant Centre of Zero Acceleration (J)**, the **angular velocity ($\omega$)**, and the **angular acceleration ($\alpha$)** of a rigid body in plane motion. It provides a direct method to locate **J** once the ICV location and the body's angular kinematics are known.

The governing scalar form is:

$$IJ \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2}}{\alpha} \cdot IP$$

where **P** is a strategically chosen point on the body whose acceleration vector $\vec{a}_{P}$ is **perpendicular** to the line $IP$.

> [!NOTE]
> The EulerŌĆōSavory Equation is a *cornerstone* of KTU Module 2 problems on **Kennedy's theorem**, **Aronhold-Kennedy**, and the construction of acceleration polygons for complex planar mechanisms. It is also called the **EulerŌĆōSavary Theorem** in several classical texts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö Why Does IŌéÉ Exist?

A rigid body in plane motion has, at any instant:

1. An **angular velocity** $\omega$ about the ICV (I).
2. An **angular acceleration** $\alpha$ about an axis perpendicular to the plane.

For any arbitrary point P on the body, the total acceleration has two components:

- **Centripetal (normal) acceleration:** $\vec{a}_{P}^{n} = \omega^{2} \overrightarrow{IP}$ directed from P **toward** I.
- **Tangential acceleration:** $\vec{a}_{P}^{t} = \alpha \cdot \overrightarrow{IP}$ perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{IP}$ (direction depends on sign of $\alpha$).

For the **Instant Centre of Zero Acceleration J**, the net acceleration must be zero:

$$\vec{a}_{J} \;=\; \vec{a}_{J}^{n} + \vec{a}_{J}^{t} \;=\; 0$$

Substituting the centripetal and tangential terms for any reference point P on the body (using the relative-acceleration principle):

$$\vec{a}_{J} \;=\; \vec{a}_{P} + \alpha \times \overrightarrow{PJ} - \omega^{2} \overrightarrow{PJ}$$

Setting $\vec{a}_{J} = 0$ and simplifying gives the **EulerŌĆōSavory relation**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic (KTU Exam Method)

1. **Locate the ICV (I)** of the moving body using Kennedy's theorem, the three-centre principle, or the rolling-condyle (centrodes) method.
2. **Identify a point P** on the body such that its acceleration $\vec{a}_{P}$ is **perpendicular** to $IP$. (In mechanisms, this is typically a slider or a point on a slot ŌĆö points whose acceleration is constrained to a known direction.)
3. **Measure the length $IP$** from the located I to point P.
4. **Draw the line $IP$** and then erect a perpendicular to $\vec{a}_{P}$ passing through P ŌĆö this perpendicular intersects $IP$ at the **inflection point PŌĆ▓** of the body.
5. **Apply the EulerŌĆōSavory ratio**: $\dfrac{IJ}{IP'} = \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\alpha}$.
6. **Plot J** on the line $IP'$ at the calculated distance, on the same side as PŌĆ▓ (or opposite, depending on sign convention).
7. **Verify** by checking the acceleration of any known point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Centripetal acceleration of P | $a_{P}^{n} = \omega^{2} \cdot r_{P/I}$ | Toward ICV; units: m/s┬▓ |
| 2 | Tangential acceleration of P | $a_{P}^{t} = \alpha \cdot r_{P/I}$ | $\perp$ to $\overrightarrow{IP}$; units: m/s┬▓ |
| 3 | Total acceleration of P | $a_{P} = \sqrt{(a_{P}^{n})^{2} + (a_{P}^{t})^{2}}$ | Vector sum |
| 4 | **EulerŌĆōSavory Equation (scalar)** | $IJ = \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\alpha} \cdot IP$ | Valid when $\vec{a}_{P} \perp IP$ |
| 5 | **EulerŌĆōSavory Equation (vector form)** | $\overrightarrow{IJ} = \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\alpha} \overrightarrow{IP'}$ | PŌĆ▓ = foot of $\perp$ from P onto acceleration direction |
| 6 | Inflection-circle radius | $\rho = \dfrac{v^{2}}{\alpha \cdot \omega}$ (or related form) | Path curvature at inflection |
| 7 | Angular-velocity ratio | $\omega_{2} = \omega_{1} \cdot \dfrac{I_{12}I_{23}}{I_{13}I_{23}}$ | Kennedy's theorem application |
| 8 | Special case (pure translation) | $I_{v} \equiv I_{a}$ (at infinity) | Only when $\omega = 0$ AND $\alpha = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** $\omega$ is in **rad/s**, $\alpha$ is in **rad/s┬▓**, lengths are in **metres (m)**, and accelerations are in **m/s┬▓**. The ratio $\omega^{2}/\alpha$ has units of **metres**, which is dimensionally consistent with a length.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EulerŌĆōSavory equation is the analytic backbone of:

- **High-speed cam design:** Predicting pressure-angle variations and inflection points in follower motion.
