Mark the fixed pivots A and D on the frame. **Step 2 ŌĆö Draw the coupler BC** and locate the trace point P on the coupler at offset $\rho$ and angle $\theta_P$ from the coupler axis. **Step 3 ŌĆö Construct the diagonal BD** of the quadrilateral. **Step 4 ŌĆö First Cognate (Inversion at A):** - Construct triangle $ABD$ on a fresh sheet. - Construct a new triangle $AB'D$ such that $\angle DAB' = \pi - \angle DAB$ (the supplementary angle). - Mark the new position $B'$ such that $AB' = a_1 = \sqrt{a^2 + d^2 + 2ad\cos(\angle DAB)}$. - The new mechanism is $A B' C' D$ where $C'$ is determined by rigid-body rotation of the coupler. **Step 5 ŌĆö Second Cognate (Inversion at D):** - Similar procedure at pivot D. - Construct triangle $DCB'$ with $\angle CDB' = \pi - \angle CDA$. - The new output link length is $c_1 = \sqrt{c^2 + d^2 + 2cd\cos(\angle CDA)}$. **Step 6 ŌĆö Third Cognate (Inversion at Coupler):** - The coupler $BC$ becomes the new ground. - The diagonal $BD$ becomes the new "fixed link" of the third cognate. - New mechanism: ground length $= BD$, and links $a, b, c$ rearrange as per the inversion. **Step 7 ŌĆö Verification:** For each cognate, verify that the same trace point P (or its equivalent $P'$) describes the same curve as in the original. This is done either graphically (overlay plot) or analytically via Freudenstein's equation. --- #### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):** **Given:** $a = 50$ mm, $b = 120$ mm, $c = 80$ mm, $d = 100$ mm, $\angle DAB = 90┬░$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the diagonal BD.** In $\triangle ABD$ with $\angle DAB = 90┬░$: $$BD^2 = a^2 + d^2 - 2ad\cos(90┬░) = 50^2 + 100^2 - 0 = 2500 + 10000 = 12500$$ $$BD = \sqrt{12500} = 50\sqrt{5} \approx 111.80 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the third cognate structure.** In the third cognate: - $a_3 = a = 50$ mm (unchanged) - $b_3 = b = 120$ mm (unchanged) - $c_3 = c = 80$ mm (unchanged) - $d_3 = BD \approx 111.80$ mm (diagonal becomes new ground) **Step 3 ŌĆö Verify Grashof for the third cognate.** Sorted link lengths of third cognate: 50, 80, 111.80, 120. - $S = 50$, $L = 120$ - $S + L = 170$ - $P + Q = 80 + 111.80 = 191.80$ - Since $S + L < P + Q$ ŌåÆ **Grashof class** Ō£ō **Step 4 ŌĆö Determine the type.** Since $a_3 = 50$ mm is the **shortest** link and it is the input, this is a **crank-rocker** mechanism (input rotates fully, follower oscillates). | Parameter | Third Cognate Value | |---|---| | $a_3$ (input) | 50 mm | | $b_3$ (coupler) | 120 mm | | $c_3$ (output) | 80 mm | | $d_3$ (ground) | 111.80 mm | | Grashof class | **Crank-rocker** | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Loss Area:** Students often confuse the third cognate's ground with the original ground $d$. The *correct* ground of the third cognate is the **diagonal BD**, not the original frame. Forgetting this loses **2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cognitive Flash-Card for KTU 2024:** - **Definition:** Cognate linkages = different four-bars, **same coupler curve**. - **RobertsŌĆōChebyshev Theorem:** Every four-bar has exactly **3 cognates**. - **First Cognate:** Invert at pivot A ŌåÆ input link changes as $a_1^2 = a^2 + d^2 + 2ad\cos\alpha_A$. - **Second Cognate:** Invert at pivot D ŌåÆ output link changes as $c_2^2 = c^2 + d^2 + 2cd\cos\alpha_D$. - **Third Cognate:** Invert at coupler ŌåÆ ground becomes diagonal $BD$. - **Grashof Invariance:** Grashof condition is **preserved** across all cognates. - **Function Class Preservation:** Crank-rocker stays crank-rocker, etc. - **Coupler Preservation:** Length $b$ and trace offset $\rho$ are **rigid-body invariants**. - **Freudenstein Connection:** Path equivalence is proved via identical input-output angle functions. - **Diagonal BD:** A key geometric quantity that governs the cognate link lengths. - **Engineering Utility:** Packaging, textile, robotics, automotive ŌĆö anywhere path generation matters. - **KTU Pitfalls to Avoid:** 1. Don't confuse $a_1$ with $a$ ŌĆö they are different. 2. Don't say "any 4-bar with same Grashof is a cognate" ŌĆö Grashof is necessary, not sufficient. 3. The third cognate's ground is the **diagonal**, not the original frame. 4. Always sort link lengths before applying the Grashof inequality. 5. State the theorem name (RobertsŌĆōChebyshev) explicitly ŌĆö examiners award marks for naming. > **End of Module 3 Notes ŌĆö Cognate Linkages** | *PERAT522 ŌĆö Advanced Mechanism Design* | *KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Robert-Chebychev Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robert-Chebychev Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robert-Chebychev Theorem (Function Generation Context):**
> *"If a continuous function $y = f(x)$ is to be generated by a mechanism possessing **'n'** independent design parameters over a prescribed interval $[x_0, x_n]$, then the structural (approximation) error is **minimised in the Chebyshev (minimax) sense** ŌĆö that is, the maximum absolute deviation is minimised ŌĆö by selecting **'n+1'** precision points located at the extrema of the Chebyshev polynomial of degree **'n'**, at which the error oscillates with equal magnitude and alternating sign."*

In simpler academic phrasing taught in PERAT512/522 at KTU:

> *"For a mechanism with **n** degrees of freedom in its synthesis variables, the best function-generating approximation is obtained when the function-generation curve coincides with the desired function curve at **n+1** points whose abscissae are the zeros (or extrema) of the Chebyshev polynomial $T_n(u)$, and at these points the structural errors are equal in magnitude and alternate in sign."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **tailor stitching a straight zip onto a curved waist** (the function $f(x)$ is the curved waistline, the zip is the straight-line mechanism output).

* If you pin the zip at just **two points** (like in *two-position synthesis*), the zip fits perfectly at those two pins but may **bulge out** in the middle ŌĆö the *equal-spacing* problem.
* If you pin it at **three points**, you have one extra degree of freedom, and you can choose where to put the middle pin.
* **Robert-Chebychev** tells you: *"Don't put the middle pin in the geometric centre. Slide it towards the ends so that the maximum bulge is the same everywhere."* This is the **minimax** or **equi-ripple** criterion.

This is the **same philosophy** behind Chebyshev filters in Electrical Engineering and Chebyshev quadrature in Numerical Methods ŌĆö *minimise the worst-case error*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Precision Point** | An abscissa $x_k$ at which the generated function $F(x)$ exactly equals the desired function $f(x)$ |
| **Structural Error** | The deviation $e(x) = f(x) - F(x)$ that arises purely from the geometry of the mechanism, not from manufacturing tolerances |
| **Chebyshev Spacing** | Non-uniform placement of precision points, denser near interval ends, defined by zeros of $T_n(u)$ |
| **Minimax Criterion** | Minimisation of $\max \vert e(x) \vert$ over the interval |
| **Equioscillation** | Equal-amplitude alternating error curve at $n+1$ extremal points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The mathematical foundation was laid by **P. L. Chebyshev (1859)** in approximation theory, and the kinematic corollary for mechanism design was stated by **S. Roberts (1875)**. Together they constitute the *Robert-Chebychev theorem* taught in PERAT522 Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **Identify the synthesis type.** The theorem applies specifically to **function generation** (e.g., a four-bar linkage whose output angle $\phi$ must mathematically emulate an inputŌĆōoutput function $y = f(x)$).
2. **Count the design parameters $n$.** These are the *independent kinematic/geometric variables* available to the designer (link lengths, fixed pivot positions, offsets, etc.).
3. **Specify the input range.** Choose the working interval $[x_0, x_n]$ over which the function must be generated accurately.
4. **Map the interval to $[-1, +1]$.** Use the linear substitution
$$u = \frac{2x - (x_0 + x_n)}{x_n - x_0}$$
This converts the physical input range to the canonical domain of the Chebyshev polynomial.
5. **Locate the $n+1$ precision points.** Evaluate the abscissae
$$u_k = \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{n}\right), \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
and invert the map to obtain $x_k$.
6. **Set up the error equations.** At each precision point $x_k$, write $F(x_k, \phi_1, \ldots, \phi_n) = f(x_k)$ for one set of signs, and $F(x_k, \phi_1, \ldots, \phi_n) = f(x_k) \pm E$ alternately for the equioscillation condition.
7. **Solve the non-linear system.** Obtain the design parameters $\phi_1, \ldots, \phi_n$ and the minimised maximum error $E_{\max}$.
8. **Validate.** Plot $e(x) = f(x) - F(x)$ over $[x_0, x_n]$ and confirm **$n+1$** equiripple extrema of magnitude $E_{\max}$.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Chebyshev Spacing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just space the precision points equally?**
> When points are equally spaced, the approximation polynomial *oscillates most wildly* near the interval boundaries (Runge's phenomenon). Chebyshev nodes **cluster points near the ends**, which:
> * Suppresses boundary overshoot
> * Spreads the maximum error **uniformly** across the interval
> * Yields the **unique minimax** approximation

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are dimensionless** unless otherwise stated. Angles are in radians during derivation, converted to degrees for engineering use.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Boundary |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Chebyshev polynomial (1st kind) | $T_n(u) = \cos(n\arccos u)$ | Defined on $u \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 2 | Recurrence | $T_{n+1}(u) = 2u\,T_n(u) - T_{n-1}(u)$ | With $T_0 = 1,\ T_1 = u$ |
| 3 | Normalised abscissa | $u_k = \cos(k\pi/n)$ | $k = 0,1,\ldots,n$ |
| 4 | Interval mapping (forward) | $u = [2x - (x_0+x_n)]/(x_n-x_0)$ | Linear bijection |
| 5 | Interval mapping (inverse) | $x_k = \tfrac{1}{2}[(x_n-x_0)u_k + (x_n+x_0)]$ | Used to obtain $x_k$ |
| 6 | First few $T_n$ | $T_0=1,\ T_1=u,\ T_2=2u^2-1,\ T_3=4u^3-3u,\ T_4=8u^4-8u^2+1$ | Memorise these |
| 7 | Minimax error | $E_{\max} = \min\limits_{\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_n}\max\limits_{x\in[x_0,x_n]} \vert e(x) \vert$ | Definition |
| 8 | Equioscillation count | Number of alternations $= n+1$ | Hallmark of Chebyshev best fit |
| 9 | Total precision points | $p = n + 1$ | Matches design DOF + 1 |
| 10 | Structural error | $e(x) = f(x) - F(x,\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_n)$ | Independent of tolerances |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive suspensions:** Cam profiles designed so that lift-vs-angle mimics a target polynomial with minimum overshoot.
* **Robotic path planning:** Chebyshev-based interpolation of joint trajectories reduces actuator jerk.
* **Aerospace control surfaces:** Chebyshev-spaced precision points govern the design of compliant mechanisms for flap actuation.
* **CNC machining:** Function-generating cams for accurate tracing of mathematical curves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chebyshev Precision Point Locations

We begin with the trigonometric definition of the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind:

$$T_n(u) = \cos(n\theta), \qquad u = \cos\theta, \qquad \theta \in [0,\pi]$$

The extrema of $T_n(u)$ on $[-1,1]$ occur when $n\theta = k\pi$, i.e., when $\theta_k = k\pi/n$.

Therefore the abscissae of the $n+1$ extrema (counting $k=0$ and $k=n$) are

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_k &= \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{n}\right), \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n.
\end{aligned}
$$

At each of these abscissae the polynomial attains $T_n(u_k) = (-1)^{k}$, i.e., the values alternate $\pm 1$.

**Conversion to the physical input variable $x$:**

The interval $[x_0, x_n]$ is mapped linearly to $[-1, 1]$ by

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{x - x_{\text{mid}}}{x_{\text{half}}}, \qquad
x_{\text{mid}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_0 + x_n), \qquad
x_{\text{half}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_n - x_0).
\end{aligned}
$$

Inverting,

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k &= x_{\text{mid}} + x_{\text{half}}\,\cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{n}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

These $n+1$ values $\{x_k\}$ are the **Chebyshev precision points**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equioscillation Condition

Let the mechanism generate a function

$$F(x) = F\!\left(x; \phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_n\right)$$

where $\phi_1, \ldots, \phi_n$ are the $n$ independent design parameters. The structural error over the working interval is

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(x) = f(x) - F(x).
\end{aligned}
$$

We seek the values of $\{\phi_i\}$ that minimise

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\max} \;=\; \max_{x\in[x_0,x_n]} \bigl\vert\, e(x) \,\bigr\vert.
\end{aligned}
$$

According to **Chebyshev's equioscillation theorem** (alternation theorem), the unique minimiser is characterised by the existence of $n+2$ points $x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_{n+1}$ in $[x_0, x_n]$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(x_i) \;=\; (-1)^{i}\,E_{\max}, \qquad i = 0, 1, \ldots, n+1.
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the mechanism provides $n$ free parameters, exactly **$n+1$** of these points are *interior* precision points (where $e=0$), and the remaining extremal points lie at the **interval boundaries**. Combining these, the Robert-Chebychev theorem for mechanism synthesis states that the **$n+1$ interior precision points must coincide with the extrema of $T_n(u)$**.

## 3.3 Closed-Form Locations for $n=2, 3, 4$ (KTU Favourite Values)

For a four-bar linkage with **3** design parameters ($n=3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_0 &= \cos(0)        = +1 \\
u_1 &= \cos(\pi/3)    = +1/2 \\
u_2 &= \cos(2\pi/3)   = -1/2 \\
u_3 &= \cos(\pi)      = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence for input range $x \in [x_0, x_3]$, the Chebyshev precision points are

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0^{\text{Cheb}} &= x_{\text{mid}} + x_{\text{half}}(+1)  = x_3 \\
x_1^{\text{Cheb}} &= x_{\text{mid}} + x_{\text{half}}(+1/2) \\
x_2^{\text{Cheb}} &= x_{\text{mid}} + x_{\text{half}}(-1/2) \\
x_3^{\text{Cheb}} &= x_{\text{mid}} + x_{\text{half}}(-1) = x_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that **two of the precision points are forced to the interval endpoints** ŌĆö a hallmark of the Chebyshev spacing.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A slider-crank mechanism is to generate $y = \tan^{-1}(x)$ over $x \in [1, 4]$. The mechanism has $n=3$ design parameters. Determine the Chebyshev precision points.

**Solution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{mid}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1+4) = 2.5 \\
x_{\text{half}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(4-1) = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the $n=3$ Chebyshev abscissae from ┬¦3.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0^{\text{Cheb}} &= 2.5 + 1.5(+1)   &= 4.00 \\
x_1^{\text{Cheb}} &= 2.5 + 1.5(+0.5) &= 3.25 \\
x_2^{\text{Cheb}} &= 2.5 + 1.5(-0.5) &= 1.75 \\
x_3^{\text{Cheb}} &= 2.5 + 1.5(-1)   &= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

These four precision points are the abscissae at which the generated function must exactly equal $\tan^{-1}(x)$, and the structural error will equioscillate between them.

## 3.5 Comparison Table ŌĆö Equal vs Chebyshev Spacing

| Property | Equal Spacing (Burmester) | Chebyshev Spacing (Robert-Chebychev) |
|----------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Locations | $x_k = x_0 + k(x_n - x_0)/n$ | $x_k = x_{\text{mid}} + x_{\text{half}}\cos(k\pi/n)$ |
| Boundary clustering | None | Strong (points near ends) |
| Error distribution | Concentrated at boundaries | Uniform (equiripple) |
| Optimality | Sub-optimal in minimax sense | **Minimax optimal** |
| Error magnitude $E_{\max}$ | Larger | **Smaller (theoretically minimum)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Robert-Chebychev Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Target function y = f of x"] --> B["Define working interval x0 to xn"]
    B --> C["Count independent design parameters n"]
    C --> D["Map interval x0 xn to canonical domain minus 1 to plus 1"]
    D --> E["Compute Chebyshev abscissae u_k = cos of k pi over n"]
    E --> F["Map back to physical x_k precision points"]
    F --> G["Formulate n plus 1 structural error equations"]
    G --> H["Apply equioscillation condition e of x_k = plus minus E_max"]
    H --> I["Solve non linear system for design parameters phi 1 to phi n"]
    I --> J["Compute minimised maximum error E_max"]
    J --> K["OUTPUT: Optimal mechanism dimensions and E_max"]
    K --> L["Validation: plot e of x and verify n plus 1 alternations"]

    classDef stepBox fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#202124,stroke-width:1px;
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L stepBox;
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping of Precision Points on the Function Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["x0 left endpoint\nprecision point"] -->|"F(x0)=f(x0)"| N1["Desired curve f(x)"]
    N1 --> P2["Chebyshev point\nnear left end"]
    P2 -->|"F(x1)=f(x1)"| N2["error = +Emax"]
    N2 --> P3["Chebyshev midpoint"]
    P3 -->|"F(x2)=f(x2)"| N3["error = -Emax"]
    N3 --> P4["Chebyshev point\nnear right end"]
    P4 -->|"F(x3)=f(x3)"| N4["error = +Emax"]
    N4 --> P5["xn right endpoint\nprecision point"]

    classDef curveNode fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#202124;
    classDef pointNode fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#202124;
    class N1,N2,N3,N4 curveNode;
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 pointNode;
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram ŌĆö Equal vs Chebyshev Spacing on the Interval

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Equal Spacing (Burmester)"]
        E1["x0"] --- E2["x1"] --- E3["x2"] --- E4["x3"] --- E5["xn"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Chebyshev Spacing (Robert-Chebychev)"]
        C1["x0"] --- C2["x1 near right end"] --- C3["x2 near left end"] --- C4["xn"]
    end

    classDef equalStyle fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#202124;
    classDef chebStyle fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#202124;
    class E1,E2,E3,E4,E5 equalStyle;
    class C1,C2,C3,C4 chebStyle;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this carefully:** In Chebyshev spacing, the interior precision points are *pushed* away from the centre and *cluster* near the boundaries. This redistribution is the geometric reason why the maximum error magnitude is *uniformly* spread across the interval.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 3]
**State the Robert-Chebychev theorem as applied to function-generating mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

> The Robert-Chebychev theorem states that when a function $y = f(x)$ is to be generated by a mechanism having **$n$** independent design parameters over an interval $[x_0, x_n]$, the structural error is **minimised in the Chebyshev (minimax) sense** by selecting $n+1$ precision points located at the abscissae of the extrema of the Chebyshev polynomial $T_n(u)$ of degree $n$, where the error function alternates sign with equal maximum magnitude $E_{\max}$. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 3]
**List the first four Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind and write the general formula for the precision-point abscissae.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0(u) &= 1 \\
T_1(u) &= u \\
T_2(u) &= 2u^2 - 1 \\
T_3(u) &= 4u^3 - 3u
\end{aligned}
$$

The precision-point abscissae are given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(x_n - x_0)\cos(k\pi/n) + (x_n + x_0)\bigr], \qquad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n. \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the Robert-Chebychev theorem and its significance in the synthesis of function-generating mechanisms. Discuss why Chebyshev spacing of precision points is preferred over equal spacing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Statement of the theorem (2 marks):** As given in ┬¦1.1 ŌĆö for $n$ design parameters, choose $n+1$ precision points at the extrema of $T_n(u)$ such that the error alternates sign with equal magnitude.

2. **Chebyshev spacing derivation (3 marks):** Show that $u_k = \cos(k\pi/n)$ and the inverse map to $x_k$ as derived in ┬¦3.1.

3. **Comparison with equal spacing (2 marks):**
   * Equal spacing leaves the boundary error larger (Runge phenomenon analogue).
   * Chebyshev spacing clusters points near boundaries, yielding uniform error distribution.
   * Chebyshev spacing guarantees the **unique** minimax-optimal approximation.

**(b)** A four-bar mechanism with **3** independent design parameters is required to generate the function $y = x^{1/2}$ over the interval $x \in [4, 9]$. Determine the Chebyshev precision points and the corresponding desired output values. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute interval parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{mid}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(4 + 9) = 6.5 \\
x_{\text{half}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(9 - 4) = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Chebyshev abscissae for $n = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_0 &= +1,\quad u_1 = +\tfrac{1}{2},\quad u_2 = -\tfrac{1}{2},\quad u_3 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Precision points in $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0 &= 6.5 + 2.5(+1)   &= 9.00 \\
x_1 &= 6.5 + 2.5(+0.5) &= 7.75 \\
x_2 &= 6.5 + 2.5(-0.5) &= 5.25 \\
x_3 &= 6.5 + 2.5(-1)   &= 4.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Corresponding target function values $y = \sqrt{x}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_0 &= \sqrt{9.00} = 3.0000 \\
y_1 &= \sqrt{7.75} \approx 2.7839 \\
y_2 &= \sqrt{5.25} \approx 2.2913 \\
y_3 &= \sqrt{4.00} = 2.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final answer tabulated (1 mark):**

| $k$ | $x_k$ | $u_k$ | $y_k = \sqrt{x_k}$ |
|-----|--------|--------|--------------------|
| 0 | 9.00 | +1 | 3.0000 |
| 1 | 7.75 | +1/2 | 2.7839 |
| 2 | 5.25 | ŌłÆ1/2 | 2.2913 |
| 3 | 4.00 | ŌłÆ1 | 2.0000 |

These four points constitute the precision points of the function generator, and the three design parameters of the four-bar are solved by enforcing $F(x_k) = y_k$ together with the equioscillation condition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive the locations of Chebyshev precision points for a mechanism synthesis problem with $n = 4$ design parameters working over the interval $[x_0, x_n]$. Express your final answer in terms of $x_0$ and $x_n$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Chebyshev polynomial of degree 4 (1 mark):**

$$T_4(u) = 8u^4 - 8u^2 + 1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Locations of extrema (2 marks):** From $T_4'(u) = 0$ and $\cos(4\theta) = \cos(4\theta)$ extrema, we get

$$u_k = \cos(k\pi/4), \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$

Explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_0 &= \cos(0)       = +1 \\
u_1 &= \cos(\pi/4)   = +\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx +0.7071 \\
u_2 &= \cos(\pi/2)   = 0 \\
u_3 &= \cos(3\pi/4)  = -\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx -0.7071 \\
u_4 &= \cos(\pi)     = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Map back to $x$-domain (3 marks):** With $x_{\text{mid}} = (x_0 + x_n)/2$ and $x_{\text{half}} = (x_n - x_0)/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0^{\text{Cheb}} &= x_n \\
x_1^{\text{Cheb}} &= \tfrac{x_0 + x_n}{2} + \tfrac{x_n - x_0}{2}\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \\
x_2^{\text{Cheb}} &= \tfrac{x_0 + x_n}{2} \\
x_3^{\text{Cheb}} &= \tfrac{x_0 + x_n}{2} - \tfrac{x_n - x_0}{2}\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \\
x_4^{\text{Cheb}} &= x_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final compact formula (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k \;=\; \frac{(x_n + x_0)}{2} \;+\; \frac{(x_n - x_0)}{2}\,\cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{4}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** With a suitable example, compare **Chebyshev spacing** of precision points with **equal spacing**. State clearly which approach gives the smaller maximum structural error and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Numerical example (3 marks):** Consider $f(x) = \sin(x)$ on $[0, \pi/2]$ with $n = 3$.

* **Equal spacing:** $x_k = 0, \pi/6, \pi/3, \pi/2$.
* **Chebyshev spacing:** $x_k = \pi/2,\, \pi/2 + (\pi/4)(0.5),\, \pi/2 - (\pi/4)(0.5),\, 0$
  $= \pi/2,\, 5\pi/8,\, 3\pi/8,\, 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Approximation error analysis (2 marks):** Using a cubic approximating polynomial fitted to each set:

| Spacing | Maximum $\vert e(x) \vert$ on $[0,\pi/2]$ |
|---------|-------------------------------------------|
| Equal | $\approx 0.0062$ |
| Chebyshev | $\approx 0.0014$ (Ōēł4├Ś smaller) |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conclusion (2 marks):** Chebyshev spacing yields a smaller maximum structural error because:
* It places more precision points near the boundaries where the Runge-type oscillation of polynomial approximation is largest.
* It enforces the **equioscillation theorem**, which is the necessary and sufficient condition for minimax optimality.
* The error magnitude is uniform across the interval ŌĆö *no single region dominates the worst case*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **Chebyshev spacing** with **equal spacing** ŌĆö they are *not* the same. Equal spacing gives $x_k = x_0 + k\Delta x$ with constant $\Delta x$; Chebyshev spacing gives $x_k$ that *cluster near the ends*. **Mention this explicitly** in your answer.
> 2. Do **not** confuse the theorem (Robert-Chebychev, about function generation) with **Freudenstein's equation** (used for *path generation*). Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. Always state the **number of precision points = $n+1$**, where $n$ is the number of independent design parameters. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. When asked to "state" the theorem, students often only give the **error-minimisation** part. They forget to mention the **alternating-sign equal-magnitude** condition. Both are essential.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorise Before Exam)**

- [ ] Robert-Chebychev theorem = **minimax-optimal function synthesis**.
- [ ] If mechanism has **$n$** design parameters, choose **$n+1$** precision points.
- [ ] Precision-point abscissae: $u_k = \cos(k\pi/n)$, mapped back to $x$ via linear transformation.
- [ ] Chebyshev spacing **clusters points near interval boundaries**.
- [ ] Equal spacing clusters points near the **centre** ŌåÆ larger boundary error.
- [ ] Chebyshev spacing guarantees **equioscillation**: error alternates $\pm E_{\max}$ at $n+1$ points.
- [ ] First four $T_n(u)$: $1,\ u,\ 2u^2-1,\ 4u^3-3u$.
- [ ] Recurrence: $T_{n+1}(u) = 2u\,T_n(u) - T_{n-1}(u)$.
- [ ] Mapping formulas (forward and inverse) are **mandatory derivations** in KTU exams.
- [ ] Theorem is for **function generation** only ŌĆö not path generation or rigid-body guidance.
- [ ] The equioscillation theorem (alternation theorem) is the **mathematical foundation**.
- [ ] The minimax error $E_{\max}$ is **uniquely defined** for a given $n$ and interval.
- [ ] Always check the unit consistency ŌĆö angles in radians during derivation, degrees in final mechanism dimensions.
- [ ] When asked for **precision points**, tabulate both $x_k$ and $y_k = f(x_k)$ in your final answer.
- [ ] Draw the **error curve** $e(x)$ in graphical problems ŌĆö examiners reward visualisation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block's Method of Synthesis, Freudenstein's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT522 - ADVANCED MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Block's Method of Synthesis, Freudenstein's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Block's Method of Synthesis ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Block's Method** is a graphical-analytical technique used in the **synthesis of planar linkages** (typically four-bar linkages) for the **function generation, path generation, and rigid body guidance (body guidance)** problems. Developed by Arthur Block, the method uses the concept of **relative poles** (also called **instantaneous centers of rotation in successive positions**) between the successive positions of a moving body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Block's method is essentially a **geometric construction technique** based on the **relative displacement theorem** (Burmester's theory). It deals with the determination of fixed and moving pivots for a dyad (two-force member) to satisfy a finite set of prescribed positions.

The method is built on the principle that the **relative motion between two positions of a rigid body** is equivalent to a **pure rotation about a point** ŌĆö the **relative pole**. By connecting the relative poles of successive positions, the method directly identifies feasible **fixed pivot locations** for a dyad member.

## 1.2 Freudenstein's Equation ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Freudenstein's Equation** is the most celebrated **exact analytical equation** in four-bar linkage synthesis and analysis. It is derived from the **vector loop-closure equation** of a planar four-bar mechanism and relates the **input crank angle** ($\theta_2$) to the **output coupler angle** ($\theta_4$ or $\theta_3$ depending on convention) through the link length ratios.

The standard form is:

$$K_1 \cos\theta_2 - K_2 \cos\theta_3 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_2)$$

where $K_1$, $K_2$, and $K_3$ are **dimensionless constants** that depend only on the fixed link lengths ($L_1$, $L_2$, $L_3$, $L_4$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout ŌĆö Loop-Closure Concept:**
> The loop-closure equation is the scalar condition that must be satisfied by any closed kinematic chain. For a four-bar linkage, it arises from the vector polygon $\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 = \vec{r}_3 + \vec{r}_4$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### ­¤Ä» Analogy for Block's Method

Imagine a **car door opening**. The door must move through a sequence of three specific positions (slightly closed, half open, fully open) without colliding with the car body. If you are an engineer designing the hinges, you need to find **where to place the hinge pins** so the door traces the required path. **Block's Method** is precisely this: given the three positions of the door (the moving body), it geometrically finds the **fixed hinge locations** that will produce the prescribed motion. The construction uses **pivot points, circle points, and center points** to systematically locate the pivots.

### ­¤Ä» Analogy for Freudenstein's Equation

Think of a **windshield wiper** mechanism. As the motor rotates the input link by an angle $\theta_2$, the wiper (output) must sweep to an angle $\theta_4$ such that the wiper blade clears a specific region of the windshield. The wiper's motion is **not linear** with respect to the motor's motion ŌĆö it follows a precise **trigonometric relation**. **Freudenstein's Equation** is the mathematical "decoder" that expresses this non-linear input-output angular relationship for any four-bar linkage, using only the four link lengths. It is the **signature equation** of the linkage ŌĆö every four-bar mechanism has its own unique Freudenstein constants.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Notation | Unit |
|-----------|--------|------------------|------|
| Fixed (ground) link length | $L_1$ | $a$ or $r_1$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Input crank length | $L_2$ | $b$ or $r_2$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Coupler link length | $L_3$ | $c$ or $r_3$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Output (rocker) link length | $L_4$ | $d$ or $r_4$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Input crank angle | $\theta_2$ | $\phi_1$ | **radians** or **degrees** |
| Output rocker angle | $\theta_4$ | $\phi_3$ | **radians** or **degrees** |
| Coupler angle | $\theta_3$ | $\phi_2$ | **radians** or **degrees** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dimensionless ratios $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$ in Freudenstein's Equation are independent of the **absolute scale** of the linkage. This means a 100 mm and a 200 mm scaled version of the same linkage will share the same $K$-constants ŌĆö only the **proportions** matter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Principles of Block's Method

### 2.1.1 Relative Pole (Burmester Pivot Point)

When a rigid body moves between two positions, the **relative displacement** is a **pure rotation about a unique point** lying on the perpendicular bisectors of the displacement vectors of every point on the body. This point is the **relative pole** $P_{ij}$ between positions $i$ and $j$.

- For **Position 1 ŌåÆ 2**: Pole $P_{12}$
- For **Position 2 ŌåÆ 3**: Pole $P_{23}$
- For **Position 1 ŌåÆ 3**: Pole $P_{13}$

### 2.1.2 Circle Points and Center Points

**Circle Points (CP):** The set of all points on the moving plane that lie on a circle with its center at the **fixed pivot** location. They are obtained by intersecting the moving plane positions with appropriate construction arcs.

**Center Points (CE):** The set of all points that, if taken as the **moving pivot**, will result in the **fixed pivot** being on a circle of constant radius (i.e., the moving pivot traces a circle).

### 2.1.3 The Three-Position Dyad Synthesis (Block's Method)

A **dyad** is a two-link sub-chain (e.g., the crank-coupler pair or the rocker-coupler pair). For a four-bar linkage, the synthesis requires two dyads:
- **Dyad 1**: Fixed pivot $O_2$ (on ground) + moving pivot $A$ (on coupler)
- **Dyad 2**: Fixed pivot $O_4$ (on ground) + moving pivot $B$ (on coupler)

**Block's Three-Position Procedure:**

1. Plot the three prescribed positions of the moving body (e.g., the coupler plane).
2. Identify the **relative poles** $P_{12}$, $P_{23}$, $P_{13}$ using perpendicular bisectors.
3. The **fixed pivot** of the input dyad is found at the **intersection of lines $O_2P_{12}$ and $O_2P_{23}$** ŌĆö but in practice, a specific construction is used: the **fixed pivot lies on the circle-point pole ray** (the line connecting the two relative poles).
4. The **moving pivot** is found at the corresponding center point.
5. Repeat for the second dyad to complete the four-bar linkage.

### 2.1.4 Key Geometric Theorems Used

- **Displacement Theorem**: Finite displacement of a rigid body in a plane is equivalent to a rotation about a fixed point.
- **Euler-Savary Equation**: Describes curvature of paths in plane motion.
- **Kreuz-Linckhage Construction**: Classical tool for Burmester curve generation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** for Block's Method and Freudenstein's Equation that are commonly tested in KTU University Examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Vector Loop-Closure (Four-Bar) | $\vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_1 = 0$ | Closed-chain condition |
| 2 | Loop-Closure (X-component) | $L_2 \cos\theta_2 - L_3 \cos\theta_3 - L_4 \cos\theta_4 + L_1 = 0$ | Sum of horizontal components |
| 3 | Loop-Closure (Y-component) | $L_2 \sin\theta_2 - L_3 \sin\theta_3 - L_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$ | Sum of vertical components |
| 4 | Freudenstein's Equation (Standard Form) | $K_1 \cos\theta_2 - K_2 \cos\theta_3 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_2)$ | Most-used form |
| 5 | Freudenstein Constant $K_1$ | $K_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | Length ratio (ground/input) |
| 6 | Freudenstein Constant $K_2$ | $K_2 = \dfrac{L_1}{L_4}$ | Length ratio (ground/output) |
| 7 | Freudenstein Constant $K_3$ | $K_3 = \dfrac{L_1^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2 + L_4^2}{2 L_2 L_4}$ | Geometric combination |
| 8 | Relative Pole Location (Two-Position) | $P_{12}$ lies on perpendicular bisector of displacement vector | Geometric construction |
| 9 | Chebyshev Spacing (Function Generation) | $\theta_i = \dfrac{(i-1)}{n-1}(\theta_{final} - \theta_{initial}) + \theta_{initial}$ | Equal-spaced input angles |
| 10 | Branch Defect (Freudenstein) | $\Delta F = F(\theta_2 + \pi) - F(\theta_2)$ | Detects assembly mode switch |
| 11 | Coupler Angle Relation | $\tan\theta_3 = \dfrac{L_2 \sin\theta_2}{L_2 \cos\theta_2 - L_1}$ | Derived from loop equations |
| 12 | Grashof Condition | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Existence of crank (S=shortest, L=longest, P,Q=other two) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip ŌĆö Sign Convention for Freudenstein's Equation:**
> The angles $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$ (or $\theta_4$) are measured from a common reference axis (usually the positive X-axis, i.e., $L_1$). Counter-clockwise is conventionally **positive**. Failure to maintain sign convention is a leading cause of mark loss in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

### Block's Method Applications
- **Automotive Suspension Design**: Synthesizing four-bar linkages for controlled wheel motion.
- **Walking Robot Legs**: Designing planar leg mechanisms that move the foot through prescribed positions.
- **Earth-Moving Equipment**: Bucket linkage synthesis for excavators and backhoes.
- **Aircraft Landing Gear**: Retraction mechanisms with prescribed path requirements.
- **Industrial Pick-and-Place Robots**: Three-position synthesis of transfer mechanisms.

### Freudenstein's Equation Applications
- **Function Generators**: Steering linkages, pump governors, computing mechanisms.
- **Quick Synthesis Tool**: Given three precision points (input-output angle pairs), the equation is solved for the three unknowns $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$, which then yield the four link lengths (up to scale).
- **Chebyshev Approximation**: For optimizing function generation error across a range of input angles.
- **Belt Drives and Walking Mechanisms**: As a basis for coupler-curve analysis.
- **Biomechanics**: Gait analysis of human joints modeled as four-bar linkages.
- **Servo-Mechanism Design**: Designing linkages that convert uniform motor rotation into a non-linear output function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> Freudenstein's Equation (1954) revolutionized linkage synthesis by **eliminating trial-and-error graphical methods**. It remains the **industry standard** for the synthesis of function-generating four-bar mechanisms, particularly in automotive steering systems and farm machinery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Freudenstein's Equation

The derivation proceeds from the **vector loop-closure equation** of a planar four-bar linkage. We adopt the following standard notation (consistent with Hartenberg-Denavit):

- $L_1$ = Ground link (between fixed pivots $O_2$ and $O_4$)
- $L_2$ = Input crank (between $O_2$ and $A$)
- $L_3$ = Coupler (between $A$ and $B$)
- $L_4$ = Output rocker (between $O_4$ and $B$)
- $\theta_2$ = Angle of $L_2$ from positive X-axis
- $\theta_3$ = Angle of $L_3$ from positive X-axis
- $\theta_4$ = Angle of $L_4$ from positive X-axis

### Step 1: Vector Loop-Closure Equation

Starting from the closed vector polygon of the four-bar linkage, traversing clockwise from $O_2$:

$$\vec{L}_1 - \vec{L}_4 - \vec{L}_3 + \vec{L}_2 = 0$$

Or equivalently (rearranging):

$$\vec{L}_2 + \vec{L}_3 = \vec{L}_1 + \vec{L}_4$$

> **Logic:** A closed kinematic loop requires the sum of all link vectors to return to the starting point. Choosing the appropriate traversal direction gives the sign convention.

### Step 2: Resolving into X and Y Components

Expressing each link vector in component form:

$$L_2 \cos\theta_2 \hat{i} + L_2 \sin\theta_2 \hat{j} + L_3 \cos\theta_3 \hat{i} + L_3 \sin\theta_3 \hat{j} = L_1 \hat{i} + L_4 \cos\theta_4 \hat{i} + L_4 \sin\theta_4 \hat{j}$$

> **Logic:** Every planar vector $\vec{L}_k$ can be resolved into its X-component ($L_k \cos\theta_k$) and Y-component ($L_k \sin\theta_k$) where $\theta_k$ is the angle from the positive X-axis.

Separating the X-components:

$$L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3 = L_1 + L_4 \cos\theta_4 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

Separating the Y-components:

$$L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin\theta_3 = L_4 \sin\theta_4 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

### Step 3: Solving the Loop-closure Equations for $\theta_4$

From Equation (I), solving for $\cos\theta_4$:

$$\cos\theta_4 = \dfrac{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3}{L_4}$$

From Equation (II), solving for $\sin\theta_4$:

$$\sin\theta_4 = \dfrac{L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin\theta_3}{L_4}$$

> **Logic:** We isolate the trigonometric functions of $\theta_4$ because $\theta_4$ is the output angle we wish to relate to the input $\theta_2$ (with the link geometry as a parameter).

### Step 4: Applying the Pythagorean Identity

Since $\cos^2\theta_4 + \sin^2\theta_4 = 1$, we can write:

$$\left(\dfrac{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3}{L_4}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin\theta_3}{L_4}\right)^2 = 1$$

Multiplying through by $L_4^2$:

$$(L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 + (L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin\theta_3)^2 = L_4^2$$

> **Logic:** The Pythagorean identity is a fundamental constraint ŌĆö any point on the unit circle satisfies this. The output link tip $B$ must lie on a circle of radius $L_4$ centered at $O_4$.

### Step 5: Expanding the Squared Terms

Expanding the first squared term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3)^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 \cos^2\theta_2 + L_3^2 \cos^2\theta_3 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 + 2 L_1 L_3 \cos\theta_3 + 2 L_2 L_3 \cos\theta_2 \cos\theta_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the second squared term:

$$
(L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin\theta_3)^2 = L_2^2 \sin^2\theta_2 + L_3^2 \sin^2\theta_3 + 2 L_2 L_3 \sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_3
$$

### Step 6: Summing the Expanded Terms

Adding the two expansions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1^2 + L_2^2(\cos^2\theta_2 + \sin^2\theta_2) + L_3^2(\cos^2\theta_3 + \sin^2\theta_3) + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 + 2 L_1 L_3 \cos\theta_3 + 2 L_2 L_3 (\cos\theta_2 \cos\theta_3 + \sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_3) = L_4^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Applying Trigonometric Identities

Using $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ and $\cos\theta_2 \cos\theta_3 + \sin\theta_2 \sin\theta_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$:

$$L_1^2 + L_2^2 + L_3^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 + 2 L_1 L_3 \cos\theta_3 + 2 L_2 L_3 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) = L_4^2$$

> **Logic:** The cosine subtraction identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$ is the key simplification that emerges from the product-to-sum transformation.

### Step 8: Isolating the Cosine of the Difference

Rearranging to isolate $2 L_2 L_3 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$:

$$2 L_2 L_3 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) = L_4^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2 - 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 - 2 L_1 L_3 \cos\theta_3$$

### Step 9: Dividing Through to Non-dimensionalize

Dividing both sides by $2 L_2 L_3$:

$$\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) = \dfrac{L_4^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2}{2 L_2 L_3} - \dfrac{L_1 \cos\theta_2}{L_3} - \dfrac{L_1 \cos\theta_3}{L_2}$$

> **Logic:** The objective is to make the equation **dimensionless** and to put it in a form where the input angle and output angle are clearly separated.

### Step 10: Final Form ŌĆö Freudenstein's Equation

Reorganizing with the standard definitions $K_1 = L_1/L_2$, $K_2 = L_1/L_3$ (note: in some textbooks, $K_1 = L_1/L_2$ and $K_2 = L_1/L_4$ depending on which angle is being solved for), and using $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) = \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_2)$ (since cosine is even):

$$\boxed{K_1 \cos\theta_2 - K_2 \cos\theta_3 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}$$

where the constants are:

$$K_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}, \quad K_2 = \dfrac{L_1}{L_4}, \quad K_3 = \dfrac{L_1^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2 + L_4^2}{2 L_2 L_4}$$

> **Logic (Final Interpretation):** The three K-constants fully characterize a four-bar linkage's input-output relationship. Given any three input-output pairs $(\theta_2, \theta_3)$ ŌĆö known as **precision points** ŌĆö the three K's can be uniquely solved. The link lengths can then be back-computed, completing the synthesis.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Block's Method ŌĆö Three-Position Dyad Synthesis

### Step 1: Position the Three Positions of the Moving Body

Let the three prescribed positions of the moving plane (e.g., coupler) be represented by three congruent triangles placed on the drawing board. Label them **Position 1**, **Position 2**, and **Position 3**.

### Step 2: Construct the Relative Poles

For two arbitrary points $M_1$ and $M_2$ on the moving body, plot their positions $M_1^{(1)}, M_1^{(2)}, M_1^{(3)}$ in each of the three positions.

- Construct the **perpendicular bisector** of segment $M_1^{(1)} M_1^{(2)}$.
- Construct the **perpendicular bisector** of segment $M_1^{(1)} M_1^{(3)}$.
- Their **intersection** is the **relative pole $P_{12}$** (for displacement 1ŌåÆ2).
- Similarly, **$P_{23}$** is the intersection of perpendicular bisectors of $M_1^{(2)} M_1^{(3)}$ and $M_2^{(2)} M_2^{(3)}$.

> **Logic:** The relative pole is the unique point in the fixed plane about which the rigid body rotates between the two positions. The perpendicular bisector property stems from the fact that all points on the moving body are equidistant from the pole in each position.

### Step 3: Construct the Circle-Point Pole Ray

The line $P_{12} P_{23}$ is the **circle-point pole ray**. The **fixed pivot** of the input dyad must lie on this line for the dyad to satisfy the three prescribed positions.

### Step 4: Locate the Fixed Pivot

Choose a point on the circle-point pole ray as the **fixed pivot $O_2$** (often chosen for practical reasons like ground clearance).

### Step 5: Locate the Moving Pivot (Center Point)

The moving pivot $A$ is found by constructing the **center point** of the dyad:
- The center point lies on the **perpendicular** from $O_2$ to the circle-point pole ray.
- The distance from the center point to the pole ray is determined by the specific geometry of the prescribed positions.

### Step 6: Verify with All Three Positions

Once the fixed and moving pivots are found, verify that the distances $O_2 A^{(1)}$, $O_2 A^{(2)}$, $O_2 A^{(3)}$ are **equal** (since $L_2$ is a rigid link). If not, the construction is invalid and the pivot choice must be revised.

### Step 7: Repeat for the Second Dyad

A second dyad with fixed pivot $O_4$ and moving pivot $B$ is similarly constructed on the opposite side of the coupler, completing the four-bar linkage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:**
> The graphical construction of Block's method requires careful use of **drafting tools**. A typical KTU question will provide the three positions of the moving plane and ask the student to identify the locations of the fixed and moving pivots. Marks are allocated for: (1) correct pole construction, (2) correct identification of the pole ray, and (3) correct verification of link-length invariance.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Freudenstein's Equation

**Problem:** A four-bar linkage is to be designed to generate the function $y = f(x)$ at three precision points given in the table below. The input crank angle $\theta_2$ and the output angle $\theta_4$ are related by the desired function. The three prescribed input-output pairs are:

| Precision Point | $\theta_2$ (degrees) | $\theta_4$ (degrees) |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 30┬░ | 60┬░ |
| 2 | 90┬░ | 90┬░ |
| 3 | 150┬░ | 120┬░ |

Use Freudenstein's Equation to determine the constants $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$.

> **Logic (Setup):** We use the alternate form of Freudenstein's equation that is directly solvable for three unknowns from three precision points. The form is:
>
> $$K_1 \cos\theta_2 - K_2 \cos\theta_4 - K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$
>
> This is a linear system in $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$.

### Solution ŌĆö Step 1: Compute Trigonometric Values

For Point 1 ($\theta_2 = 30┬░$, $\theta_4 = 60┬░$):

$$\cos\theta_2 = \cos 30┬░ = 0.8660, \quad \cos\theta_4 = \cos 60┬░ = 0.5000$$

$$\theta_2 - \theta_4 = -30┬░ \Rightarrow \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos(-30┬░) = 0.8660$$

For Point 2 ($\theta_2 = 90┬░$, $\theta_4 = 90┬░$):

$$\cos\theta_2 = \cos 90┬░ = 0.0000, \quad \cos\theta_4 = \cos 90┬░ = 0.0000$$

$$\theta_2 - \theta_4 = 0┬░ \Rightarrow \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = 1.0000$$

For Point 3 ($\theta_2 = 150┬░$, $\theta_4 = 120┬░$):

$$\cos\theta_2 = \cos 150┬░ = -0.8660, \quad \cos\theta_4 = \cos 120┬░ = -0.5000$$

$$\theta_2 - \theta_4 = 30┬░ \Rightarrow \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = 0.8660$$

### Solution ŌĆö Step 2: Formulate the Linear System

Substituting each precision point into the equation $K_1 \cos\theta_2 - K_2 \cos\theta_4 - K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$:

**Point 1:** $\quad 0.8660 K_1 - 0.5000 K_2 - K_3 = 0.8660 \quad \text{...(i)}$

**Point 2:** $\quad 0.0000 K_1 - 0.0000 K_2 - K_3 = 1.0000 \quad \text{...(ii)}$

**Point 3:** $\quad -0.8660 K_1 + 0.5000 K_2 - K_3 = 0.8660 \quad \text{...(iii)}$

### Solution ŌĆö Step 3: Solve the System

From (ii):

$$K_3 = -1.0000$$

Substituting $K_3 = -1.0000$ into (i):

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.5000 K_2 + 1.0000 = 0.8660$$

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.5000 K_2 = -0.1340 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Substituting $K_3 = -1.0000$ into (iii):

$$-0.8660 K_1 + 0.5000 K_2 + 1.0000 = 0.8660$$

$$-0.8660 K_1 + 0.5000 K_2 = -0.1340 \quad \text{...(v)}$$

Adding (iv) and (v):

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.5000 K_2 - 0.8660 K_1 + 0.5000 K_2 = -0.1340 + (-0.1340)$$

$$0 = -0.2680 \quad \text{(Contradiction!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning ŌĆö Indeterminate System:**
> The system has **no unique solution** because the three precision points are **linearly dependent** in their trigonometric content. This is a common pitfall ŌĆö when designing for function generation, the **choice of precision points** is critical. The student must verify that the three points yield a **non-singular matrix** (determinant ŌēĀ 0) before attempting to solve. Choosing the three points symmetrically (as in this case) often leads to a singular system. **A better choice** would be points like $\theta_2 = 30┬░, 90┬░, 150┬░$ paired with $\theta_4 = 45┬░, 90┬░, 135┬░$ to break the symmetry.

### Solution ŌĆö Step 4: Re-solve with Modified Precision Points

Use the alternative precision points:

| Precision Point | $\theta_2$ (degrees) | $\theta_4$ (degrees) |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 30┬░ | 45┬░ |
| 2 | 90┬░ | 90┬░ |
| 3 | 150┬░ | 135┬░ |

For Point 1: $\cos 30┬░ = 0.8660$, $\cos 45┬░ = 0.7071$, $\cos(30┬░ - 45┬░) = \cos(-15┬░) = 0.9659$.

For Point 2: $\cos 90┬░ = 0$, $\cos 90┬░ = 0$, $\cos 0┬░ = 1$.

For Point 3: $\cos 150┬░ = -0.8660$, $\cos 135┬░ = -0.7071$, $\cos 15┬░ = 0.9659$.

System of equations:

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.7071 K_2 - K_3 = 0.9659 \quad \text{...(i')}$$

$$0 K_1 - 0 K_2 - K_3 = 1 \Rightarrow K_3 = -1 \quad \text{...(ii')}$$

$$-0.8660 K_1 + 0.7071 K_2 - K_3 = 0.9659 \quad \text{...(iii')}$$

Substituting $K_3 = -1$:

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.7071 K_2 = -0.0341 \quad \text{...(iv')}$$

$$-0.8660 K_1 + 0.7071 K_2 = 0.9659 - (-1) = ... $$

> **Recompute (iii'):** $-0.8660 K_1 + 0.7071 K_2 + 1 = 0.9659$ ŌåÆ $-0.8660 K_1 + 0.7071 K_2 = -0.0341$ (v')

> **Logic:** With (iv') + (v'): $0 = -0.0682$ ŌĆö still singular! The issue is **structural** because we used Chebyshev-style symmetric input spacing with symmetric output.

### Solution ŌĆö Step 5: Use Asymmetric Precision Points (Final Working Example)

| Precision Point | $\theta_2$ (degrees) | $\theta_4$ (degrees) |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 30┬░ | 40┬░ |
| 2 | 90┬░ | 95┬░ |
| 3 | 150┬░ | 145┬░ |

For Point 1: $\cos 30┬░ = 0.8660$, $\cos 40┬░ = 0.7660$, $\cos(-10┬░) = 0.9848$.

For Point 2: $\cos 90┬░ = 0$, $\cos 95┬░ = -0.0872$, $\cos(-5┬░) = 0.9962$.

For Point 3: $\cos 150┬░ = -0.8660$, $\cos 145┬░ = -0.8192$, $\cos 5┬░ = 0.9962$.

System:

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.7660 K_2 - K_3 = 0.9848 \quad \text{...(A)}$$

$$0 K_1 - (-0.0872) K_2 - K_3 = 0.9962 \Rightarrow 0.0872 K_2 - K_3 = 0.9962 \quad \text{...(B)}$$

$$-0.8660 K_1 - (-0.8192) K_2 - K_3 = 0.9962 \Rightarrow -0.8660 K_1 + 0.8192 K_2 - K_3 = 0.9962 \quad \text{...(C)}$$

> **Logic:** This system is no longer singular.

From (B): $K_3 = 0.0872 K_2 - 0.9962$

Substitute into (A):

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.7660 K_2 - 0.0872 K_2 + 0.9962 = 0.9848$$

$$0.8660 K_1 - 0.8532 K_2 = -0.0114 \quad \text{...(D)}$$

Substitute into (C):

$$-0.8660 K_1 + 0.8192 K_2 - 0.0872 K_2 + 0.9962 = 0.9962$$

$$-0.8660 K_1 + 0.7320 K_2 = 0 \Rightarrow K_1 = \dfrac{0.7320}{0.8660} K_2 = 0.8453 K_2 \quad \text{...(E)}$$

Substituting (E) into (D):

$$0.8660 (0.8453 K_2) - 0.8532 K_2 = -0.0114$$

$$0.7320 K_2 - 0.8532 K_2 = -0.0114$$

$$-0.1212 K_2 = -0.0114$$

$$K_2 = 0.0941$$

Back-substituting:

$$K_1 = 0.8453 \times 0.0941 = 0.0795$$

$$K_3 = 0.0872 \times 0.0941 - 0.9962 = 0.0082 - 0.9962 = -0.9880$$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$K_1 = 0.0795, \quad K_2 = 0.0941, \quad K_3 = -0.9880$$
>
> **Link Length Verification (Optional):**
> With $K_1 = L_1/L_2 = 0.0795$ and $K_2 = L_1/L_4 = 0.0941$, we can choose $L_1 = 100$ mm, giving $L_2 = 1257.9$ mm and $L_4 = 1062.7$ mm. The value of $K_3$ is then used to back-calculate $L_3$, which completes the design.

## 4.4 Functional Flowchart ŌĆö Freudenstein-Based Function Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    D --> E{Singular Matrix?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Reselect Precision Points to Break Symmetry]
    F --> C

    I --> J[Compute L4 from K2]

    L --> M{Valid Four-Bar?}

    M -->|Yes| N[Finalize Linkage Design]
```

> **Mermaid Diagram Note:**
> These diagrams represent the **functional flow** of the synthesis procedures. For Block's Method, the diagram tracks the **graphical construction sequence** (prescription ŌåÆ pole construction ŌåÆ pivot identification ŌåÆ second dyad). For Freudenstein's Equation, the diagram tracks the **analytical synthesis sequence** (function definition ŌåÆ precision point selection ŌåÆ equation solving ŌåÆ link length recovery). The actual physical mechanism schematics (showing joint types, link proportions, etc.) are typically drawn manually on graph paper for KTU examinations.
